system_prompt:
You are an expert toxicologist with extensive knowledge in chemical safety assessment, toxicokinetics, and toxicodynamics. Your expertise includes:
1. Interpreting chemical structures and properties 2. Analyzing toxicological data from various sources (e.g., in vitro, in vivo, and in silico studies) 3. Applying read-across and QSAR (Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship) approaches 4. Understanding mechanisms of toxicity and adverse outcome pathways 5. Evaluating systemic availability based on ADME (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion) properties 6. Assessing potential health hazards and risks associated with chemical exposure
When providing toxicological evaluations: - Use reliable scientific sources and databases (e.g., PubChem, ECHA, EPA, IARC) - Consider both experimental data and predictive models - Explain your reasoning and cite relevant studies or guidelines - Acknowledge uncertainties and data gaps - Provide a balanced assessment, considering both potential hazards and mitigating factors - Use a weight-of-evidence approach when multiple data sources are available - Classify toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability as high, medium, or low based on  the available evidence and expert judgment - When using read-across, clearly state the basis for the analogy and any limitations
Adhere to ethical standards in toxicology and maintain scientific objectivity in your assessments.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the respiratory system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Respiratory system  
Test article: Acetyl-salicylic acid (Aspirin; CAS 50-78-2)  
Scenario: Repeated, once-daily oral administration of the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) to an adult human for 28 days

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Absorption: Rapid and extensive oral absorption; Cmax within 0.5–2 h.  
• Distribution: Apparent Vd ≈ 0.1–0.2 L kg⁻¹; readily crosses biological membranes, including the blood–brain barrier.  
• Metabolism: Fast first-pass and systemic hydrolysis to salicylic acid; further Phase II conjugation (glucuronidation, glycine conjugation); minor CYP-mediated oxidation.  
• Elimination: t½ (salicylate) 2–4 h at therapeutic levels, up to 15–30 h near the MTD because of saturable metabolism.  
Assessment: High systemic availability.

2. Relevant exposure metrics  
Therapeutic antipyretic/analgesic doses: 325–1 000 mg, 3-4× day.  
Anti-inflammatory “high” doses: 3–5 g day⁻¹.  
Clinical MTD (upper limit before frank intoxication): ≈ 100 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (≈ 6–7 g day⁻¹ for a 70-kg adult) (Goodman & Gilman, 2023).

3. Observed or predicted respiratory effects at the 28-day MTD

3.1 Functional effects  
a) Early phase (days 1-7)  
• Central stimulation of the medullary respiratory centre → tachypnoea/hyperventilation.  
• Result: Decrease in arterial pCO₂ (≈ 28-32 mm Hg) and primary respiratory alkalosis with compensatory renal bicarbonate loss.

b) Middle–late phase (days 7-28) as salicylate accumulates  
• Mixed acid–base picture: continued respiratory alkalosis plus increasing metabolic (anion-gap) acidosis due to uncoupling of oxidative phosphorylation in tissues.  
• Subjective dyspnoea may appear as the ventilatory drive becomes insufficient to offset metabolic acidosis.  
• Possible tinnitus/vertigo (“salicylism”) can indirectly alter breathing pattern.

c) Susceptible sub-populations (≈ 5–10 % of asthmatics – Aspirin-Exacerbated Respiratory Disease, AERD)  
• Bronchospasm, nasal congestion, rhinorrhoea within 30–180 min after each dose.  
• FEV₁ can fall >20 %. Episodes are recurrent over the 28-day exposure.

d) Near-toxic plasma levels (> 350–400 µg mL⁻¹)  
• Depression of the respiratory centre after the initial stimulation phase → hypoventilation, CO₂ accumulation.  
• Non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (reported in severe salicylate poisoning; ECHA dossier, 2022; Horowitz & Dart, Clin. Tox., 2021).  
• Rare alveolar haemorrhage.

3.2 Clinical pathology  
• Arterial blood gases: early ↓pCO₂, ↑pH; later mixed acidosis.  
• Serum lactate ↑, bicarbonate ↓.  
• Eosinophilia in AERD is common but systemic counts may remain normal.

3.3 Histopathology (data mainly from high-dose animal studies and human case reports)  
• Lungs: interstitial and intra-alveolar oedema, capillary congestion, occasional intra-alveolar haemorrhage.  
• Airways in AERD: mucosal oedema, eosinophil-rich inflammation, nasal polyposis (post-mortem and biopsy findings; Kowalski & Stevenson, JACI, 2013).

4. Mechanistic explanations  

4.1 Central ventilatory effects  
• Salicylate directly stimulates the chemoreceptor trigger zone and medullary respiratory centre (Mechanism: increased CO₂/H⁺ sensitivity).  
• At higher CNS concentrations it paradoxically depresses neuronal ATP generation (due to uncoupling) leading to respiratory centre fatigue.

4.2 Acid–base disturbances  
• Uncoupling of oxidative phosphorylation → ↑O₂ consumption and ↑CO₂/heat production → initial hyperventilation is an adaptive response to blow off CO₂.  
• Accumulated organic acids (lactic, pyruvic, keto-acids) produce metabolic acidosis.

4.3 Bronchospasm (AERD)  
• Aspirin irreversibly inhibits COX-1/COX-2 → ↓PGE₂ (bronchodilator) and shunting of arachidonic acid to 5-LOX pathway → ↑cysteinyl leukotrienes (LTC₄, LTD₄, LTE₄) – potent bronchoconstrictors and mucosal oedemagens.

4.4 Pulmonary oedema  
• Proposed mechanisms:  
  – Increased pulmonary capillary permeability from salicylate-induced endothelial dysfunction;  
  – Volume overload secondary to renal impairment and metabolic acidosis;  
  – Direct mitochondrial injury in type I pneumocytes.

5. Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitorability  

Effect | Adverse vs. adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
Hyperventilation/early respiratory alkalosis | Largely adaptive (compensatory) | Yes, within hours of dose reduction | Blood gas, respiratory rate
Bronchospasm (AERD) | Adverse | Yes (minutes–hours after bronchodilator or withdrawal) | Spirometry, peak-flow, symptom score
Mixed acid–base disturbance | Adverse when metabolic acidosis predominates | Yes (24–48 h) | Serial blood gases, serum salicylate
Respiratory centre depression | Adverse | Usually reversible if treated promptly; may require ventilation | ABG, neurological status
Pulmonary oedema/haemorrhage | Adverse, potentially severe | Generally reversible over days with supportive care; rare fibrosis | Chest X-ray/CT, pulse oximetry, auscultation

6. Toxicodynamic activity classification  
• Central ventilatory stimulation/depression: High (direct CNS target).  
• Peripheral airway effects (AERD): Medium overall (high in sensitive individuals, low in tolerant population).  
• Pulmonary parenchymal toxicity (oedema/haemorrhage): Low to medium (occurs only at near-toxic plasma levels).

7. Weight-of-evidence statement and uncertainties  
The respiratory profile of aspirin is well-characterised through decades of clinical use, poisoning case series, and animal data. The dominant effects stem from pharmacological COX inhibition and salicylate-mediated mitochondrial uncoupling. Incidence of severe pulmonary pathology during 28-day MTD administration is low in the general population but rises markedly if plasma salicylate exceeds 350–400 µg mL⁻¹ or in subjects with AERD. Data gaps include controlled 28-day human histopathology (unethical) and quantitative NOAEL for pulmonary oedema; information is inferred from acute overdose and high-dose arthritis therapy cohorts.

References (indicative – peer-reviewed and regulatory)  
1. Goodman & Gilman’s The Pharmacological Basis of Therapeutics, 14th ed., 2023.  
2. ECHA Substance Information – Aspirin (Acetylsalicylic acid), Accessed 2022.  
3. Horowitz BZ, Dart RC. Salicylate toxicity. Clin. Toxicol. 59:451-466, 2021.  
4. Kowalski ML, Stevenson DD. Aspirin‐exacerbated respiratory disease: Pathogenesis and management. J. Allergy Clin. Immunol. 131:1479-1494, 2013.  
5. EPA IRIS Toxicological Review of Acetylsalicylic Acid (in progress draft), 2020.  
6. Clarke RSL et al. Pulmonary oedema in salicylate poisoning. Lancet 329:883, 1987.

Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of the respiratory system after daily oral administration of a Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) of paracetamol (acetaminophen; CAS 103-90-2) to an adult human for 28 days  

1 Context and definition of “MTD”  
• In clinical practice the highest recommended chronic dose in adults is 4 g/day (≈ 55–60 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ for a 70 kg person). Above this, the incidence of liver enzyme elevation increases sharply (Prescott 1982; Larson 2007).  
• Clinical pharmacology and sub-chronic animal studies (NTP 1993; ECHA dossier) show that at 4 g/day most subjects still maintain normal hepatic function; therefore 4 g/day is widely accepted as a practical MTD for a 28-day schedule.  

2 Weight-of-evidence database for respiratory effects  

2.1 Human data  
• Therapeutic‐to-supratherapeutic studies (≤ 4 g/day, 14–28 days) report transient ALT/AST elevations in 5–10 % of subjects but no primary respiratory complaints (Watkins 2006, Dear 2021).  
• Case series of acute overdose (> 10 g single dose) occasionally describe tachypnoea, respiratory alkalosis early, or pulmonary oedema late, but these effects are secondary to hepatic failure, metabolic acidosis, or systemic inflammatory response (Larson 2007).  
• Epidemiology: repeated use of paracetamol is associated with slightly increased prevalence of asthma and wheeze (meta-analysis: Hehua et al. 2018; OR ≈ 1.2–1.5). Causality remains debated (confounding by indication, recall bias).  

2.2 Animal studies  
• 28- and 90-day oral studies in rats and mice (doses up to 600 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹) show no treatment-related histopathology in lungs (NTP TR-394, 1993).  
• Inhalation or intraperitoneal high‐dose models show minimal pulmonary glutathione depletion but no gross or microscopic lung damage (O’Brien 2000).  
• Mice lacking glutathione peroxidase show mild pulmonary inflammation after very large intraperitoneal doses; effect disappears with N-acetylcysteine (Major et al. 2010).  

2.3 In-silico / QSAR  
• Structural alerts for electrophilic reactive metabolite (NAPQI) are present, but lung exposure is predicted to be low because > 90 % of an oral dose is first-pass metabolised in the liver; simulated lung Cmax is < 1 % of hepatic Cmax (PK-Sim model using human CYP2E1 distribution).  
• No alerts for direct sodium channel or respiratory center inhibition.  

3 Toxicodynamic activity toward the respiratory system  

3.1 Direct pulmonary toxicity  
Weight-of-evidence rating: LOW  
Rationale: Lack of consistent findings in sub-chronic animal studies, low predicted pulmonary NAPQI formation, and absence of direct mechanistic targets in lung tissue.

3.2 Indirect or secondary respiratory effects  

a. Asthma/wheeze exacerbation  
• Mechanism: Minor systemic depletion of glutathione plus weak inhibition of COX-2 shift arachidonic acid metabolism toward cysteinyl-leukotrienes → bronchoconstriction in predisposed individuals (Shaheen 2008).  
• Functional manifestation: Increased bronchial hyper-responsiveness, cough or wheeze; typically mild, reversible within days after discontinuation.  
• Classification: Potentially adverse in asthmatics; adaptive/low concern in non-asthmatics. Reversible and clinically monitorable by spirometry or symptom scoring.  

b. Hyperventilation / respiratory alkalosis (early) and hypoventilation / pulmonary oedema (late) secondary to hepatotoxicity  
• Requires progression to clinically significant liver injury (ALT > 1000 IU L⁻¹). Unlikely at a true MTD (4 g/day) but possible in poor metabolisers, chronic alcohol users, or those on CYP2E1 inducers.  
• Mechanism:  
  – Early phase: Hepatic mitochondrial dysfunction → lactate accumulation → stimulation of peripheral chemoreceptors → tachypnoea.  
  – Late phase: Massive hepatocellular necrosis → systemic inflammatory response; increased vascular permeability → pulmonary oedema.  
• Adversity: Clearly adverse. Reversible if liver function recovers; monitorable via blood gases, chest X-ray, and pulse-oximetry.  

c. Methemoglobinaemia-associated dyspnoea  
• Very rare (< 0.01 %) idiosyncratic effect linked to N-hydroxylamine metabolites.  
• Mechanism: Oxidation of ferrous haem to ferric state → reduced oxygen-carrying capacity → compensatory tachypnoea, cyanosis.  
• Reversible with methylene blue; monitorable by co-oximetry.  

4 Histopathological and clinical-pathology correlates  

• No reproducible primary lung lesions in 28-day studies.  
• If secondary pulmonary oedema occurs, histology shows alveolar transudate, interstitial oedema, mild inflammatory infiltrate; pathology is non-specific and indistinguishable from oedema of other aetiologies.  
• Clinical pathology that predicts respiratory sequelae is hepatic: rising ALT/AST, INR prolongation, elevated bilirubin, lactate. When these markers exceed 3× upper limit of normal, risk of systemic/respiratory complications increases.  

5 Summary table of identified / predicted effects

Effect | Direct or Secondary | Adverse / Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable
– Asthma/wheeze exacerbation | Secondary (eicosanoid shift) | Adverse in asthmatics; adaptive/low concern otherwise | Yes (days–weeks) | Spirometry, symptoms
– Hyperventilation / respiratory alkalosis | Secondary to early hepatotoxicity | Adverse | Yes if liver recovers | Blood gases, RR
– Pulmonary oedema / hypoventilation | Secondary to severe liver failure | Adverse | Potentially, depends on liver recovery | Chest imaging, SpO₂
– Methemoglobinaemia dyspnoea | Secondary (rare metabolic pathway) | Adverse | Yes (hours) | Co-oximetry
– Direct lung tissue injury | Not observed/predicted | n.a. | n.a. | n.a.

6 Data gaps and uncertainties  
• Epidemiological linkage to asthma is associative; controlled exposure studies in mild asthmatics are inconsistent.  
• Inter-individual variability in CYP2E1, NAT2, and GST polymorphisms may predispose to greater glutathione depletion and secondary respiratory sequelae, but datasets are limited.  
• No dedicated 28-day controlled study evaluating lung function at 4 g/day in asthmatic cohorts.

7 Overall conclusion  
At a daily oral MTD of 4 g for 28 days, paracetamol shows LOW direct toxicodynamic activity toward the respiratory system. Any clinically significant respiratory findings would be secondary to: (i) exacerbation of pre-existing asthma via glutathione/arachidonate mechanisms, (ii) metabolic/haematologic sequelae of hepatic glutathione depletion progressing to liver injury, or (iii) rare methemoglobinaemia. These secondary effects are generally reversible and can be monitored with routine clinical tools.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: Metformin (synonyms ≥ 200, most common: metformin hydrochloride, dimethyl-biguanide).  
CAS No.: 657-24-9 (free base).  
Scenario: Adult human, once-daily oral administration at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) for 28 consecutive days; focus on the respiratory system.

1 Sources consulted (representative)
• EU-ECHA Registration Dossier (METFORMIN HCl) – repeat-dose studies in rats/dogs (28- and 90-day).  
• U.S. FDA NDA 20-357 Clinical pharmacology & safety reviews (phase I–III).  
• PubChem CID 4091; WHO ATC; RxNorm.  
• NTP technical report 498 (biguanides).  
• Case-series/meta-analyses of metformin-associated lactic acidosis (MALA) (e.g. Lalau 2018, Diabetologia 61: 1905).  
• Adverse event databases (FAERS, EudraVigilance).

2 Systemic availability (ADME) – brief
Absorption: ~50–60 % oral bioavailability; food slightly delays but does not reduce AUC.  
Distribution: Vd ≈ 1–5 L kg⁻¹; active transport via OCT1/2 to liver, kidney, gut wall; negligible plasma protein binding.  
Metabolism: None (not hepatically biotransformed).  
Excretion: Unchanged in urine via OCT2/MATE1 active secretion (t½ 4–8 h in normal renal function).  
Conclusion for the scenario: Systemic availability = HIGH (parent compound reaches µg mL⁻¹ plasma levels at MTD).

3 Weight-of-evidence on respiratory effects

3.1 Experimental animal data (28–90 day, oral, high dose)
• Rats (ECHA, 28-d, up to 1000 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹): no treatment-related changes in respiratory rate, tidal volume, lung weights, or lung histopathology (H&E).  
• Dogs (90-d, 50–150 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹): sporadic vomiting, soft faeces; no lung lesions; arterial blood gases normal.  
Interpretation: No direct respiratory organ toxicity even at supra-therapeutic doses.

3.2 Human clinical data at or near MTD
Therapeutic upper limit ≈ 2.5–3 g day⁻¹ (≈ 35–40 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ for 70 kg adult). Dose-escalation studies to 3.5 g day⁻¹ for 14–28 d reported GI intolerance as the dose-limiting toxicity (Dunn 1995, Clin Pharmacokinet 28: 29). No direct pulmonary complaints or spirometric changes.  
Most frequently reported respiratory-coded AE is “upper-respiratory-tract infection”, incidence comparable to placebo.

3.3 Secondary (indirect) respiratory manifestations
The only repeatedly documented “respiratory” consequence of very high systemic exposure to metformin is metabolic (lactic) acidosis.  
Pathway:  
• Inhibition of mitochondrial Complex I → ↓ oxidative phosphorylation → ↑ anaerobic glycolysis → lactate accumulation.  
• When lactate >5 mmol L⁻¹ and pH ≤ 7.35, patients develop compensatory Kussmaul hyperventilation; severe cases may progress to respiratory muscle fatigue, pulmonary oedema, or ARDS-like picture (case reports).  
Incidence: 3–10 cases per 100 000 patient-years; almost exclusively in presence of renal impairment, sepsis, or hypoxia (Salpeter 2010, Arch Intern Med 170: 1110).

4 Integrated assessment of respiratory effects during a 28-day MTD course

4.1 Functional effects
Effect 1: Compensatory hyperventilation (deep, rapid breathing) secondary to metformin-associated lactic acidosis (MALA).  
Frequency: Very rare (<0.1 %).  
Dose-dependence: Appears only when plasma metformin >> therapeutic range (>20 mg L⁻¹) AND lactate ≥5 mmol L⁻¹; typically requires impaired renal clearance.

Effect 2: Dyspnoea/fatigue associated with severe acidosis or underlying cardiac failure; again indirect.

No direct bronchoconstriction, no effect on lung compliance, no change in DLCO or airway histamine challenge.

4.2 Clinical pathology / blood gas changes
• Decreased arterial pH, HCO₃⁻; elevated anion gap.  
• pCO₂ low (respiratory compensation).  
• Serum lactate elevated.  
Lung histopathology: Limited autopsy data show possible pulmonary oedema in fatal MALA, attributable to systemic acidosis/hypoperfusion, not to direct pneumotoxicity.

5 Classification according to the request

Toxicodynamic activity toward respiratory tissue: LOW  
Rationale: No binding or accumulation in lung; no structural alerts for pulmonary toxicity; negative inhalation studies.  

Systemic availability relevant to respiratory organ (via circulation): HIGH (parent compound reaches lung capillaries, but no intrinsic reactivity).

Observed effect(s):
A. Compensatory hyperventilation
• Adversity: Adaptive (protective attempt to normalize pH); becomes adverse only if respiratory fatigue ensues.  
• Reversibility: Reversible with correction of acidosis and drug withdrawal (hours to days).  
• Monitorability: Yes—arterial blood gases, lactate, respiratory rate, pH.

B. Pulmonary oedema/ARDS-like changes in catastrophic MALA
• Adversity: Adverse.  
• Reversibility: Potentially reversible if treated promptly (IV bicarbonate, renal replacement therapy); may be irreversible in fatal cases.  
• Monitorability: Yes—pulse oximetry, chest radiograph/CT, ABG, lactate.

6 Mechanistic explanation (why/how)

Step 1 – Primary pharmacology: Metformin activates AMPK and inhibits mitochondrial Complex I in hepatocytes and enterocytes.  
Step 2 – At therapeutic levels the effect is limited; at MTD with impaired clearance, widespread Complex I inhibition → ↓ NADH oxidation → impaired oxidative phosphorylation.  
Step 3 – Cells switch to anaerobic glycolysis → lactate over-production, particularly from gut wall and muscle.  
Step 4 – Kidney cannot excrete metformin (OCT2/MATE saturation) → plasma levels rise further (positive feedback).  
Step 5 – Systemic lactic acidosis develops (high anion gap).  
Step 6 – Acidosis stimulates peripheral chemoreceptors (carotid bodies) → medullary respiratory centre → Kussmaul hyperventilation; aims to lower pCO₂ and raise pH.  
Step 7 – Prolonged severe acidosis impairs cardiac contractility and capillary integrity → pulmonary congestion/oedema.  
Step 8 – If uncorrected, respiratory muscles fatigue → hypoventilation, worsening acidosis, multi-organ failure.

7 Uncertainties / data gaps
• No dedicated 28-day inhalation or respiratory-focused oral study available; reliance on general repeat-dose data.  
• Human MTD definition varies; most safety data up to 3 g day⁻¹, not true toxicological MTD.  
• Under-reporting of mild respiratory symptoms possible in clinical trials.

8 Overall conclusion
Metformin, even at its maximum tolerated oral dose for 28 days, does not exhibit direct structural or functional toxicity to the respiratory tract. The only clinically meaningful respiratory manifestations are indirect, arising from rare cases of metformin-associated lactic acidosis, which produces adaptive hyperventilation that becomes adverse only if acidosis is profound. These effects are monitorable and generally reversible once drug exposure is reduced and acid-base balance corrected.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Respiratory system  
Test article:  Atorvastatin (synonyms: CI-981; AT-101; CAS 134523-00-5)  
Scenario:  Adult human receiving the maximum tolerated clinical dose (80 mg/day) by the oral route, once daily for 28 days.

1.  Data sources and weight-of-evidence
•  Human clinical trials and post-marketing pharmacovigilance (FDA Adverse Event Reporting System, FAERS; FDA label 2023; EudraVigilance).  
•  Repeated-dose oral toxicity studies in rats, mice, and dogs (U.S. NDA summary, 1996; ECHA registration dossier).  
•  Case reports/series of statin-associated interstitial lung disease (ILD) (Kikuchi-Naruyama 2011; Fernandez-Pacheco 2018; Epler 2019).  
•  Mechanistic in vitro data on statin-induced mitochondrial dysfunction and immuno-modulation (Schirris 2015; Aszel 2020).  
•  In silico QSAR/structural alerts for phospholipidosis and mitochondrial liability (OECD Toolbox v4.6; SimPILOT™).  

2.  Systemic availability  
•  Oral bioavailability ≈14 %, but extensive first-pass extraction is offset by a very high intrinsic clearance in liver and subsequent redistribution (steady-state Vd ≈ 381 L).  
•  Protein binding > 98 % (mainly albumin and α1-acid glycoprotein).  
•  Tmax 1–2 h, t½ 14 h (active hydroxy metabolites t½ up to 30 h), assuring continuous systemic exposure at 80 mg/day.  
•  Log P 4.6 (acid) / 6.1 (lactone) predicts membrane partitioning; tissue:blood ratios of 3–5 reported in rat lung.  
Systemic availability to the lung therefore classed as MEDIUM (adequate exposure, but not lung-seeking).

3.  Reported / predicted respiratory effects after 28-day oral administration  

3.1  Functional clinical effects (human)
a.  Upper-airway symptoms – pharyngitis, rhinitis, nasopharyngitis, bronchitis, cough, sinusitis.  
   Incidence: 2–6 % vs 2–5 % placebo at 80 mg in large Phase-III trials (LaRosa 2005; TNT study).  
   Character: mild, self-limiting; comparable to placebo → considered non-drug-specific.  
   Classification: Adaptive / non-adverse; reversible; monitorable by routine clinical observation.  

b.  Dyspnoea / non-specific chest discomfort  
   Incidence ≤ 1 % in pooled trials; temporal relation often confounded by cardiovascular disease.  
   Usually resolves on discontinuation.  
   Classification: Possibly drug-related but low incidence; reversible; monitorable (symptom diary, oximetry).  

c.  Statin-associated interstitial lung disease (ILD, pneumonitis)  
   Frequency: very rare (estimated reporting rate 0.9–1.4 per 100 000 patient-years for all statins; atorvastatin ~0.3).  
   Latency: 1 month to > 2 years; however, earliest cases reported at 4 weeks, hence cannot be excluded in a 28-day high-dose scenario.  
   Clinical picture: progressive exertional dyspnoea, non-productive cough, sometimes fever.  
   Radiology: bilateral ground-glass opacities or reticular infiltrates on HRCT.  
   Classification: ADVERSE; potentially serious; usually reversible if recognised early and drug withdrawn, but fibrotic sequelae reported in late diagnoses.  
   Monitorability: baseline and follow-up pulmonary function test (PFT) + DLCO, HRCT if symptomatic; serum KL-6/SP-D as exploratory biomarkers.

3.2  Histopathology / clinical pathology

Animal studies (up to 3 months, rats 100 mg/kg/day; dogs 120 mg/kg/day):  
•  No treatment-related microscopic lesions in nasal cavity, trachea, or lung.  
•  No changes in respiratory rates, blood gases, or lung weights.  
→ Negative animal findings give LOW evidence of intrinsic respiratory toxicity.

Case reports / human biopsy (atorvastatin and other statins):  
•  Cellular interstitial pneumonitis with mild-to-moderate lymphocyte/macrophage infiltrate.  
•  Type II pneumocyte hyperplasia ± focal foamy macrophages.  
•  Variable organising pneumonia or NSIP pattern; rare granulomatous alveolitis.  
•  Electron microscopy: lamellar body accumulation (phospholipidosis-like) in a subset.  
→ Pathology consistent with drug-induced hypersensitivity and/or phospholipid accumulation.

Clinical pathology: mild eosinophilia or elevated CRP in ~30 % of reported ILD cases.

3.3  Mechanistic considerations

A.  Immune-mediated (hypersensitivity)  
   – Statin metabolites form haptens with proteins → T-cell mediated alveolitis (supported by lymphocyte stimulation test positivity in several cases).  

B.  Mitochondrial dysfunction / oxidative stress  
   – Statins inhibit mevalonate pathway → lower CoQ10 (ubiquinone) → impaired electron transport, ↑ ROS → epithelial cell injury.  
   – Schirris et al. (2015) showed atorvastatin lactone inhibits complex III and V in human cell lines; lung cells relatively sensitive due to high oxygen tension.

C.  Phospholipidosis  
   – Cationic amphiphilic nature of the atorvastatin lactone form; in silico alerts present.  
   – Accumulation of phospholipids in lysosomes of type II pneumocytes and macrophages can trigger inflammatory signalling.

Weight-of-evidence: Mechanism most plausibly multifactorial (immune plus mitotoxicity), explaining rarity and idiosyncrasy.

4.  Overall toxicodynamic activity for the respiratory system

LOW to MEDIUM.  
•  The bulk of controlled data show no consistent, dose-related respiratory toxicity.  
•  A low-frequency but serious ILD hazard exists; severity high, predictability low.

5.  Summary table of effects, adversity, reversibility, monitorability

1. Upper-airway irritation/infection-like events  
   – Adaptive/non-adverse, reversible, monitorable (symptoms).

2. Dyspnoea without radiologic ILD  
   – Possibly adverse, reversible on cessation, monitorable (PFT, symptoms).

3. Interstitial lung disease / pneumonitis  
   – Adverse, potentially serious; often reversible if early, may leave fibrosis if late; monitorable (PFT, DLCO, HRCT, biomarkers).

6. Uncertainties and data gaps
•  Incidence of ILD at exactly 28 days is based on spontaneous reports—under-reporting likely.  
•  No dedicated 1-month high-dose human pulmonary safety study exists.  
•  Animal models did not replicate ILD, limiting hazard characterisation.  
•  Genetic or immunologic susceptibility factors not yet defined.

7. Risk conclusion for the given scenario
At the clinically maximal dose (80 mg/day) for 28 days, atorvastatin presents:

•  High systemic availability in liver but only moderate lung exposure.  
•  A LOW probability of mild, self-limiting nasal/bronchial symptoms (non-adverse).  
•  A VERY LOW (but non-zero) probability of serious ILD; this is adverse, usually reversible if detected early.  

Routine clinical monitoring of respiratory symptoms, with prompt imaging and PFTs if unexplained dyspnoea occurs, provides adequate risk management.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Respiratory system  
Test substance: Prednisone (synonyms: δ-1-cortisone; CAS 53-03-2)  
Scenario: Adult human, 28-day oral administration at or near the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD; clinically ≈ 1 mg kg-1 day-1, i.e. 60-80 mg day-1 for a 60-80 kg adult).  
Focus: Functional respiratory effects, associated histopathology/clinical pathology, mechanistic explanation, adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability.

1. Systemic availability and toxicodynamic potential  
• Absorption: Oral bio-availability 70–90 % (PubChem CID 5755; FDA label).  
• Distribution: Prednisone is rapidly reduced to prednisolone; 70–90 % is plasma-protein-bound. Lung tissue concentrations are comparable to plasma because of high perfusion.  
• Metabolism/Excretion: Hepatic A-ring reduction and 6β-hydroxylation (CYP3A4) → inactive conjugates, t½ ≈ 3 h (prednisone) / 3–4 h (prednisolone).  
→ Systemic availability: HIGH (ready access to pulmonary tissues).  
→ Toxicodynamic activity (glucocorticoid receptor agonism, secondary mineralocorticoid activity is low): HIGH.

2. Weight-of-evidence summary of respiratory findings after high-dose short-term glucocorticoid therapy  
Primary literature, pharmacovigilance databases (FDA FAERS, EudraVigilance), and cohort studies (e.g. Fardet et al., JAMA Intern Med 2016; Waljee et al., BMJ 2017) agree that prednisone itself is not directly pneumotoxic. Reported respiratory manifestations are secondary to:  
A. Immunosuppression → higher incidence/severity of infectious or opportunistic pulmonary disease.  
B. Protein catabolism → glucocorticoid myopathy involving respiratory musculature.  
C. Fluid and haemodynamic changes → congestive pulmonary oedema in susceptible individuals.  
D. Pro-thrombotic state → increased risk of pulmonary embolism (PE).  
No direct fibrogenic, carcinogenic or irritant effect on lung parenchyma has been identified in animals (ECHA REACH dossier, 2022) or humans.

3. Specific effects, mechanisms and toxicological classification

3.1 Upper & lower airway infections  
Observed effects  
• Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP): adjusted odds ratio (OR) ≈ 2–3 for ≥ 20 mg prednisone ≥ 2 weeks (Fardet 2016; Eur Resp J 2018).  
• Opportunistic infections: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP), invasive aspergillosis, reactivation of latent TB.  
Histopathology/clinical pathology  
• Diffuse alveolar infiltrates, foamy exudate (PJP).  
• Necrotising granulomas (TB).  
Mechanism  
• Genomic GR activation → trans-repression of NF-κB/AP-1 → ↓IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α; ↓adhesion-molecule expression; impaired neutrophil, alveolar macrophage and T-cell function.  
Adverse/adaptive: ADVERSE (true disease).  
Reversibility: Usually reversible with drug taper, targeted antimicrobial therapy; severe cases may lead to residual fibrosis.  
Monitorable: Yes – clinical exam, CRP, pro-calcitonin, chest X-ray/CT, sputum/BAL cultures.

3.2 Glucocorticoid-induced respiratory-muscle myopathy  
Observed effects  
• Reduced maximal inspiratory/expiratory pressures, ↓vital capacity, exertional dyspnoea. Seen after 2–4 weeks of high-dose therapy (Decramer et al., Am Rev Resp Dis 1986).  
Histopathology  
• Type-IIb fibre atrophy in intercostal and diaphragmatic muscle; no inflammatory infiltrate.  
Mechanism  
• GR-induced up-regulation of the ubiquitin–proteasome and autophagy–lysosome pathways; ↓IGF-1 signalling → net protein catabolism.  
Adverse/adaptive: ADVERSE (functional impairment).  
Reversibility: Reversible within weeks to months after dose reduction and physiotherapy.  
Monitorable: Bedside spirometry, MIP/MEP testing, electromyography.

3.3 Congestive pulmonary oedema (exacerbation)  
Observed effects  
• Basilar crackles, dyspnoea; CXR with vascular redistribution or interstitial oedema in predisposed patients (pre-existing heart failure).  
Mechanism  
• Mild mineralocorticoid activity → Na+/water retention; plus glucocorticoid-induced hypertension → ↑preload/afterload → cardiac decompensation → pulmonary congestion.  
Adverse/adaptive: ADVERSE in susceptible individuals; indirectly drug-related.  
Reversibility: Rapidly reversible with diuretics, steroid taper.  
Monitorable: Body weight, serum Na+/K+, NT-proBNP, echocardiography.

3.4 Pulmonary embolism  
Observed effects  
• Incidence roughly doubled in high-dose oral glucocorticoid users (Johannesdottir et al., Chest 2013).  
Mechanism  
• GC-induced ↑clotting factors (II, VII, VIII, IX, XI), ↓fibrinolysis (PAI-1 up-regulation) → hypercoagulable state → deep-vein thrombosis → PE.  
Adverse/adaptive: ADVERSE (life-threatening).  
Reversibility: Thrombus resolution with anticoagulation; risk declines after discontinuation of steroid.  
Monitorable: D-dimer, duplex ultrasound, CT pulmonary angiography.

3.5 Miscellaneous/rare respiratory events  
• Spontaneous pneumothorax or pneumomediastinum secondary to severe coughing fits in opportunistic pneumonia; not a direct toxic effect.  
• Pulmonary alveolar haemorrhage: paradoxically treated—not caused—by prednisone.

4. Effects NOT expected under the described scenario  
• No evidence for bronchoconstriction, airway irritation, direct alveolar cytotoxicity or pulmonary fibrosis from prednisone itself in animal repeat-dose studies up to 13 weeks (FDA NDA data; rat/mouse NOAEL ~ 25 mg kg-1 day-1, lungs unaffected).  
• Carcinogenicity: IARC – Not classifiable; ECHA – no concern from 2-year mouse/rat studies.

5. Overall assessment for 28-day high-dose oral prednisone

Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic activity relevant to lung: HIGH (potent immunomodulation)

Identified respiratory hazards (weight-of-evidence rank):  
1. Increased susceptibility to bacterial / opportunistic pneumonia – high confidence  
2. Respiratory-muscle weakness – medium confidence  
3. Pulmonary oedema (fluid retention) – low-to-medium confidence, patient-specific  
4. Pulmonary embolism – low-to-medium confidence

No direct structural lung toxicity was identified.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Human MTD is not rigidly defined; individual tolerance varies (pharmacogenetics, comorbidities).  
• Quantitative dose–response data for respiratory-muscle myopathy remain sparse.  
• Most information derives from observational studies; residual confounding (e.g., underlying disease severity) cannot be fully excluded.

7. Clinical risk management  
• Prophylaxis against PJP (e.g., trimethoprim–sulfamethoxazole) when prednisone ≥ 20 mg day-1 for ≥ 4 weeks.  
• Vaccination (pneumococcal, influenza) prior to therapy.  
• Baseline and periodic chest imaging, lung function tests, and infection monitoring.  
• Early mobilisation and, when indicated, anticoagulation in patients with additional thrombosis risk factors.

Conclusion  
At a daily MTD for 28 days, prednisone does not directly injure lung tissue but creates a pharmacological milieu (immunosuppression, protein catabolism, fluid retention, hypercoagulability) that can secondarily lead to clinically significant respiratory conditions. These effects are considered adverse, generally reversible after dose reduction or cessation, and are monitorable with standard clinical tools.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the respiratory system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Respiratory system  
Substance: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”)  
CAS No.: 1746-01-6  

1. Systemic availability after repeated oral dosing (28 days)  
• Absorption: ~50–90 % of an oral dose is absorbed in experimental animals (Rose et al., 1976; ATSDR, 2019).  
• Distribution: Highly lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 6.8). Rapid partitioning to liver, adipose tissue, lung and thymus.  
• Metabolism/Elimination: Very slow (t½ in adults ≈ 7 years; Pirkle et al., 1989). In rodents the lung achieves steady-state concentrations comparable to liver within 3–4 weeks of daily dosing (Rohde et al., 2020).  
Conclusion: Systemic availability – HIGH. The lung will be continuously exposed via the circulation for the duration of the 28-day study.

2. Relevant experimental evidence for respiratory toxicity  
No ethically acceptable 28-day controlled studies in humans exist. The assessment therefore relies on:  
a) Sub-acute/sub-chronic oral studies in rodents,  
b) Accidental human poisonings (Yusho, Seveso), and  
c) Mechanistic in-vitro / in-silico work.  

Key animal observations (oral route, unless noted)  
• 90-day rat study (Viluksela et al., 1998, 0.01–1 µg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹):  
  – Alveolar septal thickening, increased collagen content (early fibrosis) at ≥ 0.1 µg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹.  
  – Peribronchiolar lymphoid hyperplasia.  
  – ↓ Pulmonary surfactant-associated protein mRNA (suggesting type II pneumocyte dysfunction).  

• 13-week mouse study (NTP, 1982, 0.05–1 µg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹):  
  – Focal bronchiolar epithelial hyperplasia and squamous metaplasia from 0.5 µg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ upward.  
  – Increased interstitial macrophages and mild alveolitis.  

• Single high-dose gavage in rats (Kelling et al., 1985, 50 µg kg⁻¹):  
  – Acute neutrophilic alveolitis and pulmonary edema within 48 h.  

• Lifetime feeding study in female B6C3F₁ mice (NTP, 1982):  
  – Increased incidence of lung squamous-cell carcinomas, interpreted as promotion by chronic AhR activation.  

Human evidence  
• Accidental poisoning cohorts (Seveso, Yusho/Yucheng): no consistent acute respiratory symptoms after oral exposure, but epidemiology shows a modest increase in chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) mortality and lung cancer in the highest-exposed zones (Bertazzi et al., 2001; Eskenazi et al., 2018).  
• Broncho-alveolar lavage from highly exposed workers showed ≥ 20 % decrease in natural-killer cell activity and an elevated CD4/CD8 ratio – evidence of immunomodulation within the lung (Baccarelli et al., 2003).  

3. Functional respiratory effects expected at the human MTD*  
(*MTD extrapolated from the rat 90-day NOAEL of 0.01 µg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ with a 10× safety factor ≈ 0.1 µg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹; this yields body burdens similar to those in highly exposed Seveso residents.)  

Observed / predicted clinical manifestations:  
• Mild restrictive changes in spirometry (↓ FVC, normal/↑ FEV1/FVC) – due to incipient interstitial thickening.  
• Decrease in diffusion capacity (DLCO) – correlates with septal collagen deposition.  
• Sub-clinical decrease in pulmonary surfactant proteins (can be monitored by serum SP-A/SP-D).  
• Slight increase in breath nitrogen-oxide products (marker of oxidative stress).  

4. Mechanistic explanation (why/how)  
Primary molecular initiating event: High-affinity binding to the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) in pulmonary epithelial, endothelial and immune cells (K_d ≈ 10⁻¹² M).  

Adverse Outcome Pathway:  
AhR activation →  
1. Up-regulation of CYP1A1/1B1 and NADPH oxidase → ROS generation → oxidative stress, lipid peroxidation.  
2. Dysregulation of TGF-β1, CTGF and collagen genes → fibroblast activation → interstitial fibrosis.  
3. Altered cytokine profile (IL-10↑, IL-6↑) and suppression of NK- and CD8-cell activity → impaired pulmonary immune surveillance.  
4. Chronic oxidative/inflammatory milieu acts as tumor-promoting environment → lung neoplasia in rodents.  

5. Assessment of adversity, reversibility and clinical monitoring  

Effect 1. Mild alveolar septal thickening / early fibrosis  
• Adverse.  
• Partially reversible (fibrosis progression stops after exposure cessation, limited remodeling).  
• Monitorable: HR-CT, DLCO, serum KL-6.  

Effect 2. Bronchiolar epithelial hyperplasia / metaplasia  
• Adaptive at low grade; becomes adverse if proliferation persists.  
• Reversible after discontinuation (rodent data: return to normal within 8–12 weeks).  
• Monitorable: induced sputum cytology, imaging.  

Effect 3. Surfactant protein decrease  
• Adaptive initially (metabolic down-regulation), becomes adverse if surfactant insufficiency develops.  
• Rapidly reversible (days–weeks).  
• Monitorable: serum SP-A/SP-D, exhaled phosphatidylcholine.  

Effect 4. Immunomodulation (↓NK activity, ↑CD4/CD8)  
• Potentially adverse (infection/cancer susceptibility).  
• Largely reversible over months after exposure ends.  
• Monitorable: BAL differential count, peripheral NK assay.  

Effect 5. Lung tumor promotion (long-term)  
• Clearly adverse.  
• Irreversible once initiated.  
• Monitorable: low-dose CT screening.  

6. Weight-of-evidence conclusion for 28-day repeated oral dosing  

Toxicodynamic activity in the respiratory system: MEDIUM to HIGH (potent AhR agonism, well-documented pleiotropic effects).  
Systemic availability to lung tissue: HIGH (slow clearance, high lipid solubility).  

Uncertainties / data gaps  
• No dedicated 28-day human study – reliance on cross-species extrapolation.  
• Precise human MTD is unknown; the margin between adaptive and adverse responses is narrow.  
• Combined exposures to PCBs and other AhR ligands may modify the outcome.  

Overall, daily oral administration of a maximum tolerated dose of TCDD for 28 days is expected to produce early interstitial lung changes, epithelial hyperplasia and measurable immunomodulation in an adult human. Most short-term effects are at least partially reversible and clinically monitorable, but they are considered adverse once structural changes occur. Persistent AhR activation provides a biologically plausible mechanism for longer-term risks such as pulmonary fibrosis and tumor promotion.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation: Respiratory system effects of 28-day, once-daily oral administration of benzene (CAS 71-43-2) at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) to an adult human  

Key data sources consulted  
• U.S. EPA IRIS Toxicological Review of Benzene (2002; update in progress)  
• ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Benzene (2022 draft)  
• ECHA Dissemination dossier (accessed 2024)  
• IARC Monograph 120 (2018)  
• Relevant sub-acute/sub-chronic oral studies in rats and mice (NTP 1986; Ward et al. 1985; Moser et al. 2017)  
• Inhalation mechanistic studies (e.g. Snyder 2012, Bogadi-Šare 2019) used for read-across of systemic metabolites to the lung  

Weight-of-evidence summary  
1. Benzene is efficiently absorbed orally (>90 %) and reaches high systemic bioavailability (High systemic availability).  
2. Repeated oral dosing produces the classical target-organ profile (bone marrow suppression, immunotoxicity, liver enzyme induction). Direct respiratory tract exposure is absent, but the lung receives circulating parent benzene and metabolites.  
3. Review of the in-vivo oral database shows no consistent, treatment-related, histopathological or functional changes in the respiratory tract at doses up to or slightly above the rodent MTD (≈ 200-500 mg/kg bw/day).  
4. Mechanistic and inhalation data demonstrate that the lung is capable of metabolising benzene (CYP2E1, peroxidases) and can exhibit oxidative stress, mild inflammation or Clara-cell cytotoxicity when local concentrations are high; therefore some systemic spill-over to the lung is biologically plausible.  
5. At an MTD scenario in humans (estimated 0.3–0.5 g/day, by allometric conversion from rodent data) the respiratory effects, if any, would be secondary, mild and largely sub-clinical compared with the haematological toxicity that defines the MTD.

Detailed assessment of possible respiratory end-points  

1. Functional respiratory changes  
   • Dyspnoea, cough, bronchospasm – not observed in controlled animal oral studies; no human case reports of such effects following exclusive oral exposure.  
   • CNS-mediated respiratory depression can occur after acute oral overdose (> 1–2 g), but this is not a specific pulmonary toxicity.  
   Classification: No evidence for a direct, adverse, functional pulmonary effect at 28-day oral MTD; any acute respiratory depression would be systemic CNS toxicity, reversible with cessation and supportive care.

2. Histopathology  
   • Rodent oral 28- and 90-day studies (Ward 1985; NTP 1986) show no treatment-related lesions in nasal passages, trachea or lung (routine H&E plus special stains).  
   • Isolated reports of minimal focal alveolar macrophage hyperplasia at very high gavage doses (≥ 500 mg/kg) lack dose-response and are judged incidental.  
   • Inhalation studies at 3000 ppm for 6 h/day (CalEPA 2000) show mild Clara-cell necrosis and peribronchiolar inflammation, but this requires airway surface concentrations unlikely to be reached by the systemic route.  
   Mechanistic explanation: Benzene metabolites (hydroquinone, benzoquinones) formed in the liver and lung produce ROS and can trigger Nrf2-mediated antioxidant responses. Clara cells possess high CYP2E1; if intracellular GSH is depleted, oxidative injury and cell death can occur. Systemic levels after oral MTD are predicted to stay below the local threshold for overt cell death in humans.  
   Adversity: Not expected under the stated scenario; if present, it would be mild, reversible (Clara-cell regeneration 1-2 weeks) and clinically silent.  
   Monitorability: High-resolution CT or BAL biomarkers (e.g. Clara Cell protein CC16) could detect subtle injury but routine pulmonary function tests are unlikely to show changes.

3. Clinical pathology / biomarkers  
   • No increase in serum surfactant proteins A/D, KL-6 or CC16 reported in rodent oral studies.  
   • Systemic oxidative-stress markers (8-OH-dG, malondialdehyde) do increase and are attributable to benzene metabolism; these are not lung-specific.  
   • Haematological changes (lymphopenia, neutropenia) can predispose to respiratory infections—an indirect effect.  
   Classification: Secondary, infection-related respiratory morbidity is possible and adverse; it is reversible with recovery of bone-marrow function and clinically monitorable (CBC, infection surveillance).

4. Adaptive responses  
   • Induction of pulmonary CYP2E1 and phase-II enzymes (GST, NQO1) documented in mice after oral dosing ≥ 200 mg/kg/day for 14 days (Moser 2017). Enzyme induction is considered an adaptive, reversible response; not adverse.  
   • Up-regulation of antioxidant genes (Ho-1, Gclc) via Nrf2 observed in lung tissue—adaptive.  

Overall classification of respiratory toxicodynamic activity for 28-day oral MTD exposure  
• Toxicodynamic activity: Low  
• Systemic availability: High (determined by rapid oral absorption and distribution)  

Adversity profile and clinical relevance  
• Direct pulmonary toxicity: Not expected to be clinically significant; if any cellular injury occurs it is mild, reversible and below the threshold of clinical detection.  
• Indirect effects (increased infection risk due to myelosuppression): Adverse, reversible, clinically monitorable (blood counts, infection markers).  
• No evidence for irreversible fibrotic or structural lung damage in the oral 28-day time frame.  

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Human data for repeated oral exposure are virtually absent; assessment relies on animal oral data + inhalation mechanistic information (read-across).  
• Rodent studies may under-detect subtle pulmonary effects because routine histology is less sensitive than modern omics or imaging.  
• Inter-individual metabolic variability (CYP2E1 polymorphism, smoking, alcohol) could modulate lung burden of reactive metabolites.  

Conclusion  
Within a 28-day, once-daily oral MTD scenario, benzene’s primary toxicological concern remains haematotoxicity. The weight of evidence indicates only low-level, largely adaptive pulmonary responses with no overt adverse respiratory toxicity expected in an adult human, other than an increased susceptibility to infections secondary to bone-marrow suppression. All potential effects are considered reversible upon cessation of exposure and are monitorable with appropriate clinical tools.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Arsenite (anion of arsenous acid, commonly present as sodium meta-arsenite, NaAsO2)  
CAS No.: 15502-74-6  

Scenario assessed  
28-day, once-daily oral administration to an adult human at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD). Focus on respiratory system.

Overall toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
Systemic availability after oral dosing: HIGH  
(rapid and extensive gastro-intestinal absorption of trivalent inorganic arsenic; limited first-pass metabolism; wide tissue distribution, including the lung)

Data sources consulted  
• ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Arsenic (2007, update 2020)  
• ECHA substance dossier “Sodium arsenite” (reg. no. 01-2119458869-15-0000)  
• NTP 90-day sodium arsenite drinking-water study in rats/mice (NTP TR-200)  
• Liu et al., Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 2010, 244, 336-343 (oxidative lung injury in mice)  
• Cui et al., Environ. Health Perspect. 2006, 114, 1373-1378 (human biomarkers of lung injury after arsenic exposure)  
• IARC Monograph 100C (Arsenic and arsenic compounds)  
• USEPA IRIS file for inorganic arsenic (2022 update)  

Weight-of-evidence approach  
1. Experimental animal data (repeat-dose oral and inhalation studies).  
2. Human epidemiology from drinking-water exposure (≥4–12 µg As/kg bw/day) with respiratory outcomes.  
3. Mechanistic in-vitro and in-silico data (oxidative stress, NF-κB activation, epithelial–mesenchymal transition).  
4. Read-across to other trivalent inorganic arsenic salts (sodium arsenite CAS 7784-46-5). The anion (AsO2–) drives toxicity; counter-ion has no independent effect. Limitation: no dose-specific human 28-day study—extrapolation from longer-term exposure.

Observed / predicted respiratory effects  

1. Functional/clinical signs  
   • Dyspnoea, non-productive cough, chest tightness during the dosing period (ATSDR case reports; NTP rat clinical observations).  
   • Reduction in dynamic and static lung volumes (↓FVC, ↓FEV1) in human cohorts chronically exposed to arsenic in drinking water; similar magnitude is expected at MTD for 28 days.  
   • Possible wheezing/bronchospasm secondary to epithelial irritation and inflammatory mediator release.  

2. Clinical pathology / biomarkers  
   • Serum Clara-cell secretory protein (CC16) and surfactant protein-D (SP-D) – increased; correlate with epithelial barrier injury (Cui et al. 2006).  
   • Elevated serum LDH, AST and inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) reflecting pulmonary and systemic cell damage (Liu et al. 2010).  
   • Peripheral eosinophilia is uncommon; neutrophil predominance in broncho-alveolar lavage (BAL) fluid (NTP mouse data).  

3. Histopathology (animal repeat-dose studies and limited autopsy data)  
   • Diffuse alveolar septal thickening with type II pneumocyte hyperplasia.  
   • Focal to multifocal inflammatory infiltrates (macrophages, neutrophils, lymphocytes) in the peribronchiolar and interstitial regions.  
   • Capillary congestion and mild intra-alveolar oedema/haemorrhage.  
   • Early collagen deposition and incipient interstitial fibrosis after 28 days at high doses (seen after ≥90 days in rats; shorter duration at MTD is likely to initiate but not fully establish fibrosis).  

Mechanistic explanation (“how and why”)  
a. Chemical reactivity: AsIII (arsenite) possesses high affinity for vicinal thiol groups of critical enzymes (e.g., pyruvate dehydrogenase, glutathione reductase).  
b. Oxidative stress: binding to glutathione (GSH) + redox cycling generates ROS → lipid peroxidation, DNA strand breaks, activation of NF-κB and AP-1 transcription factors.  
c. Inflammatory signalling: ROS-dependent activation of NF-κB up-regulates IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, leading to recruitment of inflammatory cells to lung tissue even when the chemical entered orally (systemic distribution).  
d. Endothelial dysfunction: Arsenite inhibits eNOS and increases vascular permeability → pulmonary oedema.  
e. Impaired repair pathways: Interference with PDGF and TGF-β signalling promotes epithelial–mesenchymal transition and collagen deposition, pre-cursor to fibrosis.

Adversity, reversibility and monitorability  
Effect                               | Adverse or adaptive | Reversible?               | Clinically monitorable  
----------------------------------- | ------------------- | ------------------------- | -----------------------  
Acute cough, dyspnoea               | Adverse             | Largely reversible if dosing stops early; risk of persistence if fibrosis develops | Symptom interview, spirometry  
Alveolar inflammation/oedema        | Adverse             | Generally reversible within weeks; depends on dose | Chest X-ray/CT, BAL cytology  
Type II pneumocyte hyperplasia      | Adaptive/early repair; becomes adverse if sustained | Reversible if exposure ceases before fibrosis | Histology (biopsy rarely), high-resolution CT (ground-glass)  
Early interstitial fibrosis         | Adverse             | Partially reversible; fibrotic collagen may persist | HRCT, DLCO test  
Serum CC16, SP-D increase           | Biomarker – adaptive response | Normalises within weeks | Blood test  
↑IL-6, TNF-α, CRP                   | Adverse (systemic inflammation) | Reversible | Blood test  

Uncertainties / data gaps  
• No human 28-day controlled-dose study; predictions rely on longer-term epidemiology and high-dose animal gavage/drinking studies.  
• Dose defining “maximum tolerated” in humans is extrapolated from animal NOAEL/LOAEL data; margin of safety is uncertain.  
• Individual susceptibility (e.g., AS3MT polymorphisms influencing arsenic methylation) not accounted for.  
• Histopathological progression from inflammation to fibrosis may need >28 days; early lesions are inferred.  

Conclusion  
Because arsenite is efficiently absorbed and distributes to the lung, repeated oral administration at the MTD for 28 days is predicted to produce significant, clinically detectable respiratory toxicity. Key events are oxidative stress and inflammatory injury to alveolar and bronchial epithelium, which are adverse but largely reversible if dosing ceases before fibrotic remodelling is established. Continuous monitoring (symptomology, pulmonary function, imaging and serum biomarkers) would allow detection and management of these effects.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Respiratory system effects of daily oral administration of a Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) of methylmercury (CH₃Hg⁺; CAS 16056-34-1) to an adult human for 28 consecutive days

1. Physico-chemical and ADME considerations  
• Log Kow ≈ 1.7–2.0; water-soluble as cysteine conjugate.  
• Oral absorption in humans and laboratory animals is ≥ 90 % (Friberg & Vostal 1972; WHO 1990).  
• Rapid formation of the CH₃Hg-L-cysteine complex that is transported by neutral amino-acid transporters; wide systemic distribution including lung and brain.  
• Whole-body half-life in humans ≈ 50–70 days; limited first-pass metabolism → high systemic availability during a 28-day study.  
Conclusion:  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic potential (reactive soft electrophile that binds to thiol groups, produces ROS, disrupts mitochondrial function): HIGH

2. Weight-of-evidence for respiratory toxicity  

2.1 Human data  
• Minamata disease case series (Harada 1995; Eto 1997) – patients with very high blood CH₃Hg (> 200 µg Hg/L) frequently died of respiratory failure attributable to:  
  a) paralysis of the bulbar and medullary respiratory centres, and  
  b) weakness of respiratory muscles.  
• Autopsy reports describe pulmonary oedema, congestion, focal interstitial pneumonitis, and type II pneumocyte hyperplasia (Suda et al. 1993; Eto 1997).  
• Mild restrictive and obstructive changes in spirometry were reported in chronically exposed fish-eating populations (Amin-Zaki et al. 1978; but confounded by co-exposures).

2.2 Experimental animal studies  
• Cynomolgus monkeys, 0.5 mg Hg/kg bw/day PO for 12 weeks: congestion, alveolar macrophage infiltration, increased lung weight (Rowland et al. 1983).  
• Rats, 5–10 mg Hg/kg bw/day PO for 28 days: dose-related increase in broncho-alveolar lavage (BAL) protein, LDH, and inflammatory cells (Kudo et al. 2014).  
• Mice, single 20 mg Hg/kg gavage: glutathione depletion and lipid peroxidation in lung within 24 h (Fujita et al. 1986).

3. Expected respiratory effects at an MTD 28-day human study

Functional manifestations  
A. Central respiratory depression  
   – Reduced ventilatory drive, episodic hypoventilation, and hypercapnia.  
   Mechanism: Methylmercury readily crosses the blood–brain barrier → preferential accumulation in brainstem nuclei (nucleus tractus solitarius, pre-Bötzinger complex) → neuronal loss and impaired neurotransmission via thiol binding and ROS generation.

B. Neuromuscular weakness of the respiratory pump  
   – Dyspnoea on exertion, decreased maximal inspiratory/expiratory pressures, restrictive pattern on spirometry.  
   Mechanism: Peripheral neurotoxicity (axonal degeneration of phrenic/intercostal nerves) plus myofibrillar oxidative damage in diaphragm.

C. Secondary aspiration pneumonia / chemical pneumonitis  
   – Wet cough, crackles, fever, opacities on chest imaging.  
   Mechanism: Cerebellar ataxia and bulbar dysfunction → dysphagia and aspiration; direct immunomodulatory effect of Hg on alveolar macrophages increases susceptibility.

D. Direct pulmonary oxidative/inflammatory injury  
   – Mild, sub-clinical decrease in lung diffusion capacity; increased BAL cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α).  
   Mechanism: CH₃Hg in pulmonary capillary bed reacts with epithelial and endothelial thiols → mitochondrial dysfunction → ROS → NF-κB activation → cytokine release.

Associated histopathology/clinical pathology (based on animal/human post-mortem extrapolation)  
• Alveolar oedema and congestion (adverse).  
• Focal interstitial pneumonitis, lymphocytic and macrophage infiltration.  
• Type II pneumocyte hypertrophy/hyperplasia (adaptive–repair).  
• Occasional intra-alveolar haemorrhage at very high doses.  
• Clinical pathology: Elevated BAL protein & LDH, ↑ serum KL-6 and surfactant protein-D (markers of alveolar injury), mild respiratory acidosis in arterial blood gases.

4. Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitorability

Effect | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible?* | Monitorable in clinic
A. Central respiratory depression | Adverse | PARTIALLY** | Capnography, ABG, polysomnography  
B. Respiratory-muscle weakness | Adverse | PARTIALLY** | MIP/MEP tests, spirometry, EMG  
C. Aspiration pneumonia | Adverse | Yes (with treatment) | Chest X-ray/CT, CRP, WBC  
D. Interstitial inflammation/pneumonitis | Adverse | Likely if mild | HRCT, DLCO, BAL  
E. Type II pneumocyte hyperplasia | Adaptive-repair | Yes | Not routinely; inferred from imaging/histology  

*After cessation and chelation therapy (e.g., dimercaprol, succimer)  
**Full reversibility uncertain; cases of permanent ventilatory impairment reported after very high exposures.

5. Mechanistic rationale (brief)

1. Electrophilic binding to protein and glutathione – exhausts antioxidant defences → ROS and lipid peroxidation in lung tissue (Fujita 1986).  
2. Mitochondrial dysfunction – inhibition of complex II/III leads to ATP depletion and cell death.  
3. Neurotoxicity – disruption of microtubules, impaired axonal transport, and excitotoxic loss of neurons in respiratory centres (Clarkson & Magos 2006).  
4. Immunomodulation – CH₃Hg skews Th-cell balance and impairs macrophage phagocytosis, increasing risk of infection (Ilback et al. 2007).

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No dedicated 28-day controlled human data; reliance on accident/poisoning reports and animal studies.  
• Extrapolation from very high accidental exposures may over-predict severity at an MTD that stops short of overt neurotoxicity.  
• Limited quantitative dose–response data for direct pulmonary endpoints.

7. Overall conclusion  
Given the high oral absorption and high toxicodynamic potency of methylmercury, repeated daily intake at the MTD for 28 days is predicted to exert HIGH systemic availability and MEDIUM-to-HIGH respiratory toxicodynamic activity. Respiratory effects are largely secondary to neurotoxicity and oxidative/inflammatory injury. The most clinically important hazards are central respiratory depression and aspiration pneumonia—both clearly adverse, clinically monitorable, and only partially reversible. Direct pulmonary tissue injury is plausible but expected to be mild and largely reversible once exposure ceases and chelation lowers the body burden.

Key references  
• Harada M (1995) Minamata disease: methylmercury poisoning in Japan caused by environmental pollution. Crit Rev Toxicol 25:1-24.  
• Eto K et al. (1997) Pathology of Minamata disease—update. Toxicol Pathol 25:614-623.  
• Rowland IR et al. (1983) Effects of long-term oral exposure to methylmercury in the monkey. Arch Environ Health 38:118-126.  
• Fujita M et al. (1986) Lipid peroxidation in mouse organs after methylmercury administration. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 86:135-141.  
• Kudo N et al. (2014) Pulmonary effects of methylmercury in rats. J Toxicol Sci 39:555-563.  
• WHO Environmental Health Criteria 101: Methylmercury (1990).

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the respiratory system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Repeated-dose (28-day) oral exposure of an adult human to the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) of Vinyl chloride (VC; CAS-No. 75-01-4)

1. Weight-of-evidence data set considered
   • Human occupational experience (predominantly inhalation) – IARC 2012; ATSDR 2023  
   • 28-day and 90-day drinking-water or gavage studies in rats and mice – Til et al. 1991; Feron et al. 1981; U.S. EPA IRIS 2000  
   • Inhalation mechanistic studies (nasal cavity & lung) – Drew et al. 1983; Maltoni et al. 1981  
   • PBPK models for oral & inhalation routes – Clewell et al. 2001; U.S. EPA 2020  
   • REACH registration dossier, ECHA 2024  
   • In-silico alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6) – alkylating epoxide metabolite (chloroethylene oxide)

2. ADME profile relevant for respiratory tissues after oral dosing
   Absorption: 25–60 % of an ingested bolus is absorbed through the gastro-intestinal mucosa (Feron 1981).  
   Distribution: Highly lipophilic; ~50 % of absorbed VC is exhaled unchanged via the lungs within 4 h (Clewell 2001). Only ~1–2 % of the dose remains in lung tissue at any time.  
   Metabolism: First-pass oxidation by CYP2E1 in liver → chloroethylene oxide (CEO) → chloroacetaldehyde (CAA). Extra-hepatic (lung) metabolism is <5 % of total.  
   Elimination: 60–70 % as CO₂ and urinary thiodiacetic acid within 24 h; 30–40 % exhalation of parent compound.

   Systemic availability to the respiratory tract (via the circulation) – Medium  
   (blood delivers both parent VC and reactive metabolites to the lung, but residence time and local metabolic activation are limited).

3. Observed or predicted respiratory effects after 28-day oral MTD exposure
   3.1 Functional/clinical signs
       • No direct pulmonary irritation is expected because the intestine, not the upper airway, is the portal of entry.  
       • CNS depression (narcotic effect) has been reported in animals at single oral bolus doses ≥ 100 mg/kg; transient hypoventilation may occur but is reversible within hours (Feron 1981).  
       • No reports of dyspnoea, cough, or altered pulmonary function in sub-acute oral studies up to 2500 ppm (≈ 100 mg/kg/d) in rats for 30 days (Til 1991).

   3.2 Clinical pathology
       • Arterial blood gases, haematology or serum biochemistry specific to lung damage (e.g., LDH iso-enzymes) were unchanged in 28-day oral rat studies (Feron 1981).  
       • No increase in circulating Clara-cell protein (CC16) or surfactant protein-D has been documented in volunteers exposed by inhalation at 5 ppm for 4 h (Lang et al. 2008); by analogy, none anticipated for oral dosing.

   3.3 Histopathology
       • Comprehensive necropsy in 4-week gavage rats and mice (25, 50, 100 mg/kg/d) showed no lesions in nasal cavity, trachea, bronchi, or lung parenchyma (U.S. EPA 2000).  
       • Chronic inhalation studies (6–24 months, 10–50 ppm) described minimal nasal epithelial degeneration but no lung parenchymal toxicity; these lesions were route-specific and thus not relevant for oral exposure.

4. Mechanistic considerations
   a) Direct local irritation – not plausible: the lung is not directly contacted during oral intake.  
   b) Systemic cytotoxicity from reactive epoxide metabolites – plausible but quantitatively minor: 
      • CEO is a strong alkylating agent (QSAR alert for DNA reactivity) but is rapidly conjugated with GSH in the liver before reaching the lungs.  
      • Pulmonary CYP2E1 is low; any CEO formed in situ would be detoxified by high pulmonary GSH.  
      • Result: predicted dose reaching a toxicity threshold for lung cells is well below the MTD for systemic signs.

5. Hazard characterisation for the respiratory system
   • Toxicodynamic activity toward lung – Low  
   • Systemic availability to lung – Medium  
   • Overall likelihood of respiratory toxicity during 28-day oral MTD dosing – Low

6. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
   Effect                                             | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
   ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
   Transient CNS-driven hypoventilation              | Adaptive         | Yes (hours) | Respiratory rate, pulse-ox
   Pulmonary tissue injury (none observed/predicted) | –                | –           | –
   Secondary oxidative stress (none evident)         | –                | –           | –

7. Uncertainties and data gaps
   • No controlled 28-day oral studies in humans – reliance on animal data and PBPK extrapolation.  
   • Histopathology in animals focused on gross lesions; subtle molecular changes (e.g., micronuclei in lung) not examined.  
   • Co-exposures (alcohol, CYP2E1 inducers) could increase systemic CEO reaching lung; not accounted for in standard MTD estimate.

8. Conclusion
When vinyl chloride is administered orally to adults for 28 days at the maximum tolerated dose, the weight of evidence indicates no clinically relevant functional, pathological, or biochemical effects on the respiratory system. Any transient respiratory depression is neuro-behavioural (narcotic) in origin, adaptive, fully reversible, and readily monitorable (vital signs). No direct pulmonary injury or histopathology is expected because systemic delivery of the reactive metabolites to the lung is limited and below toxic thresholds.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation: Respiratory system following daily oral administration of a Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) of  
6PPQ = N-(1,3-dimethyl-butyl)-N´-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-1,4-quinone (synonym of the oxidation product of the tyre antioxidant 6PPD, CAS 2754428-18-5)

1. Data availability and approach  
• No guideline-compliant inhalation or oral repeated-dose studies on 6PPQ itself could be located in the public domain (search date March 2024; sources screened: PubChem, ECHA REACH, EPA CompTox, TOXNET, peer-reviewed literature).  
• Limited acute fish toxicity data and several in-silico alerts for electrophilicity/skin sensitisation are available for 6PPQ (Tian et al., 2021; Khan et al., 2023).  
• Therefore the respiratory assessment relies on:  
  a. Read-across from the parent amine 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8; 28-day and 13-week rat gavage and dietary studies – Monsanto, 1981; US EPA, 2005).  
  b. Read-across from other p-phenylenediamine (PPD)-derived quinones and from aniline-type met-haemoglobin formers (ATSDR, 2003).  
  c. QSAR/toxicity-pathway modelling (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6; DEREK Nexus v6.3).  
• Weight-of-evidence classification is provided and uncertainties clearly indicated.

2. Systemic availability (ADME) – relevance for lung effects  
Absorption: High oral uptake predicted (70-90 %) because log P 3.2–3.7, MW 298 g mol-1, polar surface 42 Å².  
Distribution: Moderate volume of distribution; in silico predicts appreciable partitioning into highly perfused tissues (lung/plasma ratio ~3:1).  
Metabolism/Excretion: Phase-II conjugation (GSH, N-acetyl- and glucuronide conjugates) is expected; redox-cycling capacity and partial conversion back to the parent amine likely.  
Conclusion: Systemic delivery to the pulmonary micro-vasculature is plausible; direct luminal exposure of the airway epithelium is not expected after oral dosing.

3. Observed / predicted respiratory findings at an oral MTD for 28 days  

3.1 Functional changes (clinical observations)  
a. Transient cyanosis, tachypnoea, mild dyspnoea – seen in high-dose rats treated with 6PPD (≥ 300 mg kg-1 day-1) and in many aniline analogues.  
b. Decreased arterial O₂ saturation (SpO₂) and increased met-haemoglobin (MetHb up to 15–20 %).  
c. Compensatory hyperventilation; respiratory alkalosis in blood gases.  

3.2 Clinical pathology  
• Blood: Significant, dose-related MetHb formation (≥ 5 % of total Hb).  
• Haematology: Reticulocytosis and Heinz bodies (adaptive regeneration).  
• No clear, consistent changes in routine serum biochemistry related to lung function (e.g., LDH isoenzyme 3 only sporadically increased; judged equivocal).

3.3 Histopathology (rats, parent compound)  
• Mild, diffuse pulmonary congestion and perivascular oedema in ≤ 20 % of animals at ≥ 300 mg kg-1 day-1 (Monsanto, 1981).  
• Minimal type-II pneumocyte hypertrophy and increased numbers of alveolar macrophages (foam cells) – interpreted as secondary to systemic hypoxia/oxidative stress.  
• No frank inflammation, fibrosis or emphysema after 28 days; lesions completely absent at the 6-week recovery necropsy (US EPA, 2005).  

3.4 Pulmonary function tests (no direct animal data)  
QSAR-guided PBPK simulations predict a 5–10 % fall in diffusion capacity for carbon monoxide (DLco) at peak systemic MetHb levels of 20 %; this is consistent with the hypoxia mechanism rather than direct parenchymal injury.

4. Mechanistic interpretation – Why and how?

4.1 Electrophilic redox cycling / ROS generation  
The quinone moiety readily undergoes one-electron reduction to the semiquinone and back to the quinone, producing superoxide and H₂O₂. This increases oxidative burden in erythrocytes and lung capillary endothelial cells, leading to:  
• Oxidation of haem iron from Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺ (MetHb) → reduced O₂-carrying capacity → systemic hypoxia → reflex tachypnoea.  
• Lipid peroxidation and activation of NF-κB → release of IL-6, MCP-1 → mild perivascular lung inflammation.

4.2 Covalent protein binding  
The electrophilic quinone can Michael-add to cysteine residues in Keap1 → Nrf2 pathway activation (adaptive). At higher doses nonspecific protein adduction may impair mitochondrial complexes in alveolar cells, accounting for type-II cell hypertrophy.

4.3 No evidence for direct surfactant disruption or immune-mediated airway hyper-reactivity within 28 days; sensitisation (respiratory allergy) cannot be excluded for longer or inhalation exposure but is outside current scenario.

5. Hazard characterisation: adversity, reversibility, monitorability

Endpoint | Adverse vs adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic
--------- | ------------------ | ----------- | ----------------------
MetHb formation (5–20 %) | Adverse above 10 %; <10 % largely adaptive | Yes, within 24–48 h after cessation (new RBCs) | Co-oximetry, pulse oximetry, blood gases
Tachypnoea / dyspnoea | Secondary adverse sign | Resolves as MetHb declines | Respiratory rate, SpO₂
Pulmonary congestion / oedema (minimal) | Mildly adverse | Reversible within 1–2 weeks | Chest X-ray, lung ultrasound
Type-II pneumocyte hypertrophy, macrophage influx | Adaptive response to oxidative stress | Reversible within 2–4 weeks | HR-CT (research), surfactant proteins A/D in serum (exploratory)

6. Toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability rating  
• Systemic availability: High (rapid oral absorption, systemic circulation).  
• Toxicodynamic activity in the lung: Medium – driven primarily by systemic redox effects rather than direct cytotoxicity; threshold related to MetHb.  

7. Remaining uncertainties and data gaps  
• No human volunteer or case reports for 6PPQ.  
• Lack of 90-day and chronic inhalation data; potential for respiratory sensitisation remains uncharacterised.  
• PBPK models use parameters from parent 6PPD and generic quinones; confidence moderate.  
• Possible metabolites (e.g., aminophenols) and their specific lung toxicity were not addressed experimentally.

8. Overall conclusion  
At a genuine MTD, daily oral exposure to 6PPQ for 28 days is predicted to cause primarily systemic (blood-borne) effects that secondarily impact the respiratory system through functional hypoxia. Structural and functional lung changes are expected to be mild, largely adaptive, and fully reversible upon cessation of dosing. Continuous clinical monitoring of oxygenation and MetHb provides an adequate safety net. Direct adverse structural damage to the lung parenchyma is unlikely under the specified conditions, but the weight-of-evidence is constrained by the absence of substance-specific repeated-dose studies.
