vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8)  
Scenario: Daily oral administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) for 28 days to a healthy adult human; focus on the respiratory system.

1. Data set and approach  
   • Primary experimental sources: ECHA Registration Dossier (2023 update), US EPA/IRIS files, ATSDR PAH profile (2020), repeat-dose rodent studies (Schuetz et al. 1996; Blevins et al. 2013), in vitro AhR/CYP1A1 induction data, and QSAR/read-across from structurally related three-ring PAHs (anthracene, fluoranthene).  
   • No controlled 28-day human studies exist; therefore the assessment combines:  
     – Rodent oral repeat-dose data (14- to 90-day studies; highest non-lethal doses 200–600 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹)  
     – In silico PBPK models for systemic distribution (Ramesh & Inyang 2015)  
     – Mechanistic knowledge on PAH toxicodynamics (AhR activation, CYP-mediated bioactivation, oxidative stress).  
   • Weight-of-evidence, including uncertainties, is provided for each endpoint.

2. Systemic availability (ADME) – relevance for lung  
   • Absorption: Phenanthrene is lipophilic (log Kow 4.57); oral absorption in rodents ~70–85 %.  
   • Distribution: Rapid dissemination via blood; highest tissue levels in fat, liver, lung. Lung:blood ratio ≈ 3:1 in rat PBPK modelling at steady state.  
   • Metabolism: Phase I CYP1A1/1B1 → phenanthrene-1,2-oxide/3,4-oxide → dihydrodiols → diolepoxides; Phase II glucuronidation and sulfation.  
   • Elimination: Biliary/faecal > urinary; terminal t½ in rodents 8–15 h.  
   Systemic availability rating: HIGH (sufficient parent compound and metabolites reach lung tissue).

3. Observed / predicted respiratory effects at an MTD after 28 days  

   3.1 Functional (clinical) effects  
   • Mild, sub-clinical impairment of pulmonary diffusion capacity (≈ 5–10 % ↓ DLCO predicted from rat data extrapolation; adaptive and reversible).  
   • Possible transient cough or throat irritation has been reported after high oral bolus dosing in volunteers with crude PAH mixtures, but not with purified phenanthrene; considered unlikely at pure phenanthrene.  
   Overall functional impact: minimal and often not perceptible in clinic.

   3.2 Clinical pathology / biomarker changes  
   • Bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) in rodents:  
     – ↑ total protein (+25 %), ↑ LDH (~2-fold), mild ↑ neutrophils and macrophages (King et al. 2001).  
   • Expected human correlate: slight ↑ serum Clara-cell protein (CC16) and surfactant protein-D – both monitorable non-invasively.  
   • All BAL changes returned to baseline within 14 days post-exposure in animals → reversible.

   3.3 Histopathology (rodent evidence extrapolated)  
   • Centriacinar/alveolar macrophage pigmentation (brown-black, PAH-laden) – grades minimal to slight (0–1/4).  
   • Mild type II pneumocyte hypertrophy and rare peribronchiolar inflammatory cell cuffs.  
   • No fibrosis, no frank necrosis at MTD levels.  
   • Lesions fully resolved or markedly regressed after 4-week recovery period (Blevins 2013).  

   3.4 Mode-of-action / mechanistic interpretation  
   a) AhR-mediated enzyme induction  
      – Phenanthrene is a low-affinity ligand (EC₅₀ ~1–10 µM).  
      – Up-regulation of CYP1A1/1B1 in bronchial and alveolar cells → ↑ ROS generation.  
      – Consequence: oxidative stress → mild epithelial irritation/inflammation.  
   b) Generation of reactive metabolites (1,2-diol-3,4-epoxide)  
      – Forms DNA adducts in lung cells (adduct frequency in mouse lung 6–10 adducts/10⁸ nucleotides; Rowlands et al. 2009).  
      – At MTD and short (28-day) duration the burden is below thresholds associated with mutagenicity-driven cytotoxicity; nonetheless provides mechanistic plausibility for minimal inflammatory signalling.  
   c) Macrophage/particle accrual  
      – Highly lipophilic parent accumulates in lung surfactant → phagocytosed by macrophages → pigment deposition (adaptive).

4. Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability  
   • Enzyme induction (CYP1A1/1B1)  
     – Adaptive; reversible within 1–2 weeks post-dose; monitorable via urinary 1-hydroxypyrene equivalents or nasal scrape CYP1A1 mRNA.  
   • Oxidative-stress-driven mild inflammation  
     – Borderline adverse (minimal, no impact on lung function); reversible; monitorable (exhaled breath condensate 8-isoprostane, serum CRP).  
   • Alveolar macrophage pigmentation / type II cell hypertrophy  
     – Generally adaptive; reversible; detected only by imaging (HR-CT) or biopsy, not routinely monitored in clinic.  
   • DNA adduct formation  
     – Potentially adverse (genotoxic). Although quantitatively low, genotoxicity is, by definition, adverse. Not directly monitorable clinically but can be assessed with PBMC 32P-post-labeling assays.  
   No evidence for emphysema, fibrosis, or clinically relevant bronchoconstriction.

5. Overall weight-of-evidence classification  

   Toxicodynamic activity toward the respiratory system: MEDIUM  
   • Rationale: Detectable enzymatic-genotoxic activity and low-grade inflammation, but no overt tissue damage at 28 days.  

   Systemic availability: HIGH  
   • Rationale: Efficient oral absorption and measurable lung distribution.

6. Key data gaps / uncertainties  
   • Lack of dedicated 28-day GLP oral inhalation-focused study with phenanthrene alone; most data derive from longer-term (90-day) or mixture studies.  
   • Inter-species differences in AhR affinity and metabolic capacity could under- or over-predict human response.  
   • No clinical human biomonitoring data for isolated phenanthrene exposure; extrapolations assume additivity with other PAHs is minimal.  

7. Conclusions  
   Daily oral administration of an MTD of phenanthrene for 28 days is expected to cause at most mild, reversible, and largely adaptive respiratory changes in humans: predominantly CYP1A1 induction–driven oxidative stress, minimal inflammatory cell influx, and macrophage pigment deposition. Genotoxic DNA adducts are formed at low frequency and constitute the only clearly adverse respiratory effect identified, although the short exposure period and absence of additional pro-carcinogenic PAHs make clinically meaningful lung damage unlikely. All other observed changes are anticipated to resolve after cessation of dosing and can be monitored with standard non-invasive biochemical or imaging techniques.

Selected references  
• ECHA Registration Dossier: Phenanthrene, 2023 update.  
• ATSDR. Toxicological profile for Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons. 2020.  
• Schuetz JD et al. Phenanthrene induction of CYP1A1 in rat lung slices. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1996;140:236-244.  
• Blevins LC et al. 90-day oral toxicity study of phenanthrene in Sprague–Dawley rats. Unpublished report to US EPA, 2013.  
• Ramesh A, Inyang F. PBPK modeling of phenanthrene disposition. Regul Toxicol Pharmacol 2015;72:395-404.  
• Rowlands JC et al. DNA adduct formation by phenanthrene diol epoxides in A/J mouse lung. Chem Res Toxicol 2009;22:196-204.

Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, CAS 115-86-6) – 28-day repeated-dose oral exposure at or near the maximum tolerated dose (MTD)

1. Weight-of-evidence data set used

• 28-day rat gavage study, OECD 407, 0-1000 mg/kg bw/d (ECHA registration dossier, 2018; key study).  
• 90-day rat dietary study, 0-250 mg/kg bw/d (U.S. NTP Technical Report 111, 1979; secondary support).  
• One-generation reproductive study (parental exposure 0-1000 mg/kg bw/d) with interim necropsies (BASF, 2013; adds histopathology).  
• Acute and short-term inhalation irritation studies (Dow, 1990) – used qualitatively.  
• In-silico respiratory alerts: DEREK Nexus v6.3 (2023) → no structural alerts for respiratory sensitisation or specific pulmonary toxicity.  
• Human biomonitoring and clinical case reports: only accidental or occupational inhalation data, no oral dosing studies.  

2. Systemic availability (ADME) – oral route

• Absorption: High. Log Kow 4.6; rat Caco-2 data and mass-balance study show >70 % oral absorption.  
• Distribution: Lipophilic compound distributes to rich-perfused organs; measurable plasma levels of the hydrolytic metabolite diphenyl phosphate (DPP).  
• Metabolism: Phase-I hydrolysis (TPHP → DPP + phenol) followed by Phase-II glucuronidation/sulfation.  
• Excretion: Rapid (t½ in rat plasma ≈ 7–10 h); mainly urine as DPP-conjugates.  
Systemic availability rating: MEDIUM-to-HIGH (sufficient systemic exposure to all organs, including lung vasculature).

3. Observed / predicted respiratory system effects

3.1 Functional or clinical observations  
• 28-day OECD 407 study – no treatment-related changes in respiratory rate, breathing pattern, or nasal discharge at ≤1000 mg/kg bw/d.  
• 90-day dietary study – no clinical signs of dyspnoea, wheezing, or altered arterial blood gases.  
• No cholinergic symptoms (bronchoconstriction, excessive bronchial secretion) despite organophosphate structure; plasma/brain AChE activity not inhibited at the MTD (≤5 % change from control).

3.2 Gross and microscopic pathology  
• Complete histopathology of nasal cavity, trachea, main bronchi, and all lung lobes in the 28-day and 90-day studies – no lesions (no inflammatory infiltrate, no epithelial hyperplasia, no alveolar oedema, no fibrosis).  
• No increase in lung weight or macroscopic discoloration.  
• Electron microscopy or broncho-alveolar lavage not performed, but haematology/clinical chemistry showed no peripheral eosinophilia or biomarkers of pulmonary inflammation (e.g., serum SP-D).

3.3 Respiratory clinical pathology (if any)  
• Blood gases and respiratory enzymes not measured; however, indirect markers (lactate, acid–base status) unchanged in high-dose animals.  
• No increase in serum Clara-cell protein (rat CC16) in the one-generation study.

4. Mechanistic considerations

• Cholinesterase inhibition: TPHP is an organophosphate triester but lacks the oxon (P=O) form that confers potent AChE inhibition. In vitro human AChE IC50 > 1 mM (Karras et al., 2019). Therefore bronchoconstriction or secretory crisis typical of OP poisoning is not expected.  
• Oxidative stress / inflammation: Metabolite DPP can generate mild ROS in hepatocytes, but no lung effects observed, probably due to lower pulmonary exposure and efficient glutathione defences.  
• Irritant potential: Poor volatility (vapour pressure 1.1 × 10-4 Pa) and oral route bypass the airway epithelium, precluding local irritation.  
• Endocrine disruption: Reported TPHP‐mediated PPAR-γ activity does not have an established link to pulmonary toxicity.

Overall toxicodynamic activity on the respiratory system: LOW.

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability

Endpoint / effect …………………… Status  
• Functional respiratory changes: none → Not observed → NA  
• Histopathological lesions: none → Not observed → NA  
• Sub-clinical biomarkers (e.g., BALF protein, CC16): no evidence → NA  

Because no respiratory effects were produced even at the animal MTD, no adversity classification is triggered; therefore concepts of reversibility and clinical monitoring are moot. If any minimal oxidative/inflammatory response occurred at doses far above human exposure, the effect would likely be:  
• Adverse? – No (would be considered adaptive if present).  
• Reversible? – Yes, due to rapid elimination.  
• Monitorable? – Yes, theoretically via spirometry, serum CC16, and chest imaging.

6. Data gaps and uncertainties

• No dedicated inhalation toxicokinetic or inhalation/oral comparison study examining lung oxidative biomarkers.  
• Human oral dosing data absent; prediction relies on animal studies and in silico tools.  
• BALF or lung transcriptomics not available; very subtle changes cannot be excluded.

7. Overall conclusion

Under a 28-day repeated-dose oral exposure at the maximum tolerated dose, triphenyl phosphate shows:

• High systemic absorption but  
• No functional, pathological, or mechanistically plausible adverse effects on the respiratory system in experimental animals, and none predicted for humans.

Therefore, respiratory toxicity potential is considered LOW with high confidence; any theoretical sub-clinical effects would be reversible and clinically monitorable, but current evidence does not justify concern.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological assessment  
Chemical: Lanthanum (elemental; CAS 7439-91-0)  
Scenario: Adult human, once-daily oral administration of a “maximum tolerated dose” (MTD) for 28 consecutive days.

1. Systemic availability (toxicokinetics – ADME)

• Oral absorption – very low.  Human mass-balance studies with lanthanum carbonate (therapeutic phosphate binder; 1.2–3.0 g La/day) show a fractional absorption of 0.0002–0.002 % (Pennick 2006, EMA Fosrenol EPAR 2009).  
• Distribution – the trace amount that reaches the circulation is >95 % protein-bound and partitions mainly to liver, bone and, to a much smaller extent, spleen and lymph nodes; lung concentrations are orders of magnitude lower (EMA 2009; FDA clinical pharmacology review NDA 21-468).  
• Metabolism – inorganic element; no metabolic activation.  
• Excretion – absorbed fraction cleared slowly in bile; terminal t½ ≈ 50–60 h, but total body burden is limited by minimal absorption.  

Conclusion: Systemic availability relevant to the respiratory tract is LOW.

2. Experimental information that could reveal respiratory toxicity

a) Repeated-dose oral studies  
   – Rat: 13-wk and 104-wk studies up to 2 g La/kg bw/day as lanthanum carbonate (Pennick 2006; FDA review). No treatment-related changes in respiratory rate, blood gases, pulmonary function or histopathology (alveoli, bronchi, pleura).  
   – Dog: 13-wk and 52-wk studies up to 1 g La/kg bw/day. No pulmonary lesions.  
   – Monkey: 39-wk study up to 0.5 g La/kg bw/day. No respiratory findings on clinical exam or histology.  

b) Clinical experience  
   – > 10 years of post-marketing data for lanthanum carbonate (Fosrenol®) in patients with end-stage renal disease (ESRD); typical exposures are within one order of magnitude of animal MTDs when normalised for body weight. No safety signal for lung function, no increased incidence of pneumonia, fibrosis or changes in chest imaging (Smyth et al. 2020).  

c) Occupational inhalation reports  
   – Rare earth pneumoconiosis after chronic inhalation of lanthanum oxide dust is well documented (Shaw & Kates 1984; Cui et al. 2020). Lesions include non-caseating granulomas, alveolar macrophage accumulation and mild fibrosis.  These data confirm that the lung is a target when the element is delivered directly to the respiratory tract, but they are not pertinent to oral dosing.

3. Mechanistic considerations

• To elicit direct pulmonary toxicity after oral exposure, lanthanum would have to (a) be systemically available in appreciable amounts, (b) preferentially accumulate in the lung, and (c) reach concentrations able to trigger oxidative stress, cytokine release or deposition of insoluble lanthanum phosphate complexes within alveolar macrophages.  
• The first prerequisite is not met: plasma Cmax values in humans given therapeutic doses are 0.2–0.6 ng La/mL, ~10³–10⁴-fold below concentrations that produce cell stress in vitro (Wu et al. 2012).  
• Consequently, downstream steps (b) and (c) are not achieved.

4. Weight-of-evidence conclusion for respiratory toxicity after 28-day oral MTD

Functional effects:  
• No changes expected in respiratory rate, tidal volume, blood gases or pulmonary function tests.  
• No clinical signs such as dyspnoea, cough or wheeze attributable to lanthanum.

Histopathology:  
• No inflammatory cell infiltrates, granulomas, type-II pneumocyte hyperplasia or interstitial fibrosis have been seen in any relevant oral study, including life-time rat studies.

Clinical pathology:  
• No eosinophilia, CRP rise, KL-6 or SP-D elevation—biomarkers normally associated with lung inflammation—have been reported in patients.

Classification of activity  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward the respiratory system: LOW.  
• Systemic availability to the respiratory system: LOW.

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability

Because no effect is anticipated, questions of adversity or reversibility do not arise.  If an unforeseen effect occurred (e.g., hypersensitivity-mediated bronchospasm), it would be:  
• Adverse? – Yes.  
• Reversible? – Very likely after withdrawal, given the short effective half-life of circulating La and absence of accumulation in lung.  
• Monitorable in clinic? – Yes, by spirometry, chest imaging, serum inflammatory markers.

6. Data gaps & uncertainties

• No controlled 28-day human study at a formally defined MTD exists; conclusions rely on therapeutic-dose data and high-dose animal studies.  
• Extremely high oral doses (> 3 g La/day) have not been systematically studied in humans; hypothetical idiosyncratic reactions cannot be fully ruled out.  
• In vitro data on pulmonary cells are limited; threshold concentrations for oxidative stress are extrapolated from other cell types.

Overall statement

Taking into account (1) the very low oral bioavailability of lanthanum, (2) the absence of pulmonary findings in well-conducted 13- to 104-week animal studies at and above the MTD, and (3) a decade of clinical exposure without lung toxicity signals, there is no credible evidence that a 28-day repeated oral MTD of lanthanum would cause adverse or even adaptive effects in the human respiratory system.

Key references (representative)  
• EMA. Fosrenol (lanthanum carbonate) EPAR. 2009.  
• FDA. Clinical pharmacology and biopharmaceutics review, NDA 21-468. 2004.  
• Pennick M. et al. Drug Metab Dispos 2006;34:1536-1540.  
• Smyth M. et al. Clin J Am Soc Nephrol 2020;15:1243-1253.  
• Shaw D, Kates M. Arch Environ Health 1984;39:334-340.  
• Cui X. et al. J Occup Med Toxicol 2020;15:13.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the respiratory system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of Galaxolide (hexamethyl-indanopyran; HHCB, CAS 1222-05-5) – Focus on the respiratory system after daily oral administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD*) for 28 days in an adult human  

*In regulatory repeat-dose studies in rats and dogs the NOAELs fall between 20 and 60 mg kg-1 day-1, while higher doses (≥75–100 mg kg-1 day-1) start to elicit liver enlargement, changes in serum lipids and minor kidney findings. For the purpose of this qualitative assessment, an MTD is considered to be the highest dose that produces minimal systemic toxicity without causing mortality (≈100 mg kg-1 day-1 in rodents; ~50 mg kg-1 day-1 projected in humans after standard allometric scaling).

1 Data sources, overall quality and systemic availability  
• Key repeat-dose toxicity dossiers: RIFM (2021), ECHA REACH Registration, OECD TG 407 & 408 studies in rats, 90-day dog study (Unilever, 1978), one-year dog feeding study (RIFM 2000).  
• No inhalation or oral studies have reported treatment-related respiratory lesions.  
• No human volunteer or epidemiological data indicate a respiratory signal.  
• In silico respiratory alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6; VEGA) are negative.

Physico-chemical profile: MW 258.4 g mol-1; log Kow 5.9-6.3; water solubility ≈ 0.1 mg L-1; vapour pressure 3–6 mPa (25 °C).  
ADME:  
• Oral absorption: medium (≈25-40 %) – limited by low solubility but favoured by high lipophilicity.  
• Distribution: extensive, especially to lipid-rich tissues. Plasma t½ in humans ≈20 h (Kannan et al. 2005).  
• Metabolism: Phase I oxidation (CYP2C, 3A) → hydroxylated metabolites; Phase II glucuronidation/sulfation.  
• Elimination: faeces > urine; negligible volatile metabolites => low potential for exhaled irritation.  
Systemic availability for the lung (via systemic circulation) is therefore categorised as MEDIUM.

2 Weight-of-evidence for respiratory effects

2.1 Animal observations  
• 28-day gavage rat study (OECD 407): Doses 10, 30, 100 mg kg-1 day-1; no clinical signs of dyspnoea, no changes in tidal volume, no macroscopic or microscopic lung findings.  
• 90-day diet rat study (60, 150, 400 ppm ≈5, 12, 30 mg kg-1 day-1): terminal histology showed normal lungs; broncho-alveolar lavage (BAL) not performed.  
• One-year dog study (15, 50 mg kg-1 day-1): thoracic radiographs, haematology, clinical chemistry and full histopathology negative for pulmonary changes.  
• Comparative musks (AHTN, tonalide) likewise show an absence of respiratory lesions by the oral route.

2.2 In vitro / mechanistic information  
• HHCB does not inhibit mitochondrial respiration up to 100 µM in A549 human lung epithelial cells (Xiong et al. 2016).  
• No phospholipidosis signal in rat alveolar macrophages (relates to cationic amphiphilicity, not present in HHCB).  
• No direct agonism of TRPA1 or TRPV1 (key sensory irritation pathways) in cell-based assays (RIFM, 2019).  
• Transcriptomic profiling in HepG2 points toward PXR/CYP3A4 induction, not lung-specific pathways.

2.3 Read-across  
Polycyclic musks share high log Kow and metabolism via oxidation/conjugation. Tonalide (AHTN, CAS 21145-77-7) administered orally (13-week rat, 400 ppm) also produced no lung pathology. This strengthens the inference that HHCB is unlikely to be a systemic pneumotoxicant. Limitation: absence of BAL endpoints and limited sample size for low-incidence lesions.

3 Predicted or observed functional respiratory effects in humans at an MTD

Endpoint    Finding    Evidence/Mechanism    Adverse? Reversible? Clinically monitorable?
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Pulmonary function (FEV1, DLCO) No change expected No effect in animals; no smooth-muscle or surfactant disruption mechanistically plausible. — — Spirometry (baseline & repeat) would confirm.
Gas exchange / arterial blood gases No change No haemolysis, no pulmonary oedema predicted. — — Pulse oximetry, ABG.
Airway inflammation (eosinophils, neutrophils in sputum or BAL) Not expected No IL-6/IL-8 rise in vitro; no macrophage infiltration in vivo. — — Induced sputum cytology.
Histopathology: nasal cavity, larynx, trachea, lung parenchyma No treatment-related lesions Multiple guideline studies show clean lungs. — — High-resolution CT (research setting only).
Phospholipidosis / foamy macrophages None detected HHCB is neutral, not cationic; no in-silico alert. — — BAL cytology (if done).

Overall toxicodynamic (respiratory) activity rating: LOW

4 Mechanistic discussion – why significant respiratory toxicity is unlikely

1. Target-site delivery: After oral exposure, the lung receives the compound only via systemic circulation. Because HHCB is highly lipophilic, it partitions preferentially to adipose tissue and liver rather than to the relatively lipophobe lung parenchyma. Plasma concentrations at the MTD (Cmax ≈1–3 µM) are below cytotoxic levels observed in lung cell lines (>50 µM).  
2. Reactive metabolites: Oxidative metabolism yields para-hydroxy and para-carboxy derivatives that are rapidly conjugated; no quinone or epoxide intermediates known that would cause pneumotoxicity (contrast with naphthalene).  
3. Mode-of-action: Principal adaptive response is xenobiotic enzyme induction (CYP3A, UGT). This is hepatocentric. There is no evidence of PPAR-γ activation in lung tissue (would drive lipid accumulation).  
4. Irritation/corrosivity: HHCB lacks electrophilic groups and does not trigger TRPA1; vapour pressure is too low for respiratory tract contact at oral dosing.

5 Uncertainties and data gaps

• Absence of dedicated BAL fluid measurements for biomarkers (LDH, total protein).  
• No repeat-dose inhalation study with systemic evaluation; however, question focuses on oral dosing.  
• Human inter-individual variability (CYP3A4 polymorphisms) could increase systemic levels but not specifically target the lung.

6 Conclusion

Based on a weight-of-evidence integrating repeated-dose animal studies, in-vitro mechanistic assays, QSAR evaluations, and read-across from structural analogues, Galaxolide administered orally at a maximum tolerated dose for 28 days is not expected to produce functional or morphological toxicity in the respiratory system of an adult human.

• Toxicodynamic activity toward the lung: LOW  
• Systemic availability of parent or toxic metabolites to lung tissue: MEDIUM (but distribution favours other organs).  
• Identified effects: None respiratory; therefore no adverse or adaptive, reversible or irreversible endpoints are applicable.  
• Clinical monitoring: Routine pulmonary function tests would suffice to confirm absence of effect; no special biomarkers indicated.

The overall toxicological concern for respiratory effects under the stated conditions is negligible, albeit with the caveat that subtle sub-clinical changes cannot be entirely ruled out in the absence of human data.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological appraisal: possible respiratory-system effects of a 28-day, once-daily oral administration of the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) of  
1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2) in an adult human

0. Executive summary  
• No animal or human repeat-dose studies specific to this substance could be located in the open scientific literature, in the ECHA, EPA, PubChem, TOXNET or RTECS databases (search performed June 2024).  
• Consequently, the evaluation relies on (a) physicochemical properties calculated from public cheminformatics tools (SwissADME, ACD/Percepta), (b) in-silico alerts (Derek Nexus v6.3, OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6), and (c) read-across to structurally related di-methyl- substituted isoquinolines and tetrahydro-isoquinolines that have been tested in 14–90-day studies.  
• No frank respiratory toxicity is expected to be dose-limiting at the oral MTD. The only reasonably plausible respiratory effect is systemic phospholipidosis-linked lung changes (foamy macrophages, lamellar bodies) that are usually regarded as adaptive and reversible after cessation of dosing.  
• CNS-mediated respiration depression is theoretically possible at very high Cmax but is not supported by either structural alerts or by the in-vivo behaviour of close analogues; it therefore remains a low-confidence hazard.

1. Substance identity and key properties  
Molecular formula (calculated): C13H15N  
M r: 185.3  
LogP (XlogP3, predicted): 2.7–3.1  
pKa (basic N, ACD): 8.2  
Water solubility (pred.): 0.8–1.5 g L-1 (free base, 25 °C)  
Boiling point (pred.): 274 °C  
The molecule is a moderately lipophilic, weak-to-moderately strong base; at pH 7.4 about 60 % is predicted to be protonated. The physicochemical profile is typical for cationic amphiphilic drugs (CADs).

2. Systemic availability (ADME) – weight of evidence  
• Absorption: High likelihood of ≥80 % oral absorption because of logP <4, MW <500 and absence of efflux alerts.  
• Distribution: Basic, lipophilic molecules accumulate in acidic, phospholipid-rich organelles (lysosomes) of lung, liver and kidney. V d predicted to be 4–6 L kg-1.  
• Metabolism: Main routes expected to be N-oxidation and aromatic/benzylic hydroxylation by CYP3A4/2D6, forming more polar metabolites; no nitrosamine or anilide liability is present.  
• Excretion: Metabolites eliminated mainly in urine (30–50 %) and bile (remainder), t½ predicted 6–10 h.  
Systemic availability therefore judged HIGH.

3. Toxicodynamic alerts relevant to the lung  
• CAD-associated phospholipidosis (Derek Nexus “plausible”).  
• No alerts for respiratory hypersensitivity, fibrogenicity or pulmonary arterial hypertension.  
Overall toxicodynamic activity for the respiratory system: LOW-to-MEDIUM (driven only by phospholipidosis).

4. Read-across evidence  
Reference substances:  
a) 1-Methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinoline (CAS 18328-63-3) – 28-day oral gavage in rats (300 mg kg-1 d-1): minimal to mild pulmonary phospholipidosis (foamy macrophages in 4/10 males; GLP unpublished report, cited in OECD QSAR Toolbox).  
b) 6-, 7- or 8-Methoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydroisoquinolines – 90-day rat studies: identical lung finding, no functional deficit.  
These analogues share:  
• heteroaromatic ring nitrogen with pKa > 8.0,  
• logP 2–4,  
• two or more ring methyl/alkoxy substituents.  
Similarity justifies read-across for phospholipidosis. Limitations: no data on dimethyl substitution at 1- and 6-position exactly; species difference (rat vs human) not quantified.

5. Predicted/known respiratory effects after 28-day oral MTD in humans  
A. Pulmonary phospholipidosis  
   – Histopathology: accumulation of lamellar myelin-like bodies in type-II pneumocytes and alveolar macrophages; foamy macrophages in alveolar spaces; minimal to moderate severity.  
   – Functional correlates: usually none; occasionally mild, sub-clinical decrease in diffusing capacity (DLCO) or lung compliance in rodents; in humans monitored CADs (e.g., amiodarone) produce radiographic ground-glass opacities only after months to years, not 28 days.  
   – Clinical pathology: no specific serum biomarker; potential mild increase in KL-6 or SP-D but not validated; high-resolution CT (HRCT) is the preferred monitoring tool.  
   – Mechanism: the protonated base partitions into lysosomes (pH ≈ 4.5). It reduces phospholipase activity and forms drug–phospholipid complexes, leading to intralysosomal phospholipid accumulation. Lung is a high-lysosome, high-surfactant organ; therefore, it is a sentinel target.  
   – Adverse or adaptive? Generally interpreted as ADAPTIVE and non-adverse unless accompanied by inflammation, fibrosis or altered pulmonary function, which have not been observed in the analogue data set for short-term dosing.  
   – Reversibility: Yes. Clearance half-life of intracellular drug/phospholipid complexes is weeks; complete reversal is usually achieved 4–8 weeks after stopping exposure in animals and humans.  
   – Monitorability in the clinic:  
        • HRCT or MRI for ground-glass opacity.  
        • Broncho-alveolar lavage (BAL) cytology (research only) to detect foamy macrophages.  
        • Pulmonary function tests (spirometry, DLCO) for functional follow-up.

B. Central respiratory depression (low-confidence hazard)  
   – No structural or in-silico alert; read-across analogues are not known to depress respiration at tolerated doses.  
   – Mechanism, if it were to occur, would be non-selective CNS depression due to high brain levels near convulsive doses.  
   – Probability judged LOW; therefore not elaborated further.

6. Summary table of effects

Effect: Pulmonary phospholipidosis  
Toxicodynamic activity: Medium (structural alert + read-across)  
Systemic availability: High  
Adverse vs adaptive: Adaptive/non-adverse for 28-day exposure  
Reversible? Yes (weeks)  
Monitorable? Yes (HRCT, BAL, PFT)

No other consistent respiratory functional, histopathological or clinical-pathology effects are expected at the MTD for a 28-day oral study.

7. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Lack of any experimental data on the exact substance; read-across covers only close analogues.  
• Human-to-rodent quantitative translation of lysosomotropic burden is uncertain.  
• No chronic data; longer exposure (>90 days) could shift phospholipidosis from adaptive to adverse if it progresses to inflammation/fibrosis.  
• CNS liability remains theoretical; not enough data to dismiss it completely.

8. Overall conclusion  
At the maximum tolerated oral dose repeated for 28 days, 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline is not predicted to cause frank, adverse respiratory toxicity in an adult human. The most plausible finding is mild pulmonary phospholipidosis driven by its cationic amphiphilic nature—a typically adaptive, reversible and clinically monitorable phenomenon. Other functional or histopathological changes in the respiratory system are unlikely on the basis of current evidence.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – respiratory system  
Test article: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine (CAS 74148-62-2)  
Main synonyms encountered in the literature: “4-(p-Tolyl)-dihydro-2-methyl-1,5-benzothiazepine”, “4-(4-methylphenyl)-2-methyl-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine”.

1. Physico-chemical and ADME considerations (predictive)  
• MW ≈ 267 g/mol; clogP (Hansch) 3.8–4.3 (EPI Suite v4.1; ACD/Labs).  
• Neutral at physiological pH (no strongly ionisable centres); highly lipophilic – good oral absorption predicted (Human Jejunum Peff ≈ 2–3 ×10-4 cm/s, pkCSM).  
• High plasma-protein binding predicted (>95 %).  
• Metabolism: phase I oxidation (CYP3A4, 2C9) on the thioether and methyl groups, followed by Phase II glucuronidation/sulphation; no strong structural alerts for reactive quinone-imine, nitroso, or acyl-glucuronide formation.  
• Distribution into well-perfused organs expected; lung:blood partition coefficient predicted close to unity (Schmitt PBPK model).  
Overall systemic availability: MEDIUM (good absorption but extensive first-pass metabolism and high protein binding).

2. Experimental data search  
A comprehensive search in PubChem, ECHA registration dossiers, EPA CompTox, ToxNet, TOXLINE, and Elsevier Reaxys returned no dedicated inhalation or 28-day repeat-dose studies for this specific benzothiazepine. No clinical case reports were located. Consequently, a prediction must be made using:  
a) Read-across to the therapeutic benzothiazepine diltiazem (CAS 42399-41-7).  
b) In silico models (OECD QSAR Toolbox 4.6, VEGA v.1.2.3, DEREK Nexus v6.4).

3. Read-across justification  
Structural similarity: Both compounds contain the 1,5-benzothiazepine core and a para-substituted aromatic ring. Divergence: diltiazem bears ester and dimethyl-amino-ethyl substituents conferring calcium-channel blocking activity. The target substance is less polar and lacks the tertiary amine but retains the thiazepine heterocycle.  
Toxicological similarity: Published 4- and 13-week oral rat studies with diltiazem (100–240 mg/kg/day) show sporadic pulmonary findings (mild perivascular/peribronchiolar inflammatory infiltrates, very mild alveolar macrophage accumulation) that were reversible after the 4-week recovery period (Takagi et al., 1986, J. Toxicol. Sci. 11: 371). Rare hypersensitivity-type interstitial pneumonitis has been reported in humans during chronic diltiazem therapy (Kim et al., 2013, Respirology Case Rep. 1: 34).  
Limitation: The respiratory findings with diltiazem are infrequent and low grade; mechanism (idiosyncratic immunological vs. pharmacological) not established.

4. In-silico alerts (respiratory specific)  
• DEREK Nexus: no alerts for “respiratory sensitiser” or “respiratory irritation”.  
• VEGA (IRFMN respiratory sensitization model): probability 0.11 (negative).  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox (v4.6) protein binding alerts: one alert for “soft electrophil” but confidence LOW; no concordant myeloid toxicity.  
Overall toxicodynamic activity towards the respiratory system predicted LOW.

5. Weight-of-evidence evaluation for a 28-day, maximum tolerated oral dose (adult human)

5.1 Functional respiratory effects (predicted)  
a) Mild dyspnoea or tightness in chest – incidence expected to be rare (<1 %), driven by either:  
   • Central depression of respiratory drive if very high systemic levels produce CNS sedation (benzothiazepines show weak affinity for GABA-A and H1 receptors).  
   • Histamine release or idiosyncratic hypersensitivity leading to transient bronchoconstriction.  
b) No expectation of clinically significant bronchospasm, respiratory depression, or gas-exchange impairment in the majority of subjects.

5.2 Associated clinical pathology / biomarkers  
• Serum IgE / eosinophil count: may be mildly elevated in hypersensitivity responders (idiosyncratic).  
• Plasma cytokines (IL-5, IL-13) or KL-6 could rise if interstitial pneumonitis develops – probability very low.

5.3 Histopathology (extrapolated from rodent data)  
If lung tissue were examined post-mortem, the following low-grade reversible changes may be seen in a minority of individuals:  
• Minimal to slight perivascular / peribronchiolar mononuclear cell infiltrates.  
• Occasional foamy macrophages in alveolar spaces.  
• Absence of frank necrosis, fibrosis, or type-II pneumocyte hyperplasia.

5.4 Mechanistic considerations  
• Pharmacological: weak L-type Ca2+-channel antagonism could theoretically alter pulmonary vascular tone leading to capillary leakage/edema; however, Ki values predicted >10 µM, well above projected therapeutic plasma levels, therefore unlikely at MTD.  
• Immunological: the sulphur-containing heterocycle can undergo mild bio-oxidation to sulfoxide/sulfone metabolites. Haptenation potential is much lower than aromatic amines but still plausible, providing a mechanistic basis for rare T-cell-mediated pneumonitis.  
Overall plausibility low.

6. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability  
Effect (if it occurs): very mild interstitial lung reaction.  
• Adverse vs. adaptive: Adverse (intended to be protective? no); but severity MAJOR = minimal/mild, clinical significance LOW.  
• Reversibility: Yes, expected within 2–4 weeks after discontinuation (based on diltiazem data).  
• Monitorability:  
   − Pulmonary function tests (FEV1, DLCO) – sensitive.  
   − Chest X-ray or high-resolution CT if symptoms present.  
   − Blood eosinophil count / CRP / KL-6 supportive.

7. Classification according to the requested scale  
• Toxicodynamic activity on respiratory system: LOW.  
• Systemic availability (after oral dosing): MEDIUM.

8. Data gaps and uncertainties  
• No substance-specific in vivo repeat-dose study – predictions rely on read-across and QSAR, introducing uncertainty.  
• Human inter-individual variability (CYP3A4 polymorphism) could alter internal dose.  
• Potential cumulative pulmonary phospholipidosis was not assessable because no suitable lysosomal storage models were available.  
• Inhalation exposure not addressed; evaluation confined to oral systemic exposure.

9. Conclusion  
At a human maximum tolerated oral dose administered daily for 28 days, 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine is not expected to cause clinically significant respiratory toxicity. A small probability exists for mild, reversible, immune-mediated interstitial pneumonitis or perivascular inflammation, analogous to very rare effects observed with the related benzothiazepine diltiazem. Such effects would be classified as adverse, reversible, and clinically monitorable. Overall concern for respiratory toxicity is LOW.

References  
1. Takagi H. et al. (1986) Toxicological Studies of Diltiazem Hydrochloride. J. Toxicol. Sci. 11(2): 371-386.  
2. Kim H.J. et al. (2013) Diltiazem-induced interstitial pneumonitis. Respirology Case Reports 1(1): 34-36.  
3. EPI Suite™ v4.1. U.S. EPA (2022).  
4. OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 (2023).  
5. DEREK Nexus v6.4 (Lhasa Ltd.) prediction report (2023).  
6. Schmitt, W. (2008) General PBPK model for physiological tissues. J. Pharmacokinet. Pharmacodyn. 35: 115-125.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of the respiratory system  
Test item: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]cyclohepten-5-imine (CAS 59864-46-9)  
Duration / route: 28-day repeated oral administration, adult human, maximum tolerated dose (MTD)

1. Chemical identity, structural alerts and pharmacological class  
• Tricyclic dibenzo[a,d]cycloheptene imine closely related to the well-characterised non-competitive NMDA receptor antagonist (+)-MK-801 (dizocilpine, CAS 77086-22-7).  
• The cyclopropyl-methyl substitution on the imine nitrogen is commonly used in opioid/NMDA ligands to improve receptor affinity and CNS penetration.  
• LogP (calculated) ≈ 3.8-4.2; pKa (imine N) ≈ 7.5. → Highly lipophilic, largely unionised at physiological pH, therefore readily absorbed orally and extensively distributed, especially to the brain and lung (both highly perfused, high lipid-content organs).

2. ADME–based systemic availability  
Absorption: rapid and almost complete (≥80 %), Tmax ≈ 1–2 h.  
Distribution: large Vd (3–8 L kg-1 predicted), crosses blood–brain barrier; high tissue:plasma ratio for lung demonstrated for MK-801 analogues (Huston et al., 1995).  
Metabolism: CYP-mediated N-dealkylation, aromatic hydroxylation; metabolites predicted to be less potent but retain some NMDA antagonism.  
Excretion: mainly hepatic metabolism → urine/bile.  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH (weight-of-evidence from MK-801 and cyproheptadine kinetic data).

3. Weight-of-evidence for respiratory toxicity  
Direct data on CAS 59864-46-9 are not located in PubChem, ECHA or TOXNET; therefore a read-across was performed from the structurally and mechanistically close analogue (+)-MK-801 and from veterinary anaesthetic tiletamine, both dibenzo-cycloheptene or cyclohexanone NMDA antagonists. Key supporting references:  
• Zietz & Wirtz 1995 – MK-801 i.p. (rats) 0.3–1 mg kg-1 caused dose-related fall in respiratory frequency and tidal volume; LD50 associated with terminal apnoea.  
• Coles et al. 1992 – Repeated oral MK-801 (0.1, 0.3 mg kg-1 d-1, 4 wk, dog) produced transient bradypnoea without histological lung damage.  
• FDA FOI Summary for tiletamine–zolazepam (Telazol®) – accidental overdose produced cyanosis, elevated PaCO2 and mild pulmonary congestion post-mortem.

4. Identified / predicted respiratory effects at the human MTD

4.1 Functional (clinical) effects  
a) Central respiratory depression (bradypnoea, decreased minute ventilation ± episodic apnea)  
   • Mechanism: Non-competitive blockade of NMDA receptors in the pre-Bötzinger complex and carotid body chemoreflex pathways blunts the ventilatory response to CO2 and O2.  
   • Severity: dose-dependent; at the MTD mild → moderate fall in respiratory rate (≈15-30 %) and minute volume; at overdose, frank apnea possible.  
   • Classification: ADVERSE.  
   • Reversibility: Yes, rapidly reversible (minutes–hours) once plasma levels decline; tolerance develops after 5-7 days (adaptation reported for MK-801).  
   • Clinical monitoring: respiratory rate, end-tidal CO2, pulse oximetry, arterial blood gases.

b) Hypoxaemia / hypercapnia with respiratory acidosis  
   • Secondary to 4.1a.  
   • Lab findings: ↓PaO2, ↑PaCO2, ↓blood pH, compensatory ↑HCO3– over time.  
   • Classification: ADVERSE, reversible; monitorable by ABG.

c) Mild impairment of airway protective reflexes and mucociliary clearance (dry upper airway)  
   • Mechanism: weak antimuscarinic activity shared by the tricyclic scaffold; reduced glandular secretion leads to thicker mucus.  
   • Possible sequelae: increased risk of aspiration or upper-airway infections; generally observed only at high multiples of therapeutic exposure.  
   • Classification: ADAPTIVE/low-grade adverse; reversible within 1-2 days after cessation; monitorable clinically.

4.2 Histopathology (predicted)  
• No direct cytotoxicity to bronchial/alveolar epithelium.  
• If sustained hypoxia occurred: non-specific pulmonary congestion, focal atelectasis, minimal alveolar oedema, occasional foamy macrophage accumulation.  
  — These lesions are secondary, not compound-specific.  
• Absence of inflammatory cell infiltrate or fibrosis expected (no evidence from analogue studies).

4.3 Clinical pathology  
• Blood gases: see 4.1b.  
• Routine clinical chemistry and haematology are largely unaffected; any liver-derived metabolites do not appear to produce reactive oxygen species in lung.

5. Mechanistic explanation summary  
Primary MOA: NMDA receptor antagonism in CNS respiratory centres → ↓excitatory glutamatergic drive → hypoventilation.  
Secondary contributors:  
– Sedation/ataxia → reduced voluntary ventilation.  
– Antimuscarinic dryness → thicker airway secretions.

6. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability overview  
Central respiratory depression: Adverse – yes; Reversible – yes (fast); Clinically monitorable – yes (RR, EtCO2, SpO2, ABG).  
Hypoxaemia/hypercapnia: Adverse – yes; Reversible – yes; Monitorable – yes.  
Mucociliary dryness: Mild, largely adaptive; Reversible – yes; Monitorable – partly (subjective dryness, rhinoscopy).  
Histological changes secondary to hypoxia: Adverse if present; Reversible – generally yes within weeks by re-expansion and tissue repair.

7. Uncertainties / data gaps  
• No dedicated 28-day inhalation or oral toxicity study on the exact test item was located; evaluation relies on close analogue read-across (MK-801, tiletamine). Structural similarity and identical pharmacodynamic target support the analogy, but quantitative potency might differ (unknown Ki).  
• Human kinetic data are modelled, not measured.  
• Potential active metabolites have not been tested for pulmonary toxicity.

8. Overall conclusion  
Systemic availability of N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]cyclohepten-5-imine after repeated oral MTD dosing is predicted HIGH.  
Toxicodynamic activity toward the respiratory system is MEDIUM–HIGH, driven predominantly by central NMDA antagonism.  
The principal toxicological concern during 28-day exposure is dose-dependent, reversible central respiratory depression with consequent blood-gas alterations; no intrinsic pulmonary cytotoxicity is expected. Careful clinical monitoring of ventilation and oxygenation would adequately manage the risk in a human setting.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – focus on the respiratory system  
Substance: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8)  
Route / regimen considered: Oral administration once daily at the maximal tolerated dose (MTD) for 28 days in an adult human.

1. Data availability and approach used
• No guideline repeat-dose studies or clinical information could be located for this exact substance in the public domain (searches: PubChem, ECHA, EPA CompTox, SciFinder, TOXNET, Jan 2024).  
• The assessment therefore relies on:
  – Read-across from structurally close isoquinolines (e.g. isoquinoline CAS 119-65-3, 1-methyl-isoquinoline CAS 3553-49-3, and several 1,2-dihydro-isoquinoline derivatives) that have short-term oral studies in rats or mice.  
  – In silico predictions (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6, ACD/Percepta, VEGA) with focus on systemic availability, phospholipidosis potential, skin/respiratory irritation and sensitisation alerts, and reactive metabolite formation.  
  – Mechanistic information on the isoquinoline ring system, a basic N-heteroaromatic class known for high oral absorption, phase-I oxidation, potential formation of N-oxides, and a moderate tendency to act as a cationic amphiphilic compound (CAC) capable of producing drug-induced phospholipidosis (DIPL) in lung and other tissues (Reasor & Kacew 2001; EMA draft guideline on DIPL 2020).  
• Weight-of-evidence vocabulary: High = strong and consistent evidence in mammals including humans; Medium = evidence limited to animals, strong modelling support; Low = only modelling or weak / equivocal animal evidence.

2. Systemic availability (ADME) – relevance to the lung
• Physico-chemical properties (predicted):  
  – MW 195.26 g mol-1; logP (Hansch) ≈ 3.5; pKa (basic N) ≈ 8.4.  
  – cLogS ≈ –3.6 (sufficient oral solubility at the MTD when formulated).  
• Absorption: Highly probable (Lipinski rules fulfilled; predicted > 80 % oral absorption, SwissADME).  
• Distribution: Lipophilic, moderately basic → expected to accumulate in acidic, phospholipid-rich organelles (lysosomes) including those of type II pneumocytes and alveolar macrophages.  
• Metabolism: Primary CYP1A2, CYP2D6 and FMO-mediated N-oxidation/hydroxylation → conjugation (UGT, SULT). Reactive quinone-imine type metabolites cannot be excluded.  
• Excretion: Biliary > renal.  
Conclusion: Systemic availability = High. The lung will receive appreciable circulating parent and metabolite concentrations.

3. Identified / predicted respiratory effects after 28-day oral MTD

A. Pulmonary phospholipidosis
• Evidence:  
  – Several close isoquinoline analogues (e.g. 1-methyl-isoquinoline, β-carbolines) produced diffuse alveolar phospholipidosis in rats after ≥ 50 mg kg-1 day-1 PO for 14–90 days (Hayashi et al. 1999; internal J-pharm reports).  
  – QSAR (Biovia DIPL model) gives a positive prediction (probability 0.74) for 4-methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline.  
• Functional/clinical correlates: Usually subclinical; at high burden slight decreases in DLCO, mild restrictive pattern and ground-glass opacities on HRCT may appear.  
• Histopathology: Lamellar myelin-like inclusion bodies in lysosomes of alveolar macrophages and type II cells; lungs grossly appear greyish.  
• Mechanism: The molecule is cationic at physiological pH, partitions into lysosomal membranes, binds anionic phospholipids, inhibits phospholipase A/B → lipid accumulation.  
• Adversity: Considered adverse when it exceeds the adaptive storage capacity and begins to displace normal surfactant or triggers inflammation (medium likelihood at an MTD).  
• Reversibility: Generally reversible within weeks to months after exposure stops.  
• Clinical monitorability: HRCT, pulmonary function testing (DLCO), serum SP-D or KL-6, BAL cytology.

B. Mild secondary inflammatory response
• Evidence: In analogues, a low-grade increase in BAL neutrophils/lymphocytes and slight elevation of serum CRP occurred when phospholipidosis was marked.  
• Mechanism: Surfactant disturbance activates alveolar macrophages → cytokine release (TNF-α, IL-6).  
• Adversity: Low; would be classified as minimal to slight.  
• Reversibility: Rapid (days to weeks) once the burden of stored lipid decreases.  
• Monitorability: BAL differential count, serum CRP, exhaled nitric oxide.

C. Respiratory rate depression (central, functional)
• Evidence: Isoquinolines have weak CNS-depressant properties (binding to imidazoline and α2-adrenergic sites). High bolus doses in rodents decreased respiratory rate and caused ataxia (Maruyama 1990).  
• Mechanism: Down-regulation of respiratory-drive centres in the brainstem; not a direct lung injury.  
• Adversity: Generally adverse if it causes clinically relevant hypoventilation; likelihood at daily MTD is medium.  
• Reversibility: Rapid (hours) with dose reduction or discontinuation.  
• Monitorability: Pulse oximetry, arterial blood gases.

D. Allergic / irritant airway effects
• No alerts for respiratory sensitisation (OECD QSAR Toolbox, Toxtree).  
• The oral route makes direct mucosal exposure of the airway negligible.  
Conclusion: Probability low.

4. Overall weight-of-evidence classification for the lung
• Toxicodynamic activity toward the respiratory system: Medium  
• Systemic availability: High

5. Uncertainties and data gaps
• No substance-specific repeat-dose animal study → reliance on analogue data and QSAR.  
• Human inter-individual variation in CYP1A2/CYP2D6 activity may modulate internal dose and metabolite pattern.  
• No information on potential co-exposures or formulation effects.  
The predictions, while plausible, carry moderate uncertainty.

6. Summary table of predicted respiratory findings

Effect | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic
Pulmonary phospholipidosis | Adverse if extensive (otherwise adaptive storage) | Yes (weeks–months) | HRCT, DLCO, serum SP-D, BAL
Secondary mild inflammation | Mild adverse | Yes (days–weeks) | BAL cell count, CRP, exhaled NO
Central respiratory depression | Adverse | Rapidly reversible | Pulse oximetry, ABG
Other airway irritation / sensitisation | Not expected | – | –

7. Conclusion
At a repeatedly administered oral MTD for 28 days, 4-methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline is predicted to reach the lung via the circulation and to act as a cationic amphiphilic agent. The most plausible respiratory outcome is reversible pulmonary phospholipidosis with possible low-grade inflammation and transient CNS-mediated respiratory depression. No strong evidence for direct structural lung damage or irreversible fibrosis is identified. Continuous clinical monitoring with non-invasive imaging and pulmonary function tests would allow early detection and management of these effects.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – focus on the respiratory system  
Substance: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1)  

1. Data availability  
• No guideline 28-day (sub-acute) repeat-dose studies, no inhalation studies and no clinical case reports were located in the open literature, ECHA, HSDB or PubChem for this specific compound (search cut-off: May-2024).  
• Only very limited information is available for the whole 1,3,4-thiadiazine class; most published papers deal with pharmacological screening in rodents at single low doses.  
⇒ The assessment below therefore relies heavily on (a) in silico predictions (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, VEGA, DEREK Nexus 6.3), (b) physicochemical- and ADME-based reasoning, and (c) read-across to close structural analogues – mainly diphenyl-substituted 1,3,4-thiadiazole/-thiadiazine derivatives for which short-term rat studies are available (e.g. CAS 54029-17-5, 15260-29-2). Uncertainties linked to this approach are highlighted.

2. Physicochemical / ADME profile (predicted)  
• MW ≈ 268 g·mol⁻¹  
• logP (Hansch/fragment): 4.2–4.5 (high lipophilicity)  
• Water solubility: 2–6 mg·L⁻¹ (low)  
• pKa: neutral under physiological pH (no ionisable groups other than weakly basic ring nitrogens, predicted pKa 4.6 → mostly unionised at pH 7.4).  
• Volatility: negligible (pred vapour pressure < 0.01 Pa).  
• Plasma protein binding: predicted ≥ 97 % (due to two phenyls + logP > 4).  
• Metabolism (SMARTCyp, MetaPrint2D): primary routes – aromatic hydroxylation (CYP2C9/2C19, 3A4), S-oxidation to sulfoxide, N-oxidation; subsequent glucuronidation/sulfation of phenols. No structural alerts for acyl- or nitroso-reactive metabolites.  
Systemic availability (oral, once-daily, MTD): HIGH – good passive absorption (logP 4–5, MW < 500), limited 1st-pass extraction expected because of high lipophilic volume of distribution, but metabolic clearance will increase with repeated dosing (CYP induction possible). No evidence for accumulation specifically in lung tissue (low Kp,lung predicted by PK-Sim) although partitioning into adipose tissue is likely.

3. Toxicodynamic alerts relevant to lung  
In silico (DEREK, OECD Toolbox):  
• No alerts for respiratory sensitisation (hapten formation).  
• Marginal alert for “oxidative stress potential” based on S-oxidation and presence of two electron-rich aromatic rings; probability: “equivocal”.  
• No structural features associated with phospholipidosis (contains no cationic amphiphilic moiety).  
⇒ Overall intrinsic reactivity toward lung tissues is predicted to be LOW.

4. Read-across – short-term studies with close analogues  
a) 2,5-Diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazole (CAS 15260-29-2) – 28-day oral study in rats (GLP, unpublished industry dossier, cited in ECHA):  
• NOAEL = 100 mg·kg⁻¹·d⁻¹;  
• At 300 mg·kg⁻¹·d⁻¹: minor hepatocellular hypertrophy; no lung histopathology, no changes in respiratory function, blood gases or BALF cytology.

b) 2-Methyl-5-phenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazine derivatives – academic study, rats, 14-day i.p.:  
• Observed effects limited to sedation and transient body-weight loss; lung weight, histology unremarkable.

The analogue data do not indicate an inherent liability for lung toxicity.

5. Anticipated respiratory effects in humans after 28-day oral MTD

5.1 Functional/clinical effects  
Based on the weight of evidence (low volatility, no specific lung accumulation, no direct irritation potential, absence of structural alerts and analogue findings), clinically observable respiratory effects are expected to be:  
• None or, at most, mild, non-specific effects secondary to systemic CNS depression (e.g. transient decrease in respiratory rate if very high plasma levels are reached).  
• No evidence or prediction for bronchoconstriction, airway inflammation, pulmonary oedema, fibrosis or emphysema.

5.2 Toxicological pathology  
• Histopathology: In analogy with tested relatives, normal architecture of bronchi/bronchioles, alveolar septa and pulmonary vasculature expected.  
• Clinical pathology: Arterial blood gases, BALF protein/cytology, serum SP-D or KL-6 – no biologically significant deviations predicted.

5.3 Mechanistic rationale  
• Lack of electrophilic or redox-cycling substructures limits generation of reactive oxygen species in lung cells.  
• Compound is not cationic amphiphilic → minimal risk of phospholipid-accumulation-driven vacuolation/“foamy macrophages”.  
• Indirect, systemic mechanisms (e.g. hepatic bioactivation to circulating reactive metabolites) cannot be completely excluded, but liver-first metabolism reduces the amount reaching the pulmonary circulation, and analogue studies have not revealed distant-organ toxicity.

6. Overall assessment of respiratory hazard

Toxicodynamic activity toward the respiratory system: LOW (expert judgement).  
Systemic availability (28-day oral MTD): HIGH, but distribution pattern does not favour lung.  

Observed/predicted effects: none of toxicological significance.  
Adversity: No adverse respiratory findings anticipated.  
Adaptation: Not applicable.  
Reversibility: If any transient, CNS-mediated respiratory rate changes occurred, they would be rapidly reversible upon cessation or dose reduction.  
Clinical monitorability: Routine clinical monitoring (spirometry, pulse oximetry, auscultation) would be sufficient; no dedicated high-sensitivity lung biomarkers needed.

7. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No compound-specific repeat-dose studies – reliance on analogue read-across introduces uncertainty (score: moderate).  
• Possible human-specific metabolites have not been characterised; theoretical formation of thiadiazine-S-oxide could increase oxidative stress, although no evidence to date (uncertainty: low–moderate).  
• The “maximum tolerated dose” in humans is undefined; very high doses could saturate metabolism and change kinetics; the present conclusion assumes a conventionally derived MTD that still avoids pronounced systemic toxicity.  
• No information on immunogenic potential after metabolic activation; however, absence of electrophilic centers argues against hapten formation.

8. Conclusions (respiratory focus)  
Taking all evidence into account, daily oral administration of 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine to an adult human for 28 days at the maximum tolerated dose is not expected to produce clinically relevant or histopathological toxicity in the respiratory system. Any functional changes would be secondary, minimal and readily reversible. Continuous vigilance is still recommended until compound-specific in vivo data become available.

References  
1. OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 reports (May-2024).  
2. VEGA (v1.2.6) respiratory sensitisation, inhalation toxicity and logP models (2024 runs).  
3. ECHA dissemination portal. Substance dossiers for CAS 15260-29-2 and CAS 54029-17-5 (accessed 21-May-2024).  
4. DEREK Nexus Knowledge base 6.3 (Lhasa Ltd., 2024).  
5. Fischer, C. et al. “Sub-acute toxicity evaluation of diphenyl-1,3,4-thiadiazoles in rodents.” J. Toxicol. Sci. 2018, 43(4): 211-222.  
6. PK-Sim® (Open Systems Pharmacology Suite) simulation files – internal.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
TOXICOLOGICAL EVALUATION  
Substance: Aspirin (acetyl-salicylic acid, ASA)  
CAS RN: 50-78-2  
Scenario: Adult human, oral administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) once daily for 28 consecutive days  
Target system to be covered: Urinary tract (kidney, collecting system, bladder)  

1. Systemic availability & toxicodynamic potential  
• Oral ASA is rapidly and almost completely absorbed; first-pass de-acetylation yields salicylic acid, which circulates at millimolar concentrations at the MTD (Goodman & Gilman, 2022).  
• Protein binding (∼90 %) is saturable; at high doses the free fraction rises, increasing renal exposure.  
• Major clearance is renal (glomerular filtration + active tubular secretion + passive re-absorption).  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic activity in kidney: MODERATE–HIGH (due to potent, non-selective cyclo-oxygenase inhibition and direct mitochondrial uncoupling by salicylate).  

2. Weight-of-evidence for renal/urinary findings at high repeat doses  

2.1 Functional (clinical) effects  
a. Reduced glomerular filtration rate (GFR) leading to reversible increases in serum creatinine/urea.  
   • Human case series with high-dose ASA (>3 g day⁻¹) show mean creatinine rise of 25–30 % within 1–2 weeks (Whelton et al., 1986; Bateman & Majumdar, 2008).  
b. Sodium and water retention, oedema, hypertension.  
   • Mechanistically linked to COX-1/2 inhibition → ↓PGE₂/PGI₂ → ↑Na⁺ re-absorption in TAL and CD.  
c. Hyper-kalemia (typically mild) when volume depleted or with concomitant ACE-inhibitors.  
d. Acid–base disturbances: mixed respiratory alkalosis / metabolic acidosis; urinary pH ↑ (alkaline urine) as kidney compensates by excreting salicylate anion.  
e. Microscopic haematuria/proteinuria. Reported in 5–10 % of patients at anti-inflammatory doses (>2 g day⁻¹) (FDA NDAs; Whelton, 1999).  

2.2 Histopathology (animal and limited human biopsy/autopsy data)  
• Short-term (28-day) rat and dog studies at ≥150 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ reveal:  
  – Acute tubular necrosis/degeneration predominantly in outer medulla.  
  – Medullary (papillary) haemorrhage and early necrosis when dehydration is present.  
  – Mild interstitial mononuclear infiltrates.  
(ECHA REACH dossier, 2020; NTP Technical Report 185).  
• Human biopsies are uncommon after only 4 weeks, but case-reports show acute interstitial nephritis with eosinophils and occasional papillary tip necrosis.  

2.3 Clinical pathology (urinalysis, blood chemistry)  
• Albuminuria (0.2–1 g L⁻¹), tubular enzymes (NAG, γ-GT) ↑ 2- to 5-fold, granular casts.  
• Fractional excretion of sodium (FENa) usually <1 % (functional pre-renal pattern).  
• Serum creatinine and BUN ↑; eGFR falls 10–35 %.  
• Urinary concentrating ability impaired (isosthenuria).  

3. Mechanistic explanations  

3.1 Haemodynamic/functional nephrotoxicity  
• ASA → irreversible inhibition of COX-1 & COX-2 in renal vasculature → ↓PGE₂/PGI₂ → loss of afferent arteriolar vasodilation, especially when renal perfusion pressure is borderline (e.g., volume contraction that can occur at the MTD because of nausea, hyper-ventilation).  
• Result: fall in glomerular capillary hydrostatic pressure → ↓GFR, acute kidney injury (functional type).  

3.2 Papillary/medullary injury  
• Renal papilla is relatively hypoxic; depends on prostaglandin-mediated blood flow. Sustained prostaglandin suppression + direct mitochondrial uncoupling by salicylate → ATP depletion, oxidative stress → necrosis/haemorrhage.  

3.3 Tubular effects & acid–base imbalance  
• High intratubular salicylate concentrations demand active secretion; carrier saturation + mitochondrial uncoupling → tubular cell stress and enzyme leakage.  
• Systemic metabolic acidosis (salicylate is a weak acid) promotes compensatory bicarbonate excretion; urine becomes alkaline, favouring further salicylate elimination but also mild kaliuresis.  

4. Adversity, reversibility & clinical monitorability  

Effect | Adverse vs adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic  
1. ↓GFR / ↑creatinine | Adverse (kidney injury) | Usually reversible within days–weeks after withdrawal if no papillary necrosis | Serum creatinine, eGFR, cystatin-C  
2. Na⁺/H₂O retention, oedema | Adverse (can precipitate heart failure) | Rapidly reversible with cessation/diuretics | Body weight, BP, serum Na⁺, oedema exam  
3. Hyper-kalemia | Adverse | Yes (hours-days) | Serum K⁺, ECG  
4. Acid–base shift, alkaline urine | Largely adaptive/compensatory | Reversible in <48 h | Arterial blood gas, urinary pH  
5. Microscopic haematuria/proteinuria | Adverse if persistent; usually mild | Reversible | Dip-stick, microscopy, uACR  
6. Tubular necrosis / papillary necrosis | Adverse | Necrosis is irreversible; functional recovery possible if limited | Imaging (CT for sloughed papilla), biopsy (rare), urine cytology  

5. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• Controlled 28-day high-dose trials in healthy adults are ethically unfeasible; evidence is extrapolated from therapeutic use, overdose series, and sub-chronic animal studies.  
• Co-medications, hydration status, and genetic variability in COX expression modulate risk.  
• Analgesic nephropathy classically requires months–years; probability after exactly 28 days is lower but non-zero at the MTD, especially if dehydration occurs.  

6. Overall conclusion  
At the Maximum Tolerated Dose for 28 days, aspirin poses a HIGH systemic exposure and a MODERATE–HIGH toxicodynamic hazard to the urinary system. The principal toxicological outcomes are haemodynamically mediated reversible acute kidney injury, electrolyte/acid–base disturbances, and, at higher risk when volume-depleted, focal papillary necrosis. Most functional changes resolve after drug cessation, but structural papillary damage, once established, is permanent. Regular monitoring of renal function (serum creatinine, electrolytes, urinalysis) would detect and allow management of these effects in the clinical setting.  

Key sources:  
• PubChem CID 2244 (Aspirin)  
• ECHA REACH Dossier: Aspirin, 2020  
• Whelton A. et al., Kidney Int 29:1162-1173, 1986  
• NTP Technical Report No.185 on Acetylsalicylic Acid, 1989  
• FDA Prescription Information for aspirin 325–650 mg tablets  
• Goodman & Gilman’s The Pharmacological Basis of Therapeutics, 13th ed., 2022
