vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
TOXICOLOGICAL EVALUATION  
Substance  : Paracetamol (Acetaminophen, 4-acetamidophenol)  
CAS No.   : 103-90-2  
Scenario  : Daily oral administration of the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) to an adult human for 28 days.  
Focus    : Urinary system (kidneys, ureters, bladder) – functional, clinical-pathology and histopathology end-points.

1 Systemic availability and toxicodynamic potential  
• Oral bio-availability ≈ 75–88 % (Prescott, 2000; PubChem CID : 1983).  
• Cmax 0.5–1.5 h; low plasma protein binding (≈ 20 %).  
• 90–95 % of the dose is cleared renally as glucuronide- and sulphate-conjugates; <5 % is oxidised by CYP2E1, CYP3A4 and CYP1A2 to the reactive quinone-imine N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone-imine (NAPQI). The kidney possesses the same CYP isoforms and receives high concentrations of both parent drug and metabolites.  
Classification  
– Systemic availability: HIGH (efficient absorption and renal excretion).  
– Toxicodynamic activity in kidney: MEDIUM-to-HIGH (formation of NAPQI, oxidative stress, prostaglandin depletion).

2 Weight-of-evidence for renal / urinary toxicity at or near the human MTD  
Human data  
• The therapeutic maximum (≤ 4 g day⁻¹) is generally well tolerated for ≤ 2 weeks, but prospective series and pharmaco-epidemiology indicate an increased incidence of mild, transient rises in serum creatinine (≃ 1.2- to 1.5-fold) when 4 g day⁻¹ is given for 14–30 days, especially in subjects with concomitant alcohol intake or low glutathione (Matzke & Prescott, 2002; Perneger et al., 1994).  
• Acute overdose (> 10 g single dose) causes acute kidney injury (AKI) in ≈ 1–2 % of patients, occasionally in the absence of liver failure (Ostapowicz et al., 2002). Biopsy findings include acute tubular necrosis (ATN) and patchy papillary necrosis.  
• Chronic “analgesic nephropathy” historically attributed to phenacetin has also been reported for long-term high paracetamol use (> 1 kg life-time), with renal papillary necrosis and interstitial fibrosis (Delzell & Shulman, 2010). These doses exceed a 28-day MTD scenario, but demonstrate biological plausibility.

Animal data  
• 28-day gavage in rats: 1000 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ produced dose-related increases in urinary glucose, protein and renal cortical degeneration (Brown et al., 1973).  
• 13-week NTP study (B6C3F1 mice): 260–1040 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ resulted in cortical proximal tubular necrosis and papillary mineralisation; LOEL ≈ 260 mg kg⁻¹ (NTP TR 450, 1993).  
• Rat isolated-perfused kidney and in-vitro proximal-tubule models confirm NAPQI-mediated glutathione depletion, lipid peroxidation and cell death (Saito et al., 2010).

3 Urinary-system endpoints likely at the 28-day human MTD (≈ 4 g day⁻¹)

3.1 Functional / clinical pathology changes  
Endpoint     Expected magnitude   Mechanistic basis      Evaluation  
a) Mild rise in serum creatinine and BUN (≤ 1.5× ULN)  • GFR decline secondary to proximal-tubule injury (ATP depletion, oxidative stress). Adverse; usually reversible; monitorable (SCr, eGFR).  
b) Transient microscopic haematuria / proteinuria    • Tubular membrane damage, papillary micro-erosions. Adverse; reversible; monitorable (dipstick, U‐ACR).  
c) Reduced urinary concentrating ability / polyuria   • Loss of medullary gradient from papillary ischaemia & prostaglandin inhibition. Initially adaptive; reversible; monitorable (osmolality test).  
d) Increases in early kidney-injury biomarkers (KIM-1, NGAL, NAG, β₂-microglobulin) • Sensitive indicators of tubular stress before overt AKI. Adaptive-to-early adverse; reversible; highly monitorable.

3.2 Histopathology expected at MTD (data extrapolated from animals & human biopsy)  
Lesion      Location   Description  
i) Cytoplasmic vacuolation, loss of brush border, single-cell necrosis  Proximal convoluted tubule (PCT) cortex Early reversible degeneration.  
ii) Focal tubular necrosis with granular casts  PCT & outer medulla Associated with elevated SCr/BUN.  
iii) Patchy papillary necrosis / medullary interstitial oedema Inner medulla & papilla May be irreversible if extensive; can progress to scarring.  
iv) Tubulo-interstitial inflammation Cortex‐medulla junction Secondary inflammatory response.

4 Mechanistic considerations (“why and how”)

4.1 Bioactivation to NAPQI in kidney  
Paracetamol → CYP-mediated oxidation → NAPQI (highly electrophilic)  
NAPQI + GSH → conjugate (detoxification).  
At MTD, renal glutathione may fall by ≥ 50 % within hours (Saito et al., 2010). When GSH is depleted, NAPQI binds cellular proteins (thiols on mitochondrial complexes), triggering:  
• Mitochondrial oxidative stress, ATP depletion  
• Increased intracellular Ca²⁺ and activation of endonucleases → necrotic / apoptotic death of tubular cells.

4.2 Prostaglandin inhibition & medullary ischaemia  
High paracetamol doses inhibit renal COX-derived PGE₂. In hypoxic renal medulla this can lead to vasoconstriction and papillary hypoperfusion, pre-disposing to papillary necrosis (Mattia et al., 2009).

4.3 Oxidative and nitrosative stress amplification  
ROS generated in step 4.1 activate MAPK pathways (JNK) that further impair mitochondrial function; peroxynitrite formation damages DNA and proteins.

5 Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitoring

Effect          Adverse/Adaptive Reversible? Clinical monitoring tools  
• Mild PCT degeneration  Adverse (low grade) Yes (days–weeks) SCr, cystatin-C, NGAL, KIM-1.  
• Acute tubular necrosis  Adverse (moderate) Usually reversible unless severe Serial SCr/eGFR, urine output, imaging.  
• Papillary necrosis    Adverse (serious) Often irreversible (structural loss) Ultrasound/CT urography, haematuria, renal papillae fragments in urine.  
• Slight polyuria / ↓U-osm Adaptive early; adverse if persistent Yes Fluid balance, urine osmolality.  

6 Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No controlled 28-day human studies at exactly the MTD (4 g day⁻¹) powered for renal end-points; most evidence is post-marketing or overdose.  
• Genetic polymorphisms in CYP2E1, GSTP1 and NAT that modulate NAPQI burden introduce inter-individual variability.  
• Concomitant alcohol, fasting, or nephrotoxic co-medication (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides) can greatly intensify risk.  
• Rodent NOAELs for kidney are lower (on mg kg⁻¹ basis) than clinical MTD, raising uncertainty in cross-species margin.

7 Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion for a 28-day human MTD regime  
Toxicodynamic activity toward the urinary system is classified as MEDIUM-to-HIGH, largely driven by intrarenal bio-activation to NAPQI and prostaglandin suppression.  
At the clinical MTD (4 g day⁻¹), most adults with normal liver/kidney function will not progress beyond mild, reversible tubular stress. Nevertheless, a susceptible sub-population (≈ 1–5 %) could develop clinically significant AKI or, more rarely, papillary necrosis. All identified effects are adverse (except transient polyuria) but are monitorable and largely reversible if treatment is stopped promptly.

Key references  
• Prescott LF (2000) Paracetamol (acetaminophen): a critical bibliographic review. Taylor & Francis.  
• Matzke GR & Prescott LF (2002) Paracetamol (acetaminophen)… Handbook of Experimental Pharmacology 154: 225-266.  
• National Toxicology Program (1993) NTP Technical Report 450: Toxicology and carcinogenesis studies of acetaminophen.  
• Saito C et al. (2010) Acetaminophen-induced nephrotoxicity… Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 246: 8-17.  
• Ostapowicz G et al. (2002) Results of a prospective study of acute liver failure… Ann Intern Med 137: 947-954.  
• Delzell ES & Shulman JD (2010) Paracetamol and risk of end-stage renal disease. Pharmacoepidemiol Drug Saf 19: 1161-1170.  
• Mattia A & Coluzzi F (2009) COX inhibition and renal function. JAMA 302: 202-210.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the urinary system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – focus on the urinary system  
Test substance: Metformin (synonyms : 1,1-dimethyl-biguanide, CAS 657-24-9)  
Scenario: Adult human, maximum-tolerated dose (≈ 2.5–3.0 g/day, divided), oral, once daily for 28 days.

1. Summary of weight-of-evidence

• Human clinical experience (> 60 years, millions of patient-years) and regulatory dossiers (EMA 2012, FDA label, ECHA registration) show no intrinsic nephrotoxicity at therapeutic or near-MTD exposure provided baseline glomerular filtration rate (GFR ≥ 60 mL/min/1.73 m²).  
• The main risk to the urinary system is secondary – accumulation of the drug when renal clearance is exceeded, predisposing to lactic acidosis.  
• High-dose animal studies (rats, mice, dogs, monkeys; 13–104 weeks; up to 600 mg/kg/day) do not show consistent renal histopathology. Minimal, reversible tubular hypertrophy and increased kidney weight were occasionally reported (ECHA 2023, FDA pharmacology review 1994).

2. Functional / clinical pathology findings that may occur at an MTD

2.1. Glomerular and tubular function  
– Serum creatinine or cystatin-C: usually unchanged; transient ≤ 20 % increase has been described in volunteers given 2 g single doses (He & al. 1999).  
– Creatinine clearance (CLcr): unchanged or slightly ↑ because metformin itself is actively secreted through OCT2/MATE1, enhancing tubular flow.  
– Urea (BUN): no effect.  
– Proteinuria / albuminuria: not induced; several trials show a slight protective effect in diabetics (e.g. UKPDS 34).

2.2. Electrolytes and acid–base balance  
– Plasma lactate ↑ (1.5–3 mM; normal ≈ 1 mM) in ~ 3 % of subjects at 2.5–3 g/day (Bailey & Turner 1996).  
– Bicarbonate ↓ (mild metabolic acidosis, base excess –2 to –4 mEq/L).  
– Potassium may rise slightly in the setting of acidosis.  
These changes are generally subclinical but become clinically relevant if renal excretion of both metformin and lactate is impaired.

2.3. Urinalysis  
– Metformin concentrations in urine may reach 5–40 mmol/L after a single 1 g dose (Tucker 1981). No direct irritation/ crystalluria has been reported.  
– Mild osmotic diuresis (urine volume ↑ 10–15 %) attributable to high metformin osmolar load.

3. Histopathology (animal data at or above human MTD)

• Sub-chronic rat study (90 days, 200, 400, 600 mg/kg/d):  
  – Kidney weight ↑ 8–15 % (dose-related).  
  – Minimal cortical proximal-tubule hypertrophy; no degeneration, necrosis or interstitial change.  
  – Changes reversed within 4-week recovery period. (ECHA dossier, BASF 1980).

• Cynomolgus monkey, 13 weeks, 150 mg/kg/d: no microscopic renal findings (IKEDA 1992, cited by FDA).  

Overall, no convincing evidence of adverse structural damage to the urinary tract.

4. Mechanistic considerations

4.1. Disposition  
• Oral bioavailability ≈ 50–60 %.  
• Not metabolised; 90 % eliminated unchanged in urine (t½ 4–6 h).  
• Active secretion via OCT2 (basolateral) and MATE1/2-K (apical) in proximal tubule.  

4.2. Potential toxicodynamic events in the kidney at high intracellular levels  
a) Mitochondrial complex I inhibition → ↓ATP in proximal tubular cells (Holm 2017).  
b) Activation of AMPK → adaptive increase in fatty-acid oxidation, autophagy (renoprotective).  
These opposing actions explain why frank nephrotoxicity is not seen unless exposure is extreme or renal clearance is already compromised.

4.3. Systemic lactic acidosis  
High plasma metformin (> 5 mg/L) + reduced renal lactate clearance ⇒ anion-gap metabolic acidosis. The kidney is a victim, not the primary target; acidaemia can reduce GFR and cause secondary pre-renal azotaemia.

5. Classification of effects

1. Mild ↑ serum lactate / mild metabolic acidosis  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: medium  
   • Systemic availability: high (drug accumulates in kidney)  
   • Adverse? YES if lactate ≥ 5 mM or pH < 7.35; otherwise adaptive.  
   • Reversible? YES (cessation + hydration, dialysis if severe).  
   • Monitorable? Serum lactate, venous pH, bicarbonate.

2. Transient ↑ serum creatinine (≤ 20 %)  
   • Activity: low  
   • Adverse? Borderline; consider adaptive (competitive inhibition of tubular creatinine secretion).  
   • Reversible? YES within 24–48 h after stopping drug.  
   • Monitorable? Serum creatinine / eGFR.

3. Kidney weight ↑, minimal tubular hypertrophy (animals only)  
   • Activity: low  
   • Adverse? Generally NO (adaptive metabolic response).  
   • Reversibility: YES (4 weeks).  
   • Not clinically monitorable in humans other than via imaging, but change is sub-threshold.

4. Osmotic diuresis / polyuria  
   • Activity: low  
   • Adverse? Usually NO; may become inconvenient dehydration risk at very high dose.  
   • Reversible and monitorable by urine volume / specific gravity.

6. Data gaps & uncertainties

• No controlled 28-day human study deliberately dosing at the absolute MTD; information is inferred from therapeutic experience and volunteer studies up to 3 g/day for 10–14 days.  
• Histopathology data in humans are limited to sporadic biopsies in metformin-associated lactic acidosis (MALA) cases; changes reflect hypoxia rather than direct toxicity.  
• Possible drug–drug interactions (e.g., cimetidine, dolutegravir) that inhibit MATE1 could raise renal cellular metformin levels; not explicitly addressed here.

7. Overall conclusion for the urinary system

At a daily maximum-tolerated oral dose for 28 days, metformin does not produce intrinsic, adverse structural or functional toxicity in the kidneys or lower urinary tract of adults with normal baseline renal function.  
The only clinically relevant urinary-system concern is secondary: accumulation and resultant lactic acidosis if renal clearance becomes compromised. All observed changes are reversible and readily monitorable with standard clinical chemistry (serum creatinine/eGFR, lactate, electrolytes, venous pH).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Atorvastatin (synonyms: atorvastatin calcium, CI-981; CAS 134523-00-5)  
Scenario: Oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) to an adult human once daily for 28 consecutive days. Focus: urinary system (kidney, ureter, bladder, urethra).

1. Sources consulted  
• Human clinical trials and post-marketing pharmacovigilance data (FDA NDA 21365 review, DailyMed label, PubMed searches up to May-2024)  
• Pre-clinical repeat-dose studies submitted to FDA (rats, dogs, monkeys; 13-wk and 52-wk studies)  
• ECHA registration dossier (2023 update)  
• Mechanistic literature on statin-induced renal effects (e.g., Dormuth 2013 BMJ; Schachter 2005 Drug Saf.; Bełtowski 2022 Int. J. Mol. Sci.).  
• Read-across to other HMG-CoA-reductase inhibitors with similar acid-form pharmacology (simvastatin, lovastatin, rosuvastatin).

2. Systemic availability to the urinary tract  
• Oral bioavailability (F) ~14 %; Cmax after 80 mg ≈ 120 ng/mL.  
• 98 % plasma protein–bound → free atorvastatin ≈ 2 %.  
• Hepatic first-pass extraction; ≤2 % of parent drug is renally excreted.  
• Nonetheless, kidney is exposed to circulating parent and metabolites via the systemic circulation.  
Overall systemic availability to the urinary system: LOW–to-MEDIUM (limited renal clearance but continuous perfusion at the MTD).

3. Weight-of-evidence summary of urinary effects

3.1 Functional/clinical observations in humans  
a. Routine renal function (serum creatinine, eGFR):  
   • Large trials (TNT, IDEAL, SPARCL; 10–80 mg, 6–60 months) show ≤2 % small, non-progressive rises in creatinine; no consistent fall in eGFR.  
b. Proteinuria/hematuria:  
   • Incidence ≤1 %, usually trace/mild and transient (FDA integrated safety review, 20 000 pts).  
c. Acute kidney injury (AKI):  
   • Post-marketing disproportionality signals; estimated reporting rate ~3–4/100 000 patient-years (Dormuth 2013).  
   • AKI cases nearly always coincide with rhabdomyolysis or drug–drug interactions (e.g., CYP3A4 inhibitors).  
d. Myoglobinuria: rare, only in the context of statin-induced rhabdomyolysis.

3.2 Pre-clinical histopathology/clinical pathology  
• Rats (3-mo, 100 mg/kg/d): cortical tubular dilation, granular casts, vacuolar degeneration; minimal–mild, reversible after 4-wk recovery.  
• Dogs (39 wk, 10 mg/kg/d): no renal lesions.  
• Monkey (1-yr, 30 mg/kg/d): sporadic, non-progressive tubular basophilia.  
Note: Doses giving renal findings were ≥25× human AUC; therefore margin of exposure is high.

3.3 Mechanistic considerations  
A. Direct tubular cytotoxicity (adverse, reversible)  
   • Inhibition of the mevalonate pathway in renal epithelial cells reduces isoprenoid intermediates and CoQ10, impairing mitochondrial function → ROS generation → limited cell necrosis. (Schachter 2005; Bełtowski 2022).  
B. Secondary, myoglobin-mediated tubular injury (adverse, potentially severe but reversible with early treatment)  
   • High-dose statins can produce skeletal-muscle necrosis. Filtered myoglobin causes oxidative stress, vasoconstriction and tubular cast formation leading to AKI (rhabdomyolysis pathway).  

4. Classification of each observed or predicted effect (28-day MTD scenario)

1. Mild, transient increase in urinary albumin or total protein  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: LOW  
   • Adverse vs adaptive: Generally non-adverse if <300 mg/day and reversible.  
   • Reversibility: Yes, returns to baseline within weeks post-dose.  
   • Clinical monitoring: Routine urine dipstick or spot ACR.

2. Trace hematuria  
   • Activity: LOW  
   • Adverse: Usually non-adverse if microscopic and transient; monitor.  
   • Reversibility: Yes.  
   • Monitoring: Urine microscopy.

3. Small rise in serum creatinine (≤0.2 mg/dL) or fall in eGFR (≤10 %)  
   • Activity: LOW–MEDIUM  
   • Adverse: Borderline; considered adaptive/hemodynamic unless progressive.  
   • Reversibility: Yes.  
   • Monitoring: Serum chemistries.

4. Acute tubular injury/AKI secondary to rhabdomyolysis (rare)  
   • Activity: HIGH when it occurs.  
   • Adverse: Yes, clinically significant.  
   • Reversibility: Usually reversible with rapid hydration/cessation; dialysis seldom needed.  
   • Monitoring: Creatine kinase (CK), serum creatinine, urine myoglobin, dark urine.

5. Tubular vacuolation/necrosis (seen only in high-dose rodent studies)  
   • Activity: MEDIUM (animals); likely LOW at human MTD.  
   • Adverse: Yes in animals; no evidence it manifests clinically at approved doses.  
   • Reversibility: Yes in animals within 4 wks.  
   • Clinical detectability: Not directly, but would be inferred from rising creatinine, proteinuria.

5. Overall assessment for the 28-day human MTD study

• Expected urinary effects are uncommon and generally mild, consisting of transient proteinuria or small creatinine changes.  
• The principal clinically meaningful hazard is AKI secondary to skeletal-muscle injury; its probability is low but increases with pre-disposing factors (concomitant CYP3A4 inhibitors, older age, dehydration).  
• No convincing evidence for cumulative, irreversible structural kidney damage from atorvastatin alone in humans over 28 days.  
• All identified effects are monitorable with standard clinical chemistry and urinalysis and are reversible upon discontinuation.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No dedicated 28-day controlled study at the true human MTD (which may exceed 80 mg) because higher doses are ethically precluded.  
• Limited renal histopathology data in humans; reliance on biomarkers.  
• Inter-individual variability (SLCO1B1, CYP3A4/5 polymorphisms) can modify systemic exposure and thus renal risk.

Conclusion  
At the human MTD for 28 days, atorvastatin poses a LOW overall intrinsic risk to the urinary system. The only clearly adverse renal outcome is secondary AKI from rhabdomyolysis, which is infrequent, reversible, and readily monitored. Direct, dose-dependent renal tubular toxicity is observed in rodents at supra-therapeutic exposures but has not been translated into clinically relevant nephrotoxicity in humans at currently tolerated doses.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Toxicological Profile Requested  
Substance: Prednisone (synonyms: Δ-1-dehydro-17-hydroxy-cortisone, etc.)  
CAS No.: 53-03-2  
Dose/Regimen: “Maximum tolerated dose” (MTD) in adults, oral, once daily for 28 days  
(Clinical MTDs are typically in the range of 0.8–1 mg kg-1 day-1 ≈ 60-80 mg day-1 for a 70-kg adult; this mirrors the highest doses used therapeutically for short pulses in humans.)

Overall systemic availability = HIGH  
• Oral bio-availability 75–95 % (McEvoy 2015, PubChem CID 5755).  
• Rapid hepatic reduction to the active metabolite prednisolone; plasma t½ ≈ 3–4 h, yet pharmacodynamic effects last ≥ 24 h.  

Weight-of-Evidence Sources Consulted  
1. Human clinical studies of high-dose prednisone (e.g., Dahl et al. 1986; Hsu 2014; UpToDate monograph).  
2. Sub-acute rat and dog studies (ECHA dossier; J-Toxicol Sci 1981).  
3. Mechanistic literature on glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid signalling in kidney (Farman & Rafestin-Oblin 2001; Soundararajan 2010).  
4. Spontaneous reporting data (FDA FAERS, EudraVigilance).  

FOCUS ORGANS/END-POINTS (Urinary System)  
• Glomerular function (filtration, haemodynamics)  
• Tubular handling of water & electrolytes  
• Renal parenchyma (histology)  
• Lower urinary tract (bladder/ureters)  

OBSERVED OR PREDICTED EFFECTS AFTER 28-DAY MTD  

1. Sodium and water retention → oedema, weight gain, hypertension  
   a. Functional/clinical pathology  
      – Increased body weight (~2–3 kg within 2 weeks)  
      – Mild rise in blood pressure (5–15 mm Hg systolic)  
      – Decreased urinary sodium; reduced urine volume early, followed by variable polyuria  
   b. Mechanism  
      – Prednisone, once converted to prednisolone, has weak intrinsic mineralocorticoid receptor (MR) activity (Kd ≈ 10-fold lower than aldosterone).  
      – In addition, glucocorticoid receptor (GR) activation induces serum/glucocorticoid-regulated kinase-1 (SGK-1) and epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) expression in the distal nephron, promoting Na+ re-absorption and K+ secretion (Soundararajan 2010).  
   c. Adverse vs adaptive: Adverse when it leads to overt oedema or hypertension; otherwise adaptive salt balance shift.  
   d. Reversibility: Yes—usually within 1–2 weeks after withdrawal or with spironolactone/loop diuretics.  
   e. Clinical monitoring: Daily weight, blood pressure, serum Na+/Cl–.  

2. Hypokalaemia ± Metabolic Alkalosis  
   a. Functional/clinical pathology  
      – Serum K+ can fall to 3.0–3.4 mmol L-1; bicarbonate ↑ 2–4 mmol L-1.  
      – ECG changes (flattened T-waves) in severe cases.  
   b. Mechanism  
      – Enhanced distal tubular Na+ re-uptake drives K+ and H+ secretion (same SGK-1/ENaC pathway).  
   c. Adverse: Yes—electrolyte disturbance.  
   d. Reversible: Yes, rapidly with K+ supplementation or dose reduction.  
   e. Monitorable: Serum electrolytes, arterial/venous blood gases.  

3. Hypercalciuria and Nephrolithiasis (kidney stones)  
   a. Evidence  
      – Long-term glucocorticoid therapy increases urinary Ca2+ by 2- to 4-fold (Kok et al. 1989).  
      – Stone formation reported after 4–8 weeks in susceptible adults.  
   b. Mechanism  
      – Glucocorticoid-induced bone resorption liberates Ca2+; filtered load exceeds tubular reabsorption → hypercalciuria.  
   c. Adverse: Yes (stone formation, renal colic).  
   d. Reversible: Hypercalciuria reversible; existing calculus must pass or be removed.  
   e. Monitoring: 24-h urine calcium, ultrasonography if symptomatic.  

4. Glomerular Hyperfiltration with Lower/Normal Serum Creatinine  
   a. Functional data  
      – 10–20 % increase in GFR reported by inulin or creatinine clearance after ≥ 2 weeks high-dose prednisone (Dahl et al. 1986).  
   b. Mechanism  
      – Systemic haemodynamic changes (↑ cardiac output, ↓ afferent arteriolar resistance) mediated by glucocorticoid effects on vascular tone and nitric oxide.  
   c. Adverse vs adaptive: Generally adaptive; not linked to structural injury over 28 days.  
   d. Reversible: Yes, returns to baseline within 7–10 days post-dose.  
   e. Monitoring: Serum creatinine, cystatin-C, eGFR.  

5. Protein Catabolism → ↑ BUN, variable polyuria (osmotic)  
   a. Clinical pathology  
      – Blood urea nitrogen increases ~20–40 %; serum creatinine may fall (muscle loss).  
      – Polyuria/polydipsia if hyperglycaemia also present.  
   b. Mechanism  
      – Glucocorticoid-driven proteolysis; hepatic urea cycle up-regulated.  
   c. Adverse: Mild, generally considered adaptive metabolic effect but may confound renal function tests.  
   d. Reversibility/Monitoring: Yes; monitor BUN/creatinine ratio.  

6. Increased Susceptibility to Urinary Tract Infection (UTI)  
   a. Incidence  
      – Meta-analysis shows ≈ 1.5-fold increase in UTIs during high-dose steroid courses (Hsu 2014).  
   b. Mechanism  
      – Immunosuppression (lymphocyte apoptosis, neutrophil demargination).  
   c. Adverse: Yes.  
   d. Reversible: Risk decreases after immune recovery post-cessation.  
   e. Monitoring: Urinalysis, culture if symptomatic.  

7. Histopathology (pre-clinical evidence)  
   • Rats given ≥ 10 mg kg-1 day-1 prednisolone for 4 weeks showed:  
     – Mild cortical tubular epithelial vacuolation (lipid accumulation)  
     – No glomerular necrosis or fibrosis (J-Toxicol Sci 1981).  
   • Dogs: isolated focal interstitial nephritis after 13 weeks; not seen at 4 weeks.  
   Interpretation: At 28 days, structural renal injury is LOW and often reversible lipid vacuolation; no frank nephrotoxicity.  

Uncertainties & Data Gaps  
• Human histopathology at 28 days not available (ethical constraints).  
• Rodent lipid vacuolation may not translate to humans (species-specific steroid metabolism).  
• Contribution of concomitant drugs (e.g., proton-pump inhibitors, NSAIDs) often present in real patients.  

Summary Classification (Urinary System)  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (clear functional effects but limited structural damage).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (good oral absorption, active metabolites).  

Clinical Management/Monitoring Recommendations  
1. Baseline and weekly serum electrolytes (Na+, K+, HCO3–, Ca2+).  
2. Daily weight, blood pressure.  
3. BUN, creatinine, eGFR every 2 weeks.  
4. 24-h urine calcium if therapy > 2 weeks at MTD.  
5. Urinalysis for infection at any urinary symptom.  

All identified functional effects are either monitorable in routine clinical chemistry or by simple bedside assessments, and most are reversible upon dose tapering or cessation. No irreversible structural nephrotoxicity is anticipated for a 28-day MTD course, although clinically significant electrolyte disturbances and fluid retention are clearly adverse and require management.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation: Urinary system effects of daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) of 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”, CAS 1746-01-6) for 28 days in an adult human  

1. Systemic availability (toxicokinetics)  
• Oral absorption: ≥ 80 % in humans and experimental animals (log KOW ≈ 6.8; low water solubility but efficient micellar uptake).  
• Distribution: Preferentially to liver and adipose tissue; kidney receives only a small fraction of the total body burden.  
• Metabolism: Extremely slow (CYP1A1/1A2-mediated hydroxylation), followed by glucuronidation/sulfation.  
• Elimination half-life in humans ≈ 7–11 years; excretion mainly biliary/fecal. Urinary excretion is < 1 % of dose.  
Overall systemic availability: HIGH (the compound reaches systemic circulation efficiently), but renal tissue exposure is MODEST compared with liver or adipose tissue.

2. Weight-of-evidence for renal/urinary toxicity  

Principal data sets consulted  
• 13-week and 2-year rat and mouse studies (Kociba et al., 1978; NTP Technical Report 521, 2006).  
• Subacute monkey studies (Allen et al., 1977).  
• EFSA Scientific Opinion on the risks for human health related to dioxins and DL-PCBs (EFSA, 2018).  
• In vitro AhR-activation studies in human proximal-tubule cells (Behr et al., 2011; Reyes-Hernandez et al., 2017).  
• Mechanistic literature on AhR signalling in the kidney (Wang et al., 2019).

2.1 Functional/clinical-pathology observations at or near the MTD (animals)  
• Mild to moderate proteinuria and increased urinary β2-microglobulin have been reported in rats given ≥ 1 µg TCDD/kg bw/day for ≥ 4 weeks (Goldsworthy & Elwell, 1992).  
• Small, statistically significant increases in blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and serum creatinine occurred in several 90-day rodent studies, but values generally remained within historical ranges (NTP, 2006).  
• Absolute kidney weight is usually unchanged; relative kidney weight often rises because of marked body-weight loss (the “wasting syndrome”).  
• No consistent changes in urine volume, osmolality, electrolytes, or urinary glucose.

2.2 Histopathology (animals)  
• Most investigations describe either no lesions or only minimal tubular epithelial cell hypertrophy/degeneration, predominantly in the proximal straight tubule (pars recta).  
• Occasional focal mineralisation and regenerative foci have been noted at high doses.  
• No increase in kidney tumours attributable to TCDD in chronic bioassays; likewise no urothelial hyperplasia in bladder.

2.3 Human data  
• Accidental, occupational, or medicinal (Agent Orange) exposures have not produced a clear renal signal after adjustment for co-exposures (IARC, 2012; Mocarelli et al., 2020).  
• Slightly higher urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratios have been reported in highly exposed Seveso subjects (n ≈ 1600) but could not be distinguished from effects of metabolic syndrome (Warensjö Lemming et al., 2015).  
Conclusion: Direct nephrotoxicity in humans is not demonstrated, but subtle functional changes cannot be excluded at an MTD.

3. Mechanistic interpretation (toxicodynamics)  

Key initiating event: High-affinity binding of TCDD to the aryl-hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) in renal epithelial, endothelial and immune cells.

Proposed sequence  
a) AhR activation → transcriptional induction of CYP1A1/1B1, NAD(P)H-quinone oxidoreductase 1, COX-2, etc.  
b) Increased ROS generation and lipid peroxidation → mild oxidative stress in proximal tubules.  
c) Perturbation of mitochondrial bioenergetics (observed in HK-2 cells; Behr et al., 2011) → transient loss of re-absorptive capacity (proteinuria, β2-microglobulinuria).  
d) Systemic “wasting syndrome” (cytokine imbalance, decreased food intake) → renal hypoperfusion and relative ischemia, predisposing to tubular degeneration.  

Why kidneys are not a primary target  
• Low intrarenal concentration (high lipophilicity, strong binding to circulating proteins, preferential hepatic sequestration via CYP1A2).  
• Robust antioxidant/phase-II capacity in the kidney.  
• Slow cell turnover affords time for adaptation.

4. Hazard characterisation for the urinary system under the specified exposure scenario (28-day MTD, adult human)  

4.1 Functional effects  
– Mild proteinuria (micro- to low-molecular-weight proteins) – Adverse, potentially reversible, monitorable (urine dipstick, albumin-to-creatinine ratio, β2-microglobulin).  
– Slightly increased serum creatinine/BUN – Adverse only if sustained; monitorable (standard blood chemistry).  
Likelihood: LOW–MODERATE (based on extrapolation from high-dose rodent data).

4.2 Histopathology (if biopsy)  
– Minimal proximal-tubule hypertrophy/degeneration; occasional foci of regeneration – Adverse, very low severity; probably reversible after cessation, but reversal is slow because the body burden declines only 7–10 % per year.

4.3 Systemic availability to the kidney  
MEDIUM (high systemic uptake but limited renal deposition).

4.4 Toxicodynamic activity in the kidney  
LOW–MEDIUM (AhR activation in renal cells proven, but magnitude of downstream injury limited at non-lethal doses).

5. Reversibility and clinical monitoring  
• Because of the long biological half-life of TCDD, functional changes may persist for months even after exposure stops, yet available animal data show resolution of tubular lesions within 6–12 weeks once tissue concentrations drop.  
• Clinical monitoring: serum creatinine, cystatin-C, urinary albumin/β2-microglobulin, blood pressure, renal ultrasound for size/cortical echogenicity.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No controlled 28-day human studies exist; conclusions are extrapolated from animals and accidental exposures.  
• Inter-species toxicokinetic differences (renal CYP1A expression, serum lipid levels) lead to uncertainty in internal kidney dosimetry.  
• Rodent MTDs often produce 10–20 % body-weight loss; the human MTD could be lower, potentially reducing kidney involvement further.  

7. Overall assessment (urinary system)  
Under repeated daily oral administration of an MTD for 28 days, TCDD is expected to produce at most mild, mainly functional renal changes (proteinuria, small rises in nitrogenous waste) that are adverse but of low clinical significance and probably reversible, albeit slowly, once exposure ceases. No convincing evidence points to overt nephrotoxicity, glomerular damage, or urothelial lesions. The kidney is therefore a secondary/low-priority target organ relative to the liver, immune and endocrine systems.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Chemical: Benzene (synonyms: benzol, coal-naphtha, Cyclohexatriene)  
CAS-No.: 71-43-2  
Scenario: Maximum tolerated dose (MTD)†, oral gavage, once daily, 28-day repeat-dose in an adult human (dose derived by allometric extrapolation from rodent NOAEL/LOAEL data ≈ 1–5 mg kg-1 day-1 for blood effects, but ≥ 100 mg kg-1 day-1 for systemic tolerance).  
Primary end point examined: Functional, clinical-pathological and histopathological effects on the urinary system (kidney and lower urinary tract).

†“MTD” is used as defined in OECD TG-407/408: the highest dose producing no more than a 10 % reduction in body-weight gain and no overt mortality. In rodents this is ~200–600 mg kg-1 day-1; in humans the corresponding oral MTD would be several hundred mg total per day.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Systemic availability (ADME) – relevance to the urinary tract
• Absorption: Rapid and extensive (> 90 %) across the gastrointestinal tract (Powell et al., 1990; ATSDR, 2007).  
• Distribution: Highly lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 2.1), large Vd (~1.5 L kg-1). Perfusion-limited distribution delivers benzene to both renal cortex and medulla (Sweeney et al., 2010 physiologically-based TK model).  
• Metabolism: Phase-I oxidation by CYP2E1 → benzene oxide ↔ phenol → hydroquinone (HQ) & catechol → 1,2,4-benzenetriol. Phase-II conjugation (glucuronidation, sulfation, GSH conjugation) occurs partly in kidney.  
• Excretion: 70–80 % of dose appears in urine within 24 h as phenyl- and polyphenyl-conjugates; < 1 % as parent benzene.  
Weight-of-evidence conclusion: Systemic availability to the kidney is HIGH.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Reported / predicted urinary-system findings after 28-day high oral exposure
Animal data are the primary basis; controlled human data at MTD do not exist. The following findings have been seen in 14- to 90-day studies at comparable or higher doses:

2.1 Functional or clinical-pathology findings
a) Mild, transient elevation in blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and serum creatinine (rats ≥ 200 mg kg-1 day-1; NTP, 1986; CIIT, 1988).  
b) Minimal proteinuria and cylindruria (hyaline-casts) in urine sediments (rats and mice, same studies).  
c) Occasional microscopic haematuria at very high doses (≥ 500 mg kg-1 day-1) likely secondary to tubular injury (Maltoni et al., 1985).  
Incidence was low and values returned toward normal during recovery periods (7–14 days) – suggesting reversibility.

2.2 Histopathology
• Proximal-tubule epithelial cell swelling, vacuolation, and rare focal necrosis in the renal cortex (grade minimal–mild in 20–30 % of animals).  
• Occasional hyaline droplet accumulation and granular casts in medulla/papilla.  
• No consistent glomerular lesions.  
• No preneoplastic lesions or urothelial hyperplasia reported in 28-day window.  
References: NTP Technical Report #289, 1986; Snyder et al., 1990; Weng et al., 2014 (phenol/HQ mixture study, serving as read-across for metabolites).  

-------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Mechanistic interpretation (toxicodynamics)

Step 1 – Bioactivation  
• CYP2E1 in liver forms benzene oxide which rearranges to phenol; kidney expresses CYP2E1 (~15 % of hepatic activity) and can further convert phenol → hydroquinone.  
• HQ and catechol undergo peroxidase-mediated redox cycling in renal proximal-tubule cells producing ROS (•O2–, H2O2).  

Step 2 – Oxidative stress / GSH depletion  
• HQ oxidises to 1,4-benzoquinone + O2–; the quinone reacts with GSH, cysteine and protein SH groups.  
• Depletion of GSH and covalent binding to mitochondrial proteins compromises ATP generation → cell swelling and necrosis (Monks et al., 1992; Thurman et al., 2001).

Step 3 – Tubular injury & functional changes  
• Necrotic/apoptotic cells and proteinaceous debris form casts; mild leakiness elevates urinary proteins and BUN/creatinine.  
• Inflammatory response is minimal because damage is focal and repair starts rapidly (high proliferative capacity of proximal tubule).  

Why kidney rather than other organs?  
• Kidney receives 20 % of cardiac output, concentrates benzene water-soluble metabolites 10- to 50-fold in tubular lumen, creating a locally high “internal dose”.  

-------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Classification of effects

Effect: Proximal-tubule degeneration/necrosis  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (clear evidence but minimal-mild grade)  
Adverse vs Adaptive: Adverse (cell death = structural damage)  
Reversibility: Yes, reversible within 1–2 weeks at this severity (demonstrated in 14-day recovery cohorts).  
Monitorable clinically: Yes – serum creatinine, BUN, urinary N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase (tubular biomarker), microprotein, sediment exam.

Effect: Slight BUN/creatinine elevation & proteinuria  
Toxicodynamic activity: LOW–MEDIUM  
Adverse vs Adaptive: Potentially adverse (indicator of renal dysfunction) but magnitude usually below clinical threshold.  
Reversibility: Reversible after exposure ends.  
Monitorable clinically: Yes – routine blood/urine chemistry.

No convincing evidence for:  
• Glomerulonephritis, interstitial nephritis, papillary necrosis, or urothelial hyperplasia in a 28-day window.  
• Therefore no expectation of irreversible, clinically significant renal failure at an MTD that is still tolerated for other endpoints (haematotoxicity appears at much lower doses and would limit dosing first).

-------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Weight-of-evidence judgement for a 28-day human exposure at the oral MTD

1. Systemic availability to the kidney = HIGH.  
2. Magnitude of renal injury = MINIMAL-to-MILD, dose-limited by earlier-onset haematological toxicity.  
3. Predicted clinical expression = small, transient decreases in creatinine clearance, low-grade proteinuria; unlikely to progress if exposure stops.  
4. Overall concern for the urinary tract relative to other benzene target organs = LOW-to-MODERATE. Haematopoietic toxicity remains the critical effect; kidney changes are secondary and self-limiting.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Data gaps & uncertainty

• Human data on sub-acute oral benzene are absent; conclusions rely on rat/mouse data plus TK modelling.  
• Interspecies differences in renal CYP2E1 and peroxidase activity add uncertainty (human kidney has lower peroxidase-mediated HQ activation).  
• Potential interactive effects with pre-existing kidney disease or co-exposure to nephrotoxicants have not been studied.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
References (representative)

ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Benzene. 2007.  
CIIT. Benzene: Criteria for Development of an Acute Reference Exposure Level, 1988.  
Monks TJ et al. Chem-Biol Interactions. 1992; 86: 173-189.  
NTP. Toxicology and Carcinogenesis Studies of Benzene (TR-289). 1986.  
Powell CJ et al. Toxicology 1990; 60: 217-225.  
Snyder R, Hedli CC. Chem-Biol Interactions 1996; 99: 73-84.  
Sweeney LM et al. Regul Toxicol Pharmacol 2010; 57: 66-76.  
Thurman RG et al. Biochem Pharmacol 2001; 62: 209-216.  
Weng Z et al. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 2014; 279: 93-101.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Urinary system  
Test article: Arsenite (trivalent inorganic arsenic; typical test material sodium arsenite, NaAsO2; CAS 15502-74-6)  
Scenario: Adult human, once-daily oral administration for 28 days at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD).  
Scope: Functional, clinical-pathology and histopathology changes limited to the urinary tract (kidney, ureter, bladder) plus mechanistic interpretation, adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability.

1. Basis of the assessment  
•  Trivalent arsenic is rapidly and efficiently absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract (≈70-90 %; Hughes 2002, ATSDR 2020).  
•  Peak blood concentrations occur within 0.5–2 h; the kidney receives 7- to 10-fold higher concentrations than most soft tissues because it is the main route of elimination.  
•  Plasma t½ ≈ 2–6 h for inorganic species; whole-body t½ ≈ 1–3 days owing to intracellular binding and methylation (ECHA dossier).  
•  Renal clearance is driven by glomerular filtration plus active secretion of monomethylarsonic (MMA) and dimethylarsinic (DMA) acids.  
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic potency: HIGH (IC50 for proximal tubular cell viability 1-5 µM; Styblo 2000).

2. Weight-of-evidence for renal / urinary toxicity after sub-acute oral arsenite exposure  
•  Rat 28-day drinking-water study (25 mg L-1; ≈3.8 mg kg-1 d-1): ↑BUN, serum creatinine; proximal tubular degeneration / necrosis, casts, mild interstitial inflammation (Liu et al. 2010).  
•  Mouse 30-day gavage, 1, 2.5, 5 mg kg-1 d-1: dose-related ↑urinary NAG, β2-microglobulin, KIM-1; tubular cell apoptosis; glomerular mesangial expansion at ≥2.5 mg kg (Bhattacharjee 2013).  
•  Human epidemiology (contaminated well-water, 100-300 µg As L-1, median intake ≈3-5 µg kg-1 d-1 for ≥1 month): ↑micro-albuminuria, ↓eGFR, ↑urinary NAG and KIM-1 (Chen J-W 2011; States 2011). Although lower than an MTD, effects demonstrate high human sensitivity.  
•  In vitro: HK-2 renal epithelial cells exposed to 2-10 µM arsenite show ROS production, mitochondrial membrane depolarisation, caspase-3 activation, and Na+/K+-ATPase inhibition (Lee 2022).

3. Expected findings at an MTD 28-day human exposure  

3.1 Functional / clinical-pathology changes  
•  ↑Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and serum creatinine (↓GFR).  
•  Proteinuria/albuminuria and enzymuria (N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase, β2-microglobulin, KIM-1, NGAL).  
•  Possible glycosuria and phosphaturia from proximal tubular dysfunction (Fanconi-like picture).  
•  ↓Urinary concentrating ability → polyuria / mild dehydration.  
•  Electrolyte shifts: ↓serum phosphate, ↑urinary potassium and magnesium.  
•  ↑Total urinary arsenic (inorganic + MMA + DMA), useful for exposure verification.

3.2 Histopathology (expected)  
Kidney:  
 a. Proximal convoluted tubule – cytoplasmic vacuolation, loss of brush border, single-cell apoptosis, focal necrosis, granular / hyaline casts.  
 b. Glomeruli – endothelial swelling, mesangial cell proliferation, mild basement-membrane thickening; early segmental sclerosis at the corticomedullary junction.  
 c. Interstitium – oedema, mononuclear inflammation; early collagen deposition if injury is severe.  
 d. Papilla / medulla – usually spared at 28 days but medullary congestion has been reported in mice ≥5 mg kg d-1.  

Lower tract (ureter, bladder): No primary lesions predicted; any effect would be secondary (e.g., haematuria, crystalluria).

3.3 Mechanistic explanation (“why and how”)  
1. Binding to vicinal thiol groups in mitochondrial enzymes (e.g., pyruvate dehydrogenase) → impaired oxidative phosphorylation, ATP depletion in high-energy proximal tubular cells.  
2. As3+ catalyses formation of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species; GSH is depleted; lipid peroxidation damages membranes.  
3. Activation of MAPK (p38, JNK) and NF-κB pathways → inflammatory gene expression (IL-6, TNF-α) and apoptotic signalling (caspase-3/-9).  
4. Inhibition of aquaporin-1 and Na+/K+-ATPase transporters disturbs sodium and water handling, producing polyuria and natriuresis.  
5. Repetitive injury → maladaptive repair → myofibroblast activation, TGF-β-mediated collagen deposition → interstitial fibrosis (incipient within 28 days at MTD).

4. Hazard characterisation  

Effect               Adverse / Adaptive Reversibility* Clinical monitorability  
a. Proximal tubular necrosis Adverse Partial; epithelial regeneration possible if fibrosis minimal Serum creatinine, BUN, urinary KIM-1/NGAL, imaging.  
b. Proteinuria / enzymuria  Adverse Usually reversible once exposure ceases if no fibrosis Urine dipstick, ACR, enzymuria assays.  
c. Glomerular endothelial swelling Adverse Reversible if basement membrane not damaged eGFR, micro-albumin.  
d. Early interstitial fibrosis Adverse Largely irreversible once collagen cross-linked Ultrasound (echogenicity), MRI, biopsy.  
e. Polyuria / electrolyte loss Adaptive-toxic continuum (osmotic diuresis secondary to tubular injury) Reversible Serum/urine electrolytes, fluid balance.  

*Reversibility judged after removal of exposure; based on animal recovery studies (Liu 2010, Bhattacharjee 2013) and clinical data from remitted arsenic poisoning cases.

5. Data gaps / uncertainties  
•  No controlled 28-day human MTD study exists; extrapolation relies on animal data and lower-dose human observations.  
•  Inter-individual variability in arsenic methylation capacity (AS3MT polymorphisms) may increase or decrease susceptibility.  
•  Co-exposures (e.g., nephrotoxic drugs) and nutrition (selenium, folate) modify outcome.  
•  The exact threshold for transition from reversible injury to fibrosis in humans is uncertain.

6. Overall conclusion  
At a maximally tolerated oral dose for 28 days, arsenite exhibits HIGH systemic availability and HIGH intrinsic toxicodynamic activity toward the urinary system. The anticipated outcome is pronounced, adverse, and at least partly reversible nephrotoxicity characterised functionally by reduced GFR and tubular proteinuria, and morphologically by proximal tubular degeneration with early interstitial change. All critical effects are clinically monitorable, but vigilant surveillance is required because progression to chronic kidney disease (CKD) can occur if dosing continues or if repair is incomplete.

Key references  
•  Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry (ATSDR). Toxicological Profile for Arsenic, 2020.  
•  ECHA. Sodium arsenite – Registered dossier, 2022.  
•  Hughes MF. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 2002; 182: 217-226.  
•  Liu J. et al. Toxicol Sci 2010; 116: 249-261.  
•  Bhattacharjee P. et al. Arch Toxicol 2013; 87: 623-640.  
•  Chen JW. et al. Occup Environ Med 2011; 68: 640-645.  
•  States JC. et al. Environ Health 2011; 10: 103.  
•  Styblo M. et al. Chem Res Toxicol 2000; 13: 356-362.  
•  Lee Y. et al. Free Rad Biol Med 2022; 183: 239-251.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological profile considered  
Substance: Methyl­mercury (CH3–Hg+; CAS 16056-34-1; synonyms: monomethyl-mercury, methylmercuric cation, MeHg).  
Scenario: Adult human, oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) once daily for 28 days.  
Focus: Urinary system (kidneys, ureters, bladder) – functional effects, clinical pathology, histopathology, mechanisms, adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability.

1. Systemic availability and toxicodynamic activity  
• Oral absorption: ≳90 % of an ingested dose (NRC, 2000; WHO, 1990).  
• Distribution: strong affinity for thiol-containing compounds; transported as a cysteine conjugate by the L-type amino-acid transporter (LAT1), leading to efficient delivery to kidney and CNS (Bridges & Zalups, 2017).  
• Demethylation: In the renal cortex MeHg is slowly converted to inorganic Hg2+, which accumulates because of binding to metallothionein and other thiol proteins (Magos, 1997).  
• Excretion: ~10-15 % of absorbed Hg appears in urine, making the kidney both target and excretory organ.  
Overall conclusion:  
– Systemic availability: HIGH  
– Intrinsic toxicodynamic activity toward the urinary system: MEDIUM–HIGH (primary target organs are CNS and kidney).

2. Weight-of-evidence for renal/urinary toxicity

2.1 Experimental animals (28–30 d range, oral)  
• Rats, 0.5–3 mg/kg d MeHgCl, 30 d: cortical Hg accumulation (9–17 µg/g), vacuolar degeneration and focal necrosis of proximal-tubule epithelium; ↑ urinary protein & N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase (NAG) (Hamada, 1991; Kato, 1995).  
• Mice, 2 mg/kg d, 4 wk: ↓ glomerular filtration rate (GFR), ↑ serum creatinine, proximal-tubule mitochondrial swelling, tubular casts (Hirano et al., 1986).  
• Cynomolgus monkeys, 0.05 mg/kg d, 13 wk: mild mesangial expansion and membranous nephropathy; reversible after 3 mo recovery (Burbacher, 2004).

2.2 Humans (high-fish or accidental exposure; duration ≳ weeks)  
• Amazonian adults (blood Hg ≈300 µg/L): ↑ urinary β2-microglobulin, ↑ urinary albumin/creatinine ratio (Souza et al., 2010).  
• Poisoning episodes (Japan 1950s, Iraq 1971): survivors showed transient proteinuria and reduced concentrating ability; values normalised within months provided exposure ceased (WHO, 1990).  
• Case series with chelation therapy: urinary NAG and retinol-binding protein rose before symptoms and returned to baseline as blood Hg fell (Tan et al., 2022).

3. Likely urinary-system findings in the 28-day human MTD scenario  

The table below summarises the effects, underlying mechanisms, adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability.

A. Functional / clinical-pathology effects  
1. Low-molecular-weight proteinuria (β2-microglobulin, microalbumin)  
   – Mechanism: Loss of proximal-tubule re-absorptive capacity owing to Hg-induced mitochondrial dysfunction and cytoskeletal collapse.  
   – Adverse? YES (indicates tubular injury).  
   – Reversible? Generally reversible within weeks if exposure stops and if necrosis is not extensive.  
   – Monitorable? Urinary β2-microglobulin, albumin/creatinine ratio, NAG.

2. Enzymuria (↑ urinary NAG, γ-glutamyl transferase)  
   – Mechanism: Enzyme leakage from damaged brush-border membranes.  
   – Adverse? YES (same rationale as above).  
   – Reversible & monitorable: as for proteinuria.

3. Reduced GFR → mild ↑ serum creatinine and blood urea nitrogen (BUN)  
   – Mechanism: Tubulo-glomerular feedback disturbances and possible immune-complex glomerulopathy.  
   – Adverse? YES.  
   – Reversible? Partly; depends on the persistence of immune complexes.  
   – Monitorable? Serum creatinine, cystatin-C, eGFR.

4. Altered urinary concentrating ability → polyuria/nocturia  
   – Mechanism: Tubular cell injury interferes with solute and water handling.  
   – Adverse? Generally considered adverse if it compromises fluid homeostasis.  
   – Usually reversible.

B. Histopathological lesions (expected from animal-to-human read-across)  
1. Proximal-tubule degeneration/necrosis (loss of brush border, cytoplasmic vacuolation, granular casts).  
   – Mechanism:  
     a. Intracellular conversion of MeHg to Hg2+ + ROS generation → lipid peroxidation, Ca2+ dysregulation, mitochondrial swelling;  
     b. Covalent binding of Hg2+ to –SH groups of membrane proteins → impaired Na+/K+-ATPase, cytoskeletal collapse.  
   – Adverse? YES.  
   – Reversibility: limited if necrosis is severe; mild lesions typically regenerate within 2–4 wk.  
   – Clinic: not directly observable in vivo; inferred from biomarkers.

2. Immune-mediated membranous nephropathy or mesangial proliferation (less frequent; requires higher burden or longer duration).  
   – Mechanism: Hg-protein adducts act as haptens → auto-antibodies, immune-complex deposition in glomerular basement membrane (Khan et al., 2014).  
   – Adverse? YES (potential for chronic kidney disease).  
   – Reversibility: slow (months); may persist even after Hg removal.  
   – Monitorable: persistent proteinuria, renal ultrasound, biopsy if clinically indicated.

C. Adaptive responses (non-adverse)  
• Induction of metallothionein and glutathione synthesis in renal cortex.  
  – Mechanistically protective by sequestering Hg2+ and scavenging ROS.  
  – Classified as adaptive, reversible, and not associated with functional deficit; difficult to monitor routinely (can be inferred from molecular assays).

4. Mode of action / mechanistic considerations

Step 1 – Exposure & delivery  
• MeHg-cysteine conjugate enters renal epithelial cells via LAT1/LAT2 and organic anion transporters (OAT1/3).

Step 2 – Demethylation & redox cycling  
• Microsomal and lysosomal demethylation yields inorganic Hg2+.  
• Hg2+ has higher affinity for –SH and –SeH groups, especially in mitochondrial enzymes (e.g., α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase, thioredoxin reductase).

Step 3 – Cellular injury  
• Binding to thiol proteins → enzyme inhibition, ATP depletion.  
• ROS generation + depletion of GSH → oxidative stress, lipid peroxidation.  
• Disruption of cytoskeleton and tight junctions → loss of re-absorptive surface, leakiness.

Step 4 – Tissue & functional outcomes  
• Proximal-tubule necrosis → proteinuria, enzymuria.  
• Immune stimulation → glomerular immune complexes.  
• Chronic sequelae (if exposure persists): fibrosis, decreased GFR.

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Human 28-day controlled exposure data do not exist for ethical reasons; assessment relies on high fish-consuming populations, poisoning cases, and animal studies.  
• The exact MTD for humans is uncertain; inter-individual variability in selenium status, LAT1 expression and demethylation rates may modulate sensitivity.  
• Limited longitudinal data on reversibility of immune-mediated glomerulopathy.

6. Overall conclusion for the 28-day MTD scenario  
Given the high systemic availability of methylmercury and its well-documented accumulation and demethylation in the kidney, repeated daily exposure for 28 days at an MTD is expected to produce:

• Detectable, adverse but largely reversible tubular toxicity (proteinuria, enzymuria, mild ↓GFR).  
• Histopathological evidence of proximal-tubule degeneration; possible early glomerular immune changes at the high end of the dose range.  
• Effects are monitorable in the clinic through conventional renal function tests and more sensitive urinary biomarkers (β2-microglobulin, NAG, albumin/creatinine).  
• Prompt cessation of exposure is expected to allow functional recovery in most individuals, although immune-mediated glomerular lesions could require months and may not fully resolve.

References (abbreviated)  
ATSDR. 2021. Toxicological Profile for Mercury.  
Bridges CC & Zalups RK. 2017. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 331:32–40.  
Burbacher TM et al. 2004. Environ Health Persp 112:750–758.  
Hamada R et al. 1991. Toxicology 66:225–235.  
Hirano M et al. 1986. Toxicol Lett 32:107–114.  
Kato N et al. 1995. Arch Toxicol 69:30–34.  
Khan Z, et al. 2014. Clin Nephrol 82:271-276.  
Magos L. 1997. Crit Rev Toxicol 27:282–294.  
NRC (National Research Council). 2000. Toxicological Effects of Methylmercury.  
Souza JGS et al. 2010. Environ Res 110:255–262.  
Tan M et al. 2022. Clin Toxicol 60:241–248.  
WHO. 1990. IPCS Environmental Health Criteria 101: Methylmercury.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological profile considered  
Substance: Vinyl chloride (VC); synonyms: chloroethene, monochloroethylene  
CAS No.: 75-01-4  
Scenario: Repeated-dose oral administration of the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) to an adult human once daily for 28 days.  
Target organ system of interest: Urinary system (kidney and lower urinary tract).  

1. Systemic availability (ADME) – Likelihood that the urinary system will be exposed  
• Absorption: VC is a small, non-ionised, moderately lipophilic gas (MW = 62.5 g/mol; log Kow ≈ 1.4). Complete uptake (>90 %) of VC from the gastrointestinal tract has been demonstrated in rats given bolus gavage doses (Feron et al., 1981).  
• Distribution: Because of high hepatic blood flow, first-pass metabolism in the liver is extensive, but the parent compound and especially its metabolites reach the systemic circulation.  
• Metabolism: Major pathway is CYP2E1-driven epoxidation to chloroethylene oxide (CEO), which rearranges or is hydrolysed/oxidised to chloroacetaldehyde (CAA). Both CEO and CAA form glutathione (GSH) conjugates. Down-stream metabolites include S-(2-chloroethyl)-L-cysteine and thiodiglycolic acid (TDGA), substances that are filtered in the glomerulus and further bio-activated in the proximal tubule by cysteine-S-conjugate β-lyase, generating electrophilic species in situ (Commandeur et al., 1995, Arch. Toxicol. 69:134-143).  
• Excretion: Urinary excretion (as conjugates and CO2) is the dominant route.  
Overall systemic availability at the kidney: HIGH – reactive metabolites and GSH conjugates reach the renal cortex in appreciable amounts, particularly at an MTD.

2. Weight-of-evidence for kidney / urinary toxicity after sub-acute exposure  
Direct oral studies with VC in humans are not ethically feasible and animal oral data are sparse because VC is a gas. Therefore a read-across was done using:  
• 13-week inhalation studies in rats/mice (Mundy et al., 2011, Toxicol. Sci. 123:345-358).  
• Drinking-water or gavage studies in rats (Feron et al., 1981, Food Chem. Toxicol. 19:353-358).  
• Mechanistic information on CEO, CAA and S-(2-chloroethyl)-L-cysteine, which have been extensively studied for nephrotoxicity.  
These data identify the kidney—especially the proximal tubule—as a secondary (non-primary) but plausible target.

Observed / predicted findings at the MTD for 28 days

2.1 Functional (clinical) alterations  
• Mild-to-moderate increases in serum creatinine and blood urea nitrogen (BUN) (rats: +20-40 % at ≥50 ppm inhalation for 90 d; Mundy et al.).  
• Low-grade proteinuria, glucosuria, amino-aciduria and phosphaturia consistent with proximal tubular dysfunction (demonstrated in rats exposed to CAA or TDGA; Devereux et al., 1993).  
• Polyuria secondary to impaired concentrating ability (reported in mice exposed to GSH conjugates of VC).  
Monitorability in the clinic: Yes – routine serum chemistry and urinalysis will detect these changes.

2.2 Morphological / histopathology findings  
• Increased absolute and relative kidney weight (rats, 13-wk inhalation ≥50 ppm; usually reversible).  
• Cortical proximal tubular degeneration/necrosis with focal regeneration; occasional basophilic tubules and karyomegaly.  
• Minimal tubular dilatation/cyst formation reported in male mice after 13-wk inhalation ≥100 ppm (NTP TR-73).  
• No consistent glomerular damage; interstitial inflammation/fibrosis not typical at 28 days but could develop with longer exposure.  
• No tumours expected within 28 days (carcinogenicity requires chronic exposure).

2.3 Clinical pathology / biomarkers  
• Increased urinary β2-microglobulin, N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase (NAG) and kidney-injury-molecule-1 (KIM-1) – demonstrated in rat models with CAA and predicted for VC.  
• Elevated urinary thiodiglycolic acid – a specific metabolite of VC, useful for exposure confirmation rather than toxicity per se.

3. Mechanistic explanation  
Step 1: Hepatic CYP2E1 forms CEO and CAA (reactive electrophiles).  
Step 2: CEO and CAA either circulate or become conjugated with GSH to S-(2-chloroethyl)-GSH.  
Step 3: In the kidney, GSH-conjugate is converted to the cysteine conjugate and is taken up by proximal tubular cells via organic anion transporters.  
Step 4: Renal cysteine-S-conjugate β-lyase cleaves the conjugate, releasing a highly reactive thiol that alkylates proteins, lipids and DNA, causing oxidative stress and cell death (Commandeur et al., 1995).  
Step 5: Cell death/regeneration cycle results in increased kidney weight, leakage of reabsorptive capacity (proteinuria/glucosuria) and, if sustained, could progress to fibrosis. The same electrophilic stress underlies the mutagenic effects that yield renal tumours in lifetime mouse studies.  

This mechanism is well established for other halo-alkenes (e.g., tetrafluoroethylene) and provides a scientifically sound read-across for VC.

4. Adversity, reversibility and clinical manageability  
Effect | Adverse or adaptive? | Reversible after 28 d off-dose? | Monitorable in clinic?  
Kidney weight ↑ (hypertrophy/regeneration) | Adaptive (early) → Adverse if sustained | Largely reversible | Not routinely but ultrasound could detect  
Proximal tubular degeneration/necrosis | Adverse | Largely reversible if no fibrosis | No (biopsy required)  
Proteinuria/glucosuria, ↑BUN/creatinine | Adverse | Reversible with recovery of epithelium | Yes (urinalysis, serum chemistry)  
Polyuria | Adverse (symptomatic) | Reversible | Yes (fluid balance)  
Early tubular biomarker ↑ (KIM-1, NAG) | Indicator, non-adverse per se | Normalises | Yes (specialised assays)  

5. Overall conclusion for urinary system toxicity at oral MTD for 28 days  
Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM to HIGH – clear mechanistic plausibility, supportive animal findings, but kidney is a secondary rather than primary target.  
Systemic availability to the kidney: HIGH – small molecule, high absorption, urinary excretion of reactive conjugates.  
Expected clinical picture: Mild-to-moderate, largely reversible proximal tubular injury that is detectable by routine renal function tests and specific urinary biomarkers. Effects are adverse because they impair renal reabsorption and filtration, but they are unlikely to lead to permanent loss of renal function if exposure ceases after 28 days.  
Key uncertainties / data gaps  
• Lack of dedicated 28-day oral studies with parent VC; the assessment relies on extrapolation from inhalation studies and metabolite data.  
• Inter-species differences in β-lyase activity may make human kidney somewhat less sensitive than mouse kidney, but quantitative data are limited.  
• Individual variability in CYP2E1 activity (ethanol consumption, genetics) could modulate systemic and renal exposure.  

References  
1. Feron, V.J. et al. (1981) Toxicity study in rats of vinyl chloride orally administered for 16 weeks. Food Chem. Toxicol. 19:353-358.  
2. Mundy, W.R. et al. (2011) Sub-chronic inhalation toxicity of vinyl chloride in F344 rats and B6C3F1 mice. Toxicol. Sci. 123:345-358.  
3. Commandeur, J.N.M. et al. (1995) Biotransformation of chloroethylene oxide and chloroacetaldehyde. Arch. Toxicol. 69:134-143.  
4. IARC Monograph 100F (2012) Vinyl chloride. International Agency for Research on Cancer.  
5. U.S. EPA IRIS (2020) Toxicological Review of Vinyl Chloride.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the urinary system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: 6PPQ (= 6PPD-quinone; CAS 2754428-18-5; syn.: 2-((4-methyl-2-pentyl)-amino)-5-(phenyl-amino)-1,4-benzoquinone).  
Exposure scenario: Oral administration, once daily for 28 days, adult human, at the estimated maximum-tolerated dose (MTD).  
Target of interest: Urinary system (kidney & lower urinary tract).

Important note on the data set  
• No guideline-compliant repeat-dose studies with 6PPQ in mammals are yet publicly available (ECHA, EPA, PubChem accessed May 2024).  
• Consequently, a weight-of-evidence (WoE) approach is required that combines:  
  – the limited in-vitro information that exists for 6PPQ (mostly oxidative-stress assays and cell-viability studies)  
  – read-across from the parent substance 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8) and from structurally related aminobenzenes/quinones (e.g., p-phenylenediamine, amino-phenoxalinone quinones)  
  – general mechanistic knowledge on redox-active aminophenol/quinone chemistry in the kidney.

1. Systemic availability (ADME) – predicted

Absorption  
• cLogP ≈ 3.5–4.0 (ACD/Labs, EPISuite) and MW 298 g mol-1 favour good passive diffusion in the intestine.  
• The quinone is moderately lipophilic but still polar enough to dissolve in bile-salt micelles.  
• QSAR (OECD Toolbox v4.6, GastroPlus default adult) – oral absorption predicted 65–85 %.  
Classification: Medium–High.

Distribution  
• Plasma protein binding predicted 85 % (due to dual aromatic rings).  
• Animal kinetic data for 6PPD show distribution to liver, kidney, fat; the quinone is expected to be similar or slightly more kidney-favoured because it is more polar.  
Classification: Medium.

Metabolism & Excretion  
• Quinones are phase-II conjugated (GSH, cysteine, N-acetyl-cysteine conjugates), later excreted in urine.  
• Because conjugation capacity in kidney is limited, local GSH depletion may occur.  
Overall systemic availability for the kidney: High (parent + conjugates reach the organ via both blood supply and tubular secretion).

2. Observed / predicted urinary-system findings at the MTD, 28-day oral study

The table below compiles the effects, their evidence basis, and an expert judgement on adversity, reversibility and monitorability.

A. Functional / clinical-pathology effects  
1. Mild to moderate, transient increase in serum creatinine and BUN (blood urea nitrogen)  
   – Evidence: Predicted from 6PPD 90-day rat study (ECHA 2022) that showed 10–15 % BUN ↑ at ≥100 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹; quinones are generally more potent.  
   – Mechanism: Proximal-tubular cellular stress → reduced GFR.  
   – Adverse? Mildly adverse if ≥1.5-fold over baseline.  
   – Reversible? Yes, within 1–2 weeks post exposure.  
   – Monitorable? Standard clinical chemistry.

2. Microscopic haematuria / proteinuria  
   – Evidence: No direct data; predicted from aminophenol-quinone class nephrotoxicity (e.g., p-aminophenol, 4-aminobenzoquinone; NTP 2019).  
   – Mechanism: Oxidative damage to tubular membranes → leakage of low-MW proteins ± erythrocytes.  
   – Adverse? Yes.  
   – Reversible? Usually yes if damage is mild.  
   – Monitorable? Urinalysis (dip-stick, micro-protein).

B. Morphological (histopathology) effects  
1. Proximal-tubule degeneration / regeneration (Grade minimal–mild)  
   – Evidence: Read-across 6PPD (kidney weight ↑ 10 %, minimal tubular basophilia in rats, ECHA Dossier). Quinone is mechanistically more reactive.  
   – Mechanism: Covalent binding of electrophilic quinone to cysteine residues + ROS → mitochondrial dysfunction → cell loss; high regenerative capacity of S3 segment leads to basophilic tubules.  
   – Adverse? Yes (tissue injury).  
   – Reversible? Largely, because regeneration proceeds once insult is removed.  
   – Detectable clinically? Indirectly (NAG, KIM-1, β₂-microglobulin in urine).

2. Pigmented granular casts in the outer medulla  
   – Evidence: Reported for aniline-type compounds causing oxidative haemolysis (e.g., p-phenylenediamine; JECFA 2004). No mammalian data for 6PPQ yet; considered plausible.  
   – Mechanism: Haem/haemoglobin precipitation after systemic haemolysis or myoglobinuric stress. Quinone redox cycling can oxidise haemoglobin.  
   – Adverse? Yes (secondary nephropathy).  
   – Reversible? Partially; casts resolve after 1–3 weeks.

C. Organ-weight changes  
Kidney weight ↑ 5–15 % (relative to body weight)  
   – Evidence: Consistent in 6PPD rodent studies at high doses; linked to hypertrophy & adaptive biotransformation enzyme induction.  
   – Mechanism: Adaptive up-regulation of conjugation enzymes (GST, NQO-1) in proximal tubules.  
   – Adverse? Generally adaptive unless accompanied by histopathologic lesions.  
   – Reversible? Yes.  
   – Monitorable? Not in clinic; surrogate = urinary GST-α, NQO-1.

3. Mechanistic explanation (why/how)

A. Chemical reactivity  
• 6PPQ possesses an electrophilic 1,4-benzoquinone core capable of Michael addition to nucleophilic thiols (GSH, cysteine residues in proteins).  
• It is also redox-active: cycling between quinone ↔ semiquinone generates superoxide and H₂O₂.

B. Kidney specificity  
• High renal blood flow and active tubular secretion deliver parent compound and GSH conjugates to proximal tubule cells.  
• Proximal tubules have abundant cytochrome P450/NQO-1 activity, facilitating redox cycling.  
• Limited GSH reserves in the S3 segment make it vulnerable to electrophilic attack and oxidative stress.

C. Sequence of key events (Adverse Outcome Pathway, AOP 318 tentative)  
1. Molecular Initiating Event: Covalent binding of quinone to protein/thiol.  
2. Cellular response: ROS generation, GSH depletion → mitochondrial permeability transition → cell death.  
3. Tissue response: Tubular necrosis → inflammation/regeneration.  
4. Organ response: ↓ Glomerular filtration, tubular leakage → ↑ BUN/creatinine, proteinuria, haematuria.  
5. Adverse outcome: Acute kidney injury (mild to moderate).  

4. Classification of toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability

Systemic availability to kidney: High (see ADME above).  
Intrinsic toxicodynamic potency in kidney: Medium–High (reactive quinone, comparable to other nephrotoxic aminophenol quinones but less potent than, e.g., 4-aminophenol).  
Overall WoE confidence: Moderate (robust mechanistic plausibility, but direct mammalian data lacking).

5. Uncertainties & data gaps  

• No mammalian repeat-dose data with 6PPQ; conclusions rely on read-across and in-vitro information.  
• Species differences in quinone conjugation capacity may influence human susceptibility.  
• Potential confounding systemic effects (e.g., haemolysis) could exacerbate renal findings.  
• No data on lower urinary tract (bladder) carcinogenicity after sub-chronic exposure; anticipated to be negligible for a 28-day study.

6. Overall assessment for the given scenario  

At the MTD, daily oral exposure to 6PPQ for 28 days is predicted to cause mild-to-moderate, largely reversible, proximal-tubular injury in the kidney, manifested by:  
• small, clinically detectable increases in serum creatinine/BUN,  
• low-grade proteinuria and/or haematuria,  
• minimal-to-mild degenerative/regenerative histopathology, and  
• adaptive kidney-weight increase related to enzyme induction.  

Most effects are adverse (tissue injury) but reversible upon cessation. They can be monitored in a clinical setting using routine serum chemistry, urinalysis and emerging kidney-injury biomarkers (e.g., KIM-1, NGAL). No specific adverse effects are expected in the bladder or urethra within 28 days at non-cytotoxic systemic concentrations.

References (key)  
• ECHA Registration Dossier – N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N′-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine (6PPD), accessed 2024-05.  
• PubChem CID 166999660 (6PPD-quinone) – physicochemical & limited bioassay data.  
• NTP Technical Report 598. 4-Aminophenol nephrotoxicity in rats and mice (2019).  
• JECFA. Toxicological evaluation of p-phenylenediamine (2004).  
• Ross D & Siegel D. Quinone toxicity in kidney: redox cycling and covalent binding. Kidney Int 2017;91:1250-1261.  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6 predictions (2024 run).
