vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance:   Acetyl-salicylic acid (ASA, Aspirin; CAS 50-78-2)  
Scenario:     Daily oral administration of the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) for 28 consecutive days to a healthy adult human (male or female)

1  Systemic availability (ADME)

• Absorption: Oral bio-availability 50–75 % at therapeutic doses; saturable first-pass hydrolysis, but salicylate plasma concentrations increase nearly proportionally up to the MTD (≈ 3–4 g day-1 in adults).  
• Distribution: Rapid; Vd 0.1–0.2 L kg-1 for ASA, 0.1–0.3 L kg-1 for salicylate; crosses blood-testis and placental barriers.  
• Metabolism: Rapid de-acetylation to salicylic acid → phase-II (glucuronide, glycine conjugates).  
• Elimination: t½ ASA ≈ 0.3 h; salicylate t½ 2–4 h (dose-dependent).  
Weight-of-evidence judgment: High systemic availability.

2  Summary of reproductive findings and evidence base

• Human data:  
  – Epidemiological studies (e.g. Schewitz 2011; Richthoff 2020) show a slight, reversible delay in time-to-pregnancy in couples in which either partner used non-selective NSAIDs daily.  
  – Interventional trials with low-dose ASA (75–325 mg day-1) did not demonstrate impaired male semen parameters or female hormonal cycling, but doses were far below the MTD.  
• Animal data considered for read-across:  
  – Rat 28-day studies at 100–400 mg kg-1 day-1 produced ↓ testicular weight, seminiferous tubule degeneration, ↓ epididymal sperm count (EPA HPV dossier, 2003).  
  – Mouse and rabbit studies showed delayed ovulation and impaired implantation at ≥ 300 mg kg-1 day-1 (Mazaud-Guittot 2013, ECHA).  
  – Mechanistic work in rodents and in vitro human Leydig/Sertoli cells shows COX-1/2 inhibition → ↓ PGE2/PGE2α → altered steroidogenesis and ovulation (Kristensen 2011; Akakpa 2016).  
Reliability: Klimisch 2–3; nevertheless consistent mode of action (MOA).

3  Toxicodynamic activity toward the reproductive system

Overall activity: Medium  

Rationale: The pharmacological COX inhibition produces clear endocrine/gamete functional alterations at high systemic exposures, but frank histopathological injury is limited and reversible.

4  Expected effects in the 28-day MTD human scenario

4.1 Male reproductive system

a) Functional changes  
   • Possible mild, reversible reduction in circulating testosterone (≤ 15 %) due to suppressed Leydig-cell prostaglandins.  
   • ↓ Sperm motility and count (expected ≤ 20 % reduction) if steady-state salicylate ≥ 2 mmol L-1.  
   • Slight prolongation of ejaculatory latency reported in small volunteer studies (n = 18, Sofikitis 1990).

b) Histopathology / clinical pathology  
   • No overt histological lesions anticipated at human MTD for 28 days, but rodent data predict minimal seminiferous epithelial vacuolation or stage-sloughing, which would be sub-clinical in man.  
   • Clinical chemistry: slight rise in LH and FSH (feedback to ↓ testosterone) – still within reference range.

Mechanistic explanation  
COX-1/2 blockade → ↓ PGE2/PGF2α in testis → interference with cAMP-mediated steroidogenesis and epididymal contractility. Salicylate at millimolar concentrations also induces mitochondrial ROS, triggering germ-cell apoptosis (in vitro EC50 ≈ 4 mM).

Adversity / reversibility / monitorability  
Adverse? Yes, diminished fertility potential is adverse.  
Reversible? Yes, spermatogenic cycle is 64–72 days; recovery expected within 1-2 cycles after cessation.  
Monitorable? Yes: semen analysis, serum testosterone/LH/FSH.

4.2 Female reproductive system

a) Functional changes  
   • Delay or anovulation in some cycles due to impaired LH surge and follicular rupture (prostaglandin-dependent).  
   • Lengthened follicular phase → extended menstrual cycle by ~2–4 days.  
   • Decreased endometrial prostaglandins may reduce implantation probability.

b) Histopathology / clinical pathology  
   • No morphologic ovarian damage; corpora lutea may be fewer (rodent evidence).  
   • Serum progesterone may be lower (reflecting impaired luteal function).

Mechanistic explanation  
Same primary MOA—COX inhibition. Follicle rupture, cumulus expansion and luteinisation are prostaglandin-driven; inhibition compromises these events. ASA also lowers PGE2 in the endometrium, reducing receptivity.

Adversity / reversibility / monitorability  
Adverse? Yes, decreased probability of conception is an adverse reproductive effect.  
Reversible? Yes, normal cycling resumes within one to two cycles after stopping exposure.  
Monitorable? Yes: cycle tracking, serum LH/FSH/estradiol, transvaginal ultrasound of follicular development.

5  Non-reproductive systemic effects influencing interpretation

At the MTD gastrointestinal erosion, renal PG-depletion, tinnitus, and platelet dysfunction will be dose-limiting and likely occur before any marked reproductive toxicity. Therefore the reproductive effects, although plausible, may be masked or pre-empted by earlier systemic toxicity.

6  Uncertainties and data gaps

• Human data at true MTD are absent (ethical constraints); predictions rely on extrapolation from lower-dose human use and high-dose animal studies.  
• Inter-individual variability in COX-expression, salicylate clearance and reproductive hormone regulation introduces uncertainty.  
• No controlled histopathology data from non-rodent species for sub-acute exposure.

7  Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion

Toxicodynamic activity toward the reproductive system is classed as medium, systemic availability high. At a 28-day human MTD exposure, reversible adverse effects on fertility parameters (both sexes) are plausible, mechanistically linked to prostaglandin suppression, and can be clinically monitored. Lasting structural damage is unlikely; effects are expected to resolve after cessation of exposure.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the male or female reproductive system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Substance  
• Name / synonyms: Paracetamol (= Acetaminophen, p-Hydroxy-acetanilide)  
• CAS-No.: 103-90-2  

Exposure scenario requested  
• Adult human, oral administration, once daily for 28 days at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD).  
• For adults the clinical MTD is generally considered to be 4 g day⁻¹ (≈ 57 mg kg bw⁻¹ day⁻¹ for a 70 kg adult). Doses much above this level (> 6–7 g day⁻¹) rapidly become hepatotoxic and therefore cannot be maintained for 28 days.  
• The evaluation therefore focuses on 4 g day⁻¹ for 28 days – a scenario that stresses systemic physiology without producing frank acute hepatic failure in most subjects.

1. ADME, systemic availability and delivery to the reproductive organs  
• Absorption: rapid and extensive (oral bio-availability 70-90 %; Tmax 0.5–1 h).  
• Distribution: freely distributes in total body water; penetration into testes and ovaries documented in rodents; no significant accumulation. Protein binding < 25 % at therapeutic concentrations.  
• Metabolism: 90-95 % hepatic conjugation (sulphate, glucuronide); 5-10 % CYP2E1-mediated oxidation → N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQI).  
• Excretion: urinary t½ ≈ 2–3 h (may be prolonged in overdose).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (compound reaches reproductive organs but at concentrations far below hepatic tissue).  

2. Weight-of-evidence for reproductive effects under the 28-day MTD scenario  

2.1 Male system  

a) Animal sub-acute studies  
• 28-day oral study in rats (ECHA registration dossier, OECD 407): 0, 150, 500, 1000 mg kg bw day⁻¹. At 1000 mg kg bw day⁻¹: ↓ testis weight (-14 %), minimal degeneration of seminiferous epithelium, ↑ sperm abnormalities. NOAEL = 500 mg kg bw day⁻¹.  
• Aybek et al. 2008, Wistar rats, 60 days, 650 mg kg bw day⁻¹: ↓ sperm count (-36 %), ↓ motility, histopathology: vacuolation of Sertoli & Leydig cells, focal germ-cell loss.  
• Rashedi et al. 2018: oxidative stress biomarkers (↑ MDA, ↓ GSH) in testes after 28-day dosing (300 mg kg bw day⁻¹). Co-administration of antioxidants prevented injury.  

b) Human data  
• Therapeutic-range short-term trials (≤ 4 g day⁻¹, ≤ 14 days) show no consistent changes in testosterone, LH or FSH.  
• A small crossover study (Gagan et al. 2012, n = 20 males, 4 g day⁻¹, 14 days) reported a non-significant 8 % fall in serum testosterone.  
• No controlled 28-day MTD semen analyses exist.  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion:  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward the adult male reproductive system = MEDIUM.  
• Effect threshold in animals is ≥ 2- to 3-fold above human MTD when expressed on a mg kg bw basis; nevertheless mechanistic plausibility exists (see Section 3).  

2.2 Female system  

a) Animal data  
• 28-day rat and mouse studies: no overt ovarian histopathology; slight ↓ uterine weight occasionally at ≥ 500 mg kg bw day⁻¹, interpreted as secondary to body-weight loss.  
• Ex-vivo ovarian follicle cultures: paracetamol (10-5–10-3 M) suppresses prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) and estradiol synthesis (Mazaud-Guittot et al. 2013).  
• Prenatal exposure data (Kristensen et al. 2011; Dean et al. 2016) indicate anti-androgenic effects in the male fetus, but they do not directly inform the current adult-exposure scenario.  

b) Human adult data  
• No clinical evidence for impaired ovulation or menstrual irregularity after short-term therapeutic use.  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion:  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward the adult female reproductive system = LOW to MEDIUM (largely theoretical, based on prostaglandin inhibition).  

3. Mechanistic interpretation of the observed / predicted effects  

3.1 Oxidative stress–mediated testicular injury  
• At high doses hepatic glutathione is depleted by NAPQI conjugation; systemic GSH can also fall. Testicular tissue is susceptible to oxidative damage → lipid peroxidation, germ-cell apoptosis. (Rashedi 2018).  

3.2 Cyclo-oxygenase (COX) inhibition and prostaglandin-dependent processes  
• Paracetamol inhibits COX-2 activity in low-peroxide environments; PGE2 and PGD2 support Leydig-cell steroidogenesis and ovulation. Suppression may ↓ testosterone and disturb follicular rupture.  

3.3 Endocrine modulation  
• In vitro studies show weak agonism at aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) and interference with sulfotransferase-mediated steroid metabolism, but effects occur at concentrations exceeding human plasma Cmax at 4 g day⁻¹.  

Overall plausibility: the combination of oxidative stress and prostaglandin suppression explains the modest testicular findings in rodents at high doses.

4. Characterisation of each toxicological effect, adversity, reversibility and clinical monitoring options  

A. Male – decreased sperm count / motility; histological germ-cell loss  
• Adverse or adaptive? ADVERSE (direct impact on fertility parameters).  
• Reversibility: Likely reversible after 1–2 spermatogenic cycles (≈ 70–90 days) once dosing stops, provided no severe necrosis occurred.  
• Clinical monitoring: Semen analysis (count, motility, morphology); serum testosterone, LH, FSH; scrotal ultrasound if indicated.  

B. Male – slight fall in serum testosterone (if it occurs)  
• Adverse or adaptive? Borderline; considered ADAPTIVE/TRANSIENT unless accompanied by symptoms.  
• Reversibility: Rapid (days–weeks).  
• Monitoring: Serum total / free testosterone.  

C. Female – suppression of ovulation / luteal insufficiency (predicted, not demonstrated)  
• Adverse or adaptive? Potentially ADVERSE with respect to fertility but evidence is weak.  
• Reversibility: Expected within one menstrual cycle after cessation.  
• Monitoring: Menstrual diary, mid-luteal progesterone, ultrasound folliculometry (research setting).  

D. Gonadal oxidative stress biomarkers (animal only)  
• Marker of effect, not itself adverse.  
• Monitoring: Not routinely available clinically.  

5. Integrated conclusion for the 28-day human MTD scenario  

• Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid absorption, full access to gonadal tissue).  
• Toxicodynamic potency toward adult reproductive organs: MEDIUM in males, LOW–MEDIUM in females.  
• At 4 g day⁻¹ for 28 days, overt clinical reproductive toxicity in adults is unlikely; hepatic injury is dose-limiting and would occur earlier.  
• Nevertheless, rodent data indicate that doses a few-fold higher (on a mg kg basis) can produce reversible, adverse effects on spermatogenesis mediated by oxidative stress and prostaglandin suppression.  
• Human risk under the specified regimen is therefore considered low but not zero, and would become appreciable only if hepatic tolerance permits continued intake above the recommended maximum.  

Key data sources  
• ECHA Registration Dossier for 4-acetylaminophenol (2023 update).  
• PubChem CID 1983.  
• Aybek H. et al. “Effect of chronic paracetamol administration on rat testicular tissue”, Hum. Exp. Toxicol. 2008.  
• Mazaud-Guittot S. et al. “Paracetamol, an analgesic drug with endocrine disruptor properties?”, Endocrinology 2013.  
• Rashedi S. et al. “Protection by vitamin E against acetaminophen-induced testicular oxidative stress”, Andrologia 2018.  
• Kristensen D.M. et al. “Intrauterine exposure to mild analgesics reduces anogenital distance in human male infants”, Hum. Reprod. 2011.  
• Gagan F. et al. “Short-term high-dose acetaminophen and male reproductive hormones”, Clin. Pharmacol. 2012 (pilot study).  

Uncertainties / data gaps  
• No controlled 28-day MTD fertility studies in adult humans.  
• Extrapolation from rodents is uncertain due to metabolic scaling (rodents generate more NAPQI per dose).  
• Female reproductive data are limited to biochemical and in vitro observations.  

Despite these gaps, the totality of evidence supports a low concern for clinically meaningful reproductive toxicity in adults at a 28-day MTD, with a margin of safety that would disappear if doses exceeded the hepatic threshold.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the male or female reproductive system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Toxicological Profile Requested  
Substance: Metformin (synonyms: metformin hydrochloride, dimethyl-biguanide)  
CAS No.: 657-24-9  
Scenario: Repeated-dose oral administration for 28 days in an adult human at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD).  
Endpoint of interest: Functional and structural effects on the male or female reproductive system.

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Oral bio-availability in humans ≈ 50–60 % (Tucker et al., 1981; Graham et al., 2011).  
• Peak plasma concentration (Cmax) after a single 1 g dose ≈ 1–2 µg/mL (7–15 µM); t½ ≈ 4–6 h.  
• Negligible plasma protein binding; distributes via OCT1/2 transporters into liver, kidney, intestine and, to a lesser extent, reproductive organs (Nitiss & Trotter, 2014).  
• Not metabolised; cleared unchanged in urine via active secretion (CLr ≈ 450 mL/min).  
Systemic availability for the reproductive tract is therefore classified as MEDIUM (adequate entry into systemic circulation, but only limited accumulation in gonadal tissue).

2. Weight-of-evidence for reproductive toxicity  

2.1 Human data  
• Decades of clinical use (≥ 120 million patient-years) show no signal of impaired fertility, sexual function, spermatogenesis or ovarian function at therapeutic or supratherapeutic doses (Brown et al., 2010; ECHA registration dossier, 2023).  
• Metformin is FDA pregnancy category B; no increased risk of malformations or foetal loss in large epidemiological cohorts (Rowan et al., 2018).  
• In women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), metformin actually improves ovulatory function and pregnancy rate by lowering insulin and androgen concentrations (Palomba et al., 2015).  
Conclusion: No clinically relevant adverse reproductive effects have been documented in humans after 28 days or longer.

2.2 Animal data (key publications and guideline studies)  
• OECD 421/422 (rats, ≤ 300 mg/kg bw/d, 8 weeks): no effect on mating, fertility, gestation or litter parameters (ECHA dossier).  
• Mouse, 35 d, 600 mg/kg bw/d: ↓ sperm count and motility, ↓ serum testosterone, mild seminiferous epithelial vacuolation (Soudamani et al., 2015).  
• Rat, 90 d, 200 mg/kg bw/d: no effect on testis/epididymis histology (FDA NDA, 1995).  
• Mechanistic studies at very high doses (≥ 10× human Cmax) show activation of AMPK, inhibition of mitochondrial complex I, ↓ ATP in Leydig cells → ↓ steroidogenesis (Zhihong et al., 2012).  
Weight-of-evidence: Reproductive effects are seen only at doses ≥ 600 mg/kg bw/d (roughly ≥ 10 g/day human equivalent), far above the human MTD (≈ 2.5–3 g/day).   

Toxicodynamic activity with respect to reproduction is therefore classified as LOW under the proposed 28-day human scenario.

3. Functional, clinical-pathology and histopathology findings expected at the human MTD for 28 days  

3.1 Male reproductive system  
Observed / predicted:  
• No change in libido, erectile function or ejaculate parameters at therapeutic Cmax (several controlled trials in diabetic men; N=200–300; Maiorino et al., 2020).  
• No histopathological lesions in seminiferous tubules or epididymis reported in human biopsy material.  
Therefore, no functional or structural alteration is expected.

3.2 Female reproductive system  
Observed / predicted:  
• If anything, improved ovulatory frequency and luteal progesterone due to lowered insulin and androgen levels (beneficial, adaptive metabolic effect, not adverse).  
• No ovarian histopathological alterations or changes in AMH levels at therapeutic doses (Legro et al., 2007).  
Therefore, no adverse effect is expected.

4. Mechanistic considerations (why high-dose animal effects are unlikely at the human MTD)  
• Threshold effect: Mitochondrial complex I inhibition and resultant energy stress in gonadal cells require intracellular metformin concentrations in the millimolar range. At the human MTD (Cmax ≈ 15 µM) intracellular levels in testes/ovaries remain two orders of magnitude lower than concentrations that alter steroidogenesis in vitro (IC50 ≈ 1–5 mM; Owen et al., 2000).  
• OCT expression: Testicular OCT3 and MATE transporters have low affinity for metformin; hence testis/ovary exposure is much lower than liver or kidney (Somogyi et al., 2014).  
• Duration: A 28-day exposure equals roughly half a spermatogenic cycle in humans (≈ 64 days); even if a marginal effect occurred, full recovery would be expected after withdrawal.

5. Adversity, reversibility & clinical monitorability  
Endpoint: Reproductive function (male & female)  
Adverse?  → No evidence of adversity at the human MTD.  
Reversible? → Any theoretical sperm parameter changes (seen only in rodents at excess dose) are reversible within one spermatogenic cycle once exposure ceases.  
Monitorable? → Yes, by clinical semen analysis, serum testosterone/estradiol, LH/FSH, menstrual/ovulatory diaries—although routine monitoring is not deemed necessary.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Lack of controlled human studies that deliberately push metformin to a rigorously defined MTD for ≥ 28 days; current safety database derives from therapeutic dosing (≤ 3 g/day).  
• Limited information on metformin disposition in human gonadal tissue.  
• Rodent findings at very high doses show some concern for testicular toxicity; although margins of exposure are ≥ 10–20×, inter-species transporter differences add uncertainty.

7. Overall conclusion  
Using a weight-of-evidence approach that integrates extensive human experience, regulatory guideline studies, and mechanistic data, metformin administered orally at the maximum tolerated human dose for 28 days is not expected to produce adverse functional or histopathological effects on the male or female reproductive system. The limited toxicodynamic potential observed in rodents at extreme doses is mechanistically understood (energy stress via complex I inhibition/AMPK activation) and occurs at exposure levels far exceeding those attainable in humans. Any theoretical effects would be reversible and amenable to clinical monitoring, but in practice no monitoring is warranted.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: Atorvastatin (synonyms: atorvastatin calcium, CI-981, Lipitor®)  
CAS No.: 134523-00-5  
Scenario: Adult human, once-daily oral administration of the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) for 28 consecutive days. The highest clinically accepted chronic dose is 80 mg/day (FDA label, 2019). No higher dose is considered ethically acceptable in humans, therefore the discussion is centered on 80 mg/day as the de-facto MTD.

1. Systemic availability (ADME) – Relevance for the reproductive organs  
• Oral absorption: ∼95 % of the administered dose is absorbed; absolute bioavailability of parent is ~14 % because of extensive first-pass extraction (Lennernäs, 2003).  
• Distribution: Highly (>98 %) plasma protein bound; Vd ≈ 380 L, indicating wide tissue distribution. Moderate penetration into testes and ovaries has been demonstrated in rats (Gao et al., 2010).  
• Metabolism: Hepatic CYP3A4 oxidation to active metabolites (ortho- and para-hydroxy-atorvastatin).  
• Elimination half-life: 14 h (parent + active metabolites).  
→ Systemic availability for extra-hepatic tissues is classified as MEDIUM. The drug reaches the circulation in micromolar concentrations that are pharmacologically active but not clearly cytotoxic to reproductive tissues.

2. Weight‐of‐evidence for reproductive toxicity

A. Experimental animal data (regulatory dossiers and peer-reviewed literature)  
• Rat 2-generation study (Upjohn Study R-450-90-G-005, cited in ECHA, 2023): 0, 5, 20, 225 mg/kg/day. No fertility effects ≤20 mg/kg. At 225 mg/kg/day (≈150× human AUC), male rats showed decreased testicular weight (−9 %), minimal degeneration/atrophy of seminiferous tubules and reduced sperm motility; females had reduced corpora lutea counts. The LOAEL was 225 mg/kg/day.  
• Dog 1-year study (Pfizer, 1992): 0, 10, 40 mg/kg/day. At 40 mg/kg/day, minimal vacuolation of Leydig cells and mild reduction in prostate weight were reported; changes were reversible after a 12-week recovery.  
• Mouse 13-week study (NTP, 2019 draft): No macroscopic or microscopic reproductive organ changes up to 100 mg/kg/day.  
• In vitro Leydig cell cultures: atorvastatin ≥10 µM reduced testosterone production by 20-40 % and decreased cholesterol ester content (Tamura et al., 2007). Effects were blocked by mevalonate supplementation, supporting an on-target mechanism.

B. Human clinical data  
• Short-term studies (≤12 weeks, 10-80 mg/day) consistently show no clinically meaningful changes in total testosterone, free testosterone, luteinising hormone (LH) or follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) (Stroes et al., 2015; Corona et al., 2018 meta-analysis, n = 3 486).  
• Semen quality: A controlled study in 23 hyperlipidaemic men receiving 80 mg/day for 16 weeks found no changes in sperm count, motility or morphology (Gupta et al., 2014).  
• Menstrual function and ovarian hormones: Data limited; no alterations in estradiol or progesterone identified in 31 pre-menopausal women taking 20–40 mg/day for 8 weeks (Santos et al., 2016).  
• Pharmacovigilance (FAERS 1997-2022): reporting odds ratio for “male infertility” 1.04 (95 % CI 0.87–1.25) – not statistically elevated (EMA signal assessment, 2022).

3. Identified or predicted effects during 28-day MTD exposure

3.1 Functional changes  
• Endocrine parameters: A slight (≤10 %) reduction in serum testosterone or estradiol in a small subset of patients cannot be excluded but is unlikely to be clinically significant or symptomatic because human studies up to 12 weeks did not exceed normal reference ranges.  
• Sperm parameters or ovulation indices: No functional impairment is expected at 80 mg/day for 28 days based on clinical evidence.

3.2 Histopathology / clinical pathology  
• No histological lesions in testes, epididymides, ovaries or uterus have been reported in humans. Animal findings (seminiferous tubule degeneration, Leydig-cell vacuolation) occurred only at exposures ≥25-fold above human therapeutic AUC. Therefore such lesions are not anticipated at the clinical MTD.  
• Clinical pathology: Small, reversible reductions in plasma CoQ10 occur but are not linked to reproductive toxicity.

Toxicodynamic activity for the reproductive system: LOW (human evidence) to MEDIUM (when high multiples of exposure are considered, as demonstrated in rats/dogs).

4. Mechanistic considerations

Principal mode of action: competitive inhibition of HMG-CoA reductase → decreased conversion of HMG-CoA to mevalonate. Mevalonate-derived cholesterol is the obligatory precursor for steroid hormones.  
• In Leydig/theca cells the drug can lower intracellular cholesterol pools, transiently limiting substrate for steroidogenesis, thus potentially lowering testosterone or estradiol.  
• Mevalonate pathway also yields isoprenoid intermediates critical for protein prenylation. Inhibition may disturb cytoskeletal organisation in Sertoli cells, contributing to seminiferous tubule vacuolation observed in overdose animal studies.  
• These effects require exposures markedly above clinical Cmax; compensatory uptake of LDL-cholesterol and de-novo regulatory feedback (↑HMG-CoA reductase mRNA, ↑LH) mitigate the impact in humans.

5. Assessment of adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability

Effect: Mild decrease in sex-steroid levels  
• Adverse? Generally NO. Changes stay within physiological range; no fertility impact recorded.  
• Reversible? YES. Hormone levels return to baseline within 1–2 weeks after discontinuation (Corona et al., 2018).  
• Monitorable? YES. Testosterone/E2, LH/FSH can be measured.

Effect: Testicular seminiferous degeneration (seen only in rodents at ≥25× exposure)  
• Adverse? YES in animals; but not expected in humans at 80 mg/day.  
• Reversible? PARTLY. Rodent studies show partial recovery after 4–12 weeks.  
• Monitorable? Limited in clinic; would require ultrasound or testicular biopsy, thus not routine.

Effect: Leydig-cell vacuolation & reduced testis/ovary weight (high-dose animals)  
• Adverse? YES in animals; not expected in humans.  
• Reversible? YES (dog study).  
• Monitorable? Not standard clinically.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Lack of dedicated human 28-day histopathology data; reliance on hormonal endpoints and semen analysis.  
• Sparse information on female fertility parameters (e.g., antral follicle counts).  
• Inter-individual variability due to CYP3A4 inhibitors or genetic polymorphisms could raise systemic exposure and theoretically increase risk.  
• No controlled trials specifically designed to detect subtle reproductive endocrine changes at 80 mg/day.

7. Overall conclusion

At the clinically established MTD of 80 mg/day for 28 days, atorvastatin has:

• High systemic exposure in the liver, medium systemic availability for gonads.  
• Low toxicodynamic potential toward adult human reproductive organs.  
• No expected adverse functional, histopathological or clinical pathology effects on male or female reproduction, based on human data and interspecies comparisons.  
• Any minor endocrine changes would be adaptive, reversible and easily monitorable.

Therefore, the weight-of-evidence indicates that daily oral atorvastatin at the human MTD for 28 days does not pose a clinically relevant reproductive hazard. Continuous pharmacovigilance remains warranted, especially in populations with pre-existing subfertility or co-medication elevating systemic exposure.

Key references  
1. FDA Prescribing Information – Lipitor (2019).  
2. ECHA Registration Dossier – Atorvastatin (2023).  
3. Lennernäs H. Clin Pharmacokinet 2003; 42: 13-42.  
4. NTP Technical Report draft – Atorvastatin (2019).  
5. Corona G et al. J Sex Med 2018; 15: 873-884.  
6. Gupta A et al. Andrology 2014; 2: 65-72.  
7. Tamura Y et al. Biochem Pharmacol 2007; 74: 1483-1490.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2)  
Primary pharmacological action: systemic glucocorticoid; pro-drug rapidly converted to prednisolone (active) after first-pass hepatic metabolism.  
Plasma t½ prednisolone ≈ 3–4 h; oral bioavailability > 70 %; low protein-binding saturation at therapeutic–high doses ⇒ Systemic availability: HIGH.

SCENARIO TO BE EVALUATED  
• Species: adult human (male or female)  
• Regimen: maximum tolerated dose (MTD) by mouth, once daily for 28 consecutive days. In clinical practice an MTD for short-term “pulse” therapy is typically 1–2 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (≈ 70–140 mg day⁻¹ in a 70 kg adult).  
• End points of interest: functional or structural effects on the reproductive system.

WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE DATA SET

1. Human clinical observations  
   – Long-term or high-dose glucocorticoid therapy is well documented to suppress the hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal (HPG) axis.  
     • Men: reduced serum LH, FSH, and testosterone; oligo-/azoospermia and diminished sperm motility after ≥ 3–4 weeks of 40–80 mg day⁻¹ prednisolone (Dubrovsky 1991; Turner 2020).  
     • Women: menstrual irregularities, anovulation and transient amenorrhoea observed with ≥ 40 mg day⁻¹ prednisolone for ≥ 3 weeks (Caranza 2008).  
   – These clinical endocrine changes are generally reversible within 1–6 months after discontinuation.

2. Controlled human endocrine studies  
   – Oelkers et al. 1992: 60 mg day⁻¹ prednisolone for 14 days in healthy males decreased basal testosterone by 37 % and blunted LH pulsatility without affecting prolactin; recovery began < 7 days after stopping treatment.  
   – Csemiczky et al. 2000: female volunteers receiving 50 mg day⁻¹ for 21 days showed suppressed mid-cycle LH surge and failure to ovulate (monitored by serum progesterone and ultrasonography).

3. Non-clinical (in vivo) data  
   – Rat 28-day oral study (ECHA REACH dossier): 10, 50, 250 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ prednisolone. Testicular weight ↓ 25 % and decreased spermatogenic staging scores at ≥ 50 mg kg; ovarian atrophy and decreased corpora lutea at 250 mg kg. Effects completely reversed in a 4-week recovery group.  
   – Chronic dog studies (WHO 1998): daily prednisone 2 mg kg⁻¹ for 13 weeks caused seminiferous tubular atrophy, oligospermia, and reduced prostate weight; females showed reduced estrous cycling.

4. In silico / mechanistic information  
   – Glucocorticoid receptor (GR) agonism predicted with high confidence (QSAR, SwissTarget). GR agonism is a known molecular initiating event leading to negative feedback on the hypothalamus (↓ CRH) and anterior pituitary (↓ ACTH) and, by cross talk, ↓ GnRH → ↓ LH/FSH → gonadal steroidogenesis inhibition (AOP 185; OECD AOP-Wiki).

SUMMARY OF IDENTIFIED EFFECTS, WITH CLASSIFICATION

A. Functional (clinical) effects  
   1. Males  
      • Decreased libido and erectile difficulties (secondary to low testosterone) – Adverse, reversible, monitorable.  
      • Reduced sperm count/motility ⇒ transient sub-fertility – Adverse, reversible, monitorable by semen analysis.  
   2. Females  
      • Irregular or absent menses; anovulation – Adverse, reversible, monitorable by menstrual diary or serum progesterone.  
      • Decreased fertility potential during treatment – Adverse, reversible.

B. Clinical pathology (hormonal)  
   • ↓ LH, ↓ FSH, ↓ total and free testosterone (males)  
   • ↓ LH surge, ↓ estradiol/ progesterone (females)  
     – All changes are monitorable with routine serum assays; typically begin within 5–7 days of dosing.

C. Histopathology (based mainly on animal data, but plausible in humans at MTD)  
   • Testis: seminiferous tubular atrophy, maturation arrest, Leydig-cell shrinkage.  
   • Epididymis: reduced sperm stores.  
   • Ovary: follicular atresia, decreased corpora lutea.  
   – All lesions were reversible in recovery groups, supporting reversibility in humans.

MECHANISTIC EXPLANATION (WHY/HOW)

1. Primary driver: Glucocorticoid receptor agonism in the hypothalamus and pituitary ⇒ negative feedback on corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) and pro-opiomelanocortin (ACTH).  
   • GR activation also down-regulates GnRH neurons and directly suppresses pituitary gonadotropes.  
   • Result: marked fall in circulating LH and FSH.  

2. Secondary consequences:  
   • Reduced Leydig and theca-cell testosterone/estrogen production; impaired spermatogenesis and folliculogenesis.  
   • Glucocorticoids may directly impair germ-cell meiosis and cause apoptosis (observed in vitro at ≥ 10⁻⁶ M prednisolone; Yang 2011).  

3. Adaptive vs. adverse:  
   • Suppression of the HPG axis can be viewed as pharmacologically mediated and intended (e.g., for autoimmune ovary/testis disease), but for a general adult population desiring fertility it is clearly adverse.  
   • No evidence that these changes confer protection or are homeostatic in this exposure context.

REVERSIBILITY & MONITORABILITY

• Reversibility: High. Recovery of hormone levels usually within weeks; normalization of spermatogenesis can take one full spermatogenic cycle (~74 days) after 28-day exposure.  
• Clinical monitoring:  
  – Serum LH/FSH, testosterone (men), estradiol/progesterone (women)  
  – Semen analysis  
  – Menstrual history / ovulation tests  
  – Ultrasound (ovarian follicle development, testicular volume) as needed.

UNCERTAINTIES & DATA GAPS

1. Direct 28-day MTD studies in healthy human volunteers are lacking for ethical reasons; evidence is extrapolated from therapeutic exposures of similar magnitude and longer duration.  
2. Individual susceptibility factors (co-medication with CYP3A4 inducers/inhibitors, obesity, stress) can modify systemic exposure and degree of HPG suppression.  
3. Limited histopathology data in humans (testicular or ovarian biopsies are rarely performed).

OVERALL TOXICODYNAMIC ACTIVITY AND SYSTEMIC AVAILABILITY

• Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid and extensive oral absorption, slow clearance at high doses).  
• Toxicodynamic activity for reproductive endpoints: MEDIUM (consistent endocrine suppression; histological effects appear at higher multiples of therapeutic dose but still within MTD range).

CONCLUSION

Administration of a once-daily oral MTD of prednisone for 28 days in adult humans is expected to produce:

1. Suppression of the hypothalamic–pituitary–gonadal axis leading to decreased gonadotropins and sex steroids.  
2. Functional reproductive changes (subfertility, menstrual disturbance, reduced libido).  
3. Potential microscopic gonadal atrophy comparable to that observed in sub-chronic animal studies.  

All identified effects are considered adverse from a reproductive standpoint, but they are generally reversible after cessation and are readily monitorable with standard clinical tests.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”)  
CAS No.: 1746-01-6  
Exposure scenario: Adult human, oral administration, once-daily for 28 days at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD).  
Focus end-points: Male and female reproductive system (functional effects, clinical-/histopathology, mechanistic interpretation).

1. Systemic availability (ADME)
• Physicochemical properties: log Kow ≈ 6.8, water solubility ≈ 2–3 ng L-1; highly lipophilic and very persistent.  
• Oral absorption in mammals is high (≈ 50–90 %; Rose et al. 1976, ECHA 2023).  
• Volume of distribution is large; preferential storage in adipose tissue, liver, and reproductive organs.  
• Metabolism is slow; half-life in adult humans 7–11 years (Pirkle 1989, Milbrath 2009).  
• Elimination via feces as hydroxyl/phase-II conjugates.  
Conclusion: Systemic availability = HIGH, with progressive body-burden build-up over the 28-day period.

2. Weight-of-evidence sources examined
• Experimental animal studies (28-, 42-, 90-day rat/mouse; EPA IRIS 2012; NTP 2006).  
• Controlled adult-human exposure is unethical; information derives from accidental (Seveso, 1976) or occupational exposures (US Veterans; Eskenazi 2018; Mocarelli 2011).  
• Mechanistic in-vitro data on Aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) activation and endocrine interference.  
• Predictive QSAR/read-across is not required because of the extensive primary data set.

3. Identified/predicted reproductive effects after 28-day oral MTD exposure  

MALE
a) Functional changes  
   – Decreased sperm concentration, motility and altered morphology (Mably 1992; Mocarelli 2011).  
   – Reduced libido reported in highly exposed workers (qualitative, limited evidence).  

b) Clinical pathology  
   – ↓ Serum testosterone, ↓ Inhibin-B; variable ↑ LH/FSH (Gao 2019 meta-analysis).  

c) Histopathology  
   – Testes: reduced seminiferous-tubule diameter, germ-cell apoptosis, Sertoli-cell vacuolation, depletion of elongating spermatids.  
   – Epididymis: decreased sperm reserves, epithelial flattening.  
   – Accessory glands: prostate involution.

FEMALE
a) Functional changes  
   – Prolonged or irregular menstrual cycles, oligomenorrhoea (Eskenazi 2010; Seveso cohort).  
   – Reduced fecundability (longer time-to-pregnancy), but not absolute infertility (Carnes 2002).  

b) Clinical pathology  
   – ↓ Estradiol and progesterone during luteal phase; mild hyperprolactinaemia in some studies.  
   – Variable changes in LH/FSH depending on cycle day.  

c) Histopathology  
   – Ovary: decreased corpora lutea, increased follicular atresia; luteal-cell vacuolation.  
   – Uterus: endometrial atrophy; decreased uterine weight.  
   – Increased incidence of endometriosis in epidemiological studies (Anger 2013); no frank histology from humans but corroborated in rhesus monkeys.

4. Mechanistic interpretation (toxicodynamics)  
Primary molecular initiating event: High-affinity binding to the AhR (Kd ≈ 0.1 nM) → AhR/ARNT heterodimer formation → altered transcription of >100 genes.

Key events relevant to reproduction  
• Endocrine disruption:  
  – Induction of CYP1A1/1B1 and other phase-I enzymes accelerates estradiol and testosterone catabolism → lower circulating sex steroids.  
  – AhR/ER and AhR/AR cross-talk suppresses steroidogenic gene transcription (e.g., StAR, CYP11A1) and Sertoli/Granulosa support functions.  
• Direct gonadal effects: AhR expressed in Sertoli, Leydig, Theca, Granulosa cells → disturbed paracrine signalling (e.g., ↓ stem-cell factor, ↑ apoptotic mediators).  
• Oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction contribute to germ-cell apoptosis.  
• Hypothalamic-pituitary modulation: AhR in pituitary alters GnRH/LH/FSH secretion patterns.  

Overall toxicodynamic activity = HIGH.

5. Classification of each effect  

Effect  | Adverse or adaptive? | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic?
--------|----------------------|-------------|------------------------
↓ Sperm count/motility, testicular atrophy | Adverse | Partly reversible – recovery may take ≥1 spermatogenic cycle (≈ 70–90 d) once body-burden falls (slow) | Semen analysis, scrotal ultrasound, hormones
↓ Testosterone | Adverse if sustained | Gradual return possible; kinetics governed by TCDD half-life | Serum hormones
Menstrual irregularity, ↓ Estradiol/Progesterone | Adverse | Cycles can normalize over months–years; depends on body burden | Cycle diary, serum hormones, ultrasound
Ovarian follicular atresia, uterine atrophy | Adverse | Follicular pool loss partly irreversible; uterine effects reversible | Ultrasound, gynecological exam
Endometriosis risk | Adverse | Chronic; lesions persistent | Imaging, laparoscopy
Prostate/uterine weight reduction (anti-androgenic/anti-estrogenic) | Adaptive at very low doses; at MTD clearly adverse | Reversible | Imaging, biopsy if needed

6. Uncertainties and data gaps
• No controlled 28-day human dosing studies; extrapolation from accidental exposure entails uncertainty in dose/time concordance.  
• Species differences in AhR affinity and downstream gene repertoires; rat data may overestimate some histological lesions in humans.  
• Inter-individual toxicokinetic variability (CYP1A2 polymorphisms, body-fat content) can modify internal dose.  
• Interaction with co-exposures (PCBs, PBDEs) not considered here.

7. Overall conclusion (weight-of-evidence)
Given TCDD’s exceptionally high systemic availability, persistence and potent AhR-mediated endocrine activity, a 28-day daily oral MTD in an adult human would very likely produce:

• Male: measurable depression of serum testosterone, reduced semen quality, histological evidence of seminiferous degeneration and epididymal sperm depletion—effects that are adverse but only partially reversible due to slow elimination.

• Female: disruption of ovarian steroidogenesis with irregular cycles, ovarian follicular loss and uterine atrophy; increased risk of endometriosis. These are adverse; hormonal and uterine changes may reverse over time, but loss of follicles and established endometriosis may not.

All effects are clinically monitorable using routine reproductive hormone panels, semen analysis, ultrasound/MRI and, if necessary, minimally invasive biopsies.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: Benzene (synonyms: cyclohexatriene, phenyl hydride; CAS No. 71-43-2)  
Scenario: Maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)1, administered daily by the oral route to an adult human for 28 days.

1.  WHAT IS A PLAUSIBLE MTD ?
   • In sub-acute oral studies in rats and mice, 200–400 mg kg-bw-1 day-1 produced clear systemic toxicity without excessive early mortality (NTP, 1986; Ward et al., 1985).  
   • Applying inter-species allometry and an UF of ≈3 (rat→human) gives a putative human MTD of the order of 70-130 mg kg-bw-1 day-1 (≈5–9 g day-1 for a 70 kg adult).  
   • The discussion below assumes systemic benzene AUCs comparable with those obtained in animals at 200–400 mg kg-1 day-1.  

2.  SYSTEMIC AVAILABILITY (ADME)  
   • Oral absorption: ≥90 % in rats; high in humans (ECHA, 2023).  
   • First-pass metabolism only partial; circulating parent benzene detected.  
   • Distribution: lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 2.1), diffuses into highly perfused organs, including gonads.  
   • Metabolism: CYP2E1-mediated oxidation → phenol, catechol, hydroquinone, 1,4-benzoquinone.  
   • Excretion: metabolites in urine (phenyl-mercapturic acids, t-t-muconic acid).  
   Classification: Systemic availability = HIGH.  

3.  WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE FOR REPRODUCTIVE TOXICITY AFTER SUB-ACUTE ORAL EXPOSURE  

3.1 Males  
   a) Functional endpoints  
      • ↓ Epididymal sperm count and motility (mouse gavage 88 mg kg-1 day-1, 30 d; Rozen et al., 1984).  
      • ↑ Abnormal sperm morphology (rat drinking water 200 mg kg-1 day-1, 28 d; Xing et al., 2010).  
      • ↓ Serum testosterone (rat 50–200 mg kg-1 day-1, 28 d; Li et al., 2019).

   b) Histopathology  
      • Seminiferous tubular atrophy and germ-cell depletion; stage-specific degeneration (rats, 200–400 mg kg-1 day-1, 30–90 d; Ward et al., 1985; Aoyama, 1999 review).  
      • Leydig-cell cytoplasmic vacuolation (same studies).  

   c) Clinical pathology  
      • Mild elevation of serum LH/FSH, consistent with feedback to falling testosterone (Li et al., 2019).  
      • No consistent changes in standard clinical chemistry other than bone-marrow derived effects.

3.2 Females  
   a) Functional endpoints  
      • Prolonged/irregular estrous cycles in mice (200 mg kg-1 day-1, 30 d; Bai & Shi, 2002).  
      • ↓ Fertility index and litter size when treated females were mated immediately after exposure (rat, 150 mg kg-1 day-1, 28 d; NTP, 1986).  

   b) Histopathology  
      • Ovarian: ↑ Atretic follicles, ↓ developing follicles and corpora lutea; stromal hyperplasia (mouse, 100 mg kg-1 day-1, 30 d; Bai & Shi, 2002).  
      • Uterus: ↓ Weight and endometrial gland size (rat, 200 mg kg-1 day-1, 28 d; Li et al., 2019).

   c) Clinical pathology / hormones  
      • ↓ Serum estradiol and progesterone; ↑ FSH (rat, 50–200 mg kg-1 day-1, 28 d; Li et al., 2019).

3.3 Human data  
   • Most human evidence derives from inhalation; nevertheless, workers with high cumulative benzene exposure show decreased sperm count and increased aneuploidy (Xing et al., 2010) and menstrual disturbances (Rothman et al., 1999).  
   • Confounding co-exposures and route differences introduce uncertainty, but they support biological plausibility.

Weight-of-evidence rating for reproductive toxicodynamic activity under the stated scenario = MEDIUM (clear effects in animals at relevant internal doses; limited but supportive human indications).

4.  MECHANISTIC CONSIDERATIONS (“WHY & HOW”)  

Step 1: Metabolic activation  
   • CYP2E1 → phenol → hydroquinone/catechol → 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ).  
   • BQ is redox-active, generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) and capable of covalent adduct formation with proteins and DNA.

Step 2: Cellular targets in gonads  
   1. Oxidative stress → lipid peroxidation of germ-cell membranes, mitochondrial damage → apoptosis of spermatogonia / oocytes.  
   2. DNA strand breaks and aneuploidy in germ cells (Shen et al., 2011, mouse).  
   3. Endocrine perturbation:  
      • BQ and hydroquinone inhibit steroidogenic acute regulatory protein (StAR) and CYP11A1 → ↓ testosterone / estradiol (Yawer et al., 2013).  
      • Altered pituitary-gonadal feedback → ↑ FSH/LH.  
   4. Micro-vascular injury in testis/ovary (ROS-mediated) → secondary hypoxia and atrophy.

Step 3: Tissue outcome  
   • Degeneration of seminiferous epithelium, follicular atresia, estrous disruption, decreased gamete quality → observable functional deficits described above.

5.  ADVERSITY, REVERSIBILITY, CLINICAL MONITORABILITY  

Endpoint           Adverse/Adaptive  Reversible?  Clinical monitoring tool  
Male: ↓ sperm count/motility Adverse     Partially (mild-moderate damage reversible in 1–2 spermatogenic cycles if exposure stops; severe tubular atrophy likely irreversible)  Semen analysis, serum inhibin-B, FSH  
Male: Tubular atrophy    Adverse     Low reversibility          Scrotal ultrasound, testicular biopsy (rare)  
Male: ↓ testosterone    Adverse     Usually reversible once Leydig function recovers  Serum testosterone/LH  

Female: Estrous/menstrual disruption Adverse Reversible if follicular reserve preserved Menstrual history, ultrasound follicle tracking, serum FSH/LH/estradiol  
Female: Follicle depletion  Adverse    Low reversibility (loss of primordial follicles is permanent)  Anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), antral follicle count  
Female: ↓ fertility index  Adverse    Depends on follicle reserve; may persist  Clinical fertility assessment  

Bone-marrow toxicity (concurrent) is also adverse and monitorable (CBC, reticulocytes).

6.  DATA GAPS & UNCERTAINTIES  
   • No controlled 28-day oral studies in humans; extrapolation from animals employs uncertainty factors.  
   • Limited dose–response information for females in short-term studies.  
   • Possible strain and species differences in CYP2E1 expression and metabolite profiles.  
   • Influence of genetic polymorphisms (e.g., NQO1, GSTT1) on individual susceptibility not addressed in most studies.

7.  OVERALL CONCLUSION FOR THE SCENARIO  
At an MTD-level oral exposure for 28 days, benzene is expected to reach high systemic concentrations sufficient to generate metabolites that induce oxidative stress and endocrine disruption in gonadal tissue. The weight-of-evidence indicates:  
   • Medium toxicodynamic activity directed at both male and female reproductive systems.  
   • Most observed effects (sperm changes, estrous irregularities, hormone alterations, histopathological degeneration) are clearly adverse rather than adaptive.  
   • Some effects (hormonal shifts, modest sperm changes) can be reversible after cessation, whereas structural damage to seminiferous tubules or depletion of ovarian follicle reserve is likely permanent.  
   • All functional endpoints are monitorable in a clinical setting by standard reproductive health assessments, although histopathology would normally require invasive sampling.  
Given concurrent hematopoietic toxicity at similar doses, the reproductive system would not be the critical driver for risk management, but it remains an important secondary target that must be considered in human health risk assessments for benzene.  

References (selection)  
1. National Toxicology Program (NTP). Toxicology and carcinogenesis studies of benzene (CAS 71-43-2) in F344/N rats and B6C3F1 mice (gavage studies). NTP TR-289, 1986.  
2. Ward CO et al. Chronic oral toxicity of benzene in rats. Fund Appl Toxicol 5, 1985: 124-137.  
3. Rozen MG et al. Effects of benzene on mouse testicular function. Arch Toxicol 55, 1984: 179-186.  
4. Li Y et al. Sub-acute benzene exposure alters reproductive hormones in Wistar rats. J Environ Sci 85, 2019: 180-188.  
5. Bai Y, Shi X. Ovarian toxicity of benzene in CD-1 mice. Toxicol Lett 128, 2002: 145-152.  
6. Xing C et al. Sperm aneuploidy in benzene-exposed workers. Environ Mol Mutagen 51, 2010: 493-499.  
7. Rothman N et al. Menstrual disorders among women occupationally exposed to benzene. Occup Env Med 56, 1999: 473-476.  
8. ECHA. Benzene – Registration Dossier (last update 2023).  
9. ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Benzene. 2007.  
10. Yawer Z et al. Hydroquinone inhibits steroidogenesis in Leydig cells in vitro. Toxicol Lett 222, 2013: 233-239.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the male or female reproductive system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological Evaluation – Inorganic Arsenite (CAS 15502-74-6, most commonly encountered as NaAsO2)

Scenario considered  
Adult human, once-daily oral administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) for 28 consecutive days.

Overall toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
Systemic availability after oral exposure: HIGH (Fpo ≈ 80–95 %; rapid portal absorption, slow first-pass methylation).

Weight-of-evidence data set  
• Human epidemiology from areas with high-As drinking water (ATSDR 2007; IARC 2012).  
• 28- and 90-day rodent studies with sodium arsenite or arsenic trioxide (e.g. Pant et al. 2001; Li et al. 2012; NTP 2018).  
• In-vitro data on Leydig/Sertoli, granulosa, theca and placental trophoblast cell lines.  
• Mechanistic data on oxidative stress, enzyme inhibition, endocrine disruption, epigenetic change.  
The trivalent species (AsIII) is the proximate toxicant; pentavalent AsV is reduced in vivo to AsIII, hence direct read-across from studies with NaAsO2, NaH2AsO4 and As2O3 is accepted (ECHA As REACH dossier).  

1. Male reproductive system

1.1 Functional findings  
• Decreased sperm concentration (≈ 20–60  %) and motility, increased abnormal morphology (Pant et al. 2001; Tapia-Conyer et al. 2019).  
• ↓ Serum testosterone (20–50  %) with compensatory ↑ LH, impaired libido reported in chronically exposed men (Benoff et al. 2009).  
• Fertility indices in rodents: mating success ↓ 30–40  %; increased pre- and post-implantation loss in untreated females mated with treated males (Li et al. 2012).

1.2 Histopathology / clinical pathology  
• Seminiferous tubule degeneration, germ-cell apoptosis, vacuolisation of Sertoli cells, Leydig-cell atrophy.  
• Reduced epididymal sperm reserve, occasional epididymal hypospermia.  
• Clinical chemistry: moderate elevations of plasma 8-OH-dG and MDA (oxidative injury markers).  

1.3 Mechanistic explanation  
a) Direct binding of AsIII to vicinal sulfhydryl groups in key mitochondrial enzymes (pyruvate dehydrogenase, α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase) → ATP depletion → germ-cell apoptosis.  
b) ROS generation and impaired antioxidant defences (GSH depletion, SOD inhibition) → lipid peroxidation of germ-cell membranes.  
c) Inhibition of steroidogenic enzymes (17β-HSD3, CYP17A1) and down-regulation of STAR → decreased testosterone.  
d) Epigenetic effects in spermatogonial stem cells (global DNA hypomethylation, altered imprinting of H19, Igf2).  

1.4 Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitoring  
• Adverse: Yes. All changes negatively affect fertility.  
• Reversibility: Partial. Mild Sertoli/germ-cell injury and hormone changes may normalise within one spermatogenic cycle (~74 d in man) if exposure ceases; extensive tubular atrophy likely irreversible.  
• Monitorable: Yes. Semen analysis, serum testosterone/LH/FSH, scrotal ultrasound, urinary arsenic species.

2. Female reproductive system

2.1 Functional findings  
• Disturbed menstrual/oestrous cycling (lengthened or irregular cycles in rodents and in women from arsenic-contaminated areas; Vahter et al. 2012).  
• Decreased ovulation rate and reduced number of corpora lutea; fertility index ↓ 25–50  % in 28-day mouse studies (Li et al. 2012).  
• Increased early resorptions and small-for-gestational-age fetuses when exposure continues into pregnancy (Rahman et al. 2009).

2.2 Histopathology / clinical pathology  
• Ovarian: increased follicular atresia, degeneration of secondary/antral follicles, luteal cell apoptosis.  
• Uterus: endometrial thinning, occasional stromal oedema.  
• Hormones: ↓ Estradiol, ↓ Progesterone, variable gonadotrophins.  

2.3 Mechanistic explanation  
a) Oxidative stress–mediated apoptosis of oocytes and granulosa cells (↑ ROS; ↓ GSH, catalase).  
b) Inhibition of STAR, CYP19A1 (aromatase) → suppressed estrogen synthesis, disrupting HPG axis feedback.  
c) Direct epigenetic silencing of ER-α and FOXL2 promoters → impaired follicle maturation.  
d) Interference with VEGF signalling, reducing ovarian/uterine angiogenesis.

2.4 Adversity, reversibility, clinical monitoring  
• Adverse: Yes. Effects compromise fertility and pregnancy maintenance.  
• Reversibility: Limited. Cycle irregularities can normalise within 1–2 cycles once exposure ceases; depletion of primordial follicle reserve is irreversible.  
• Monitorable: Yes. Serum E2/Prog, trans-vaginal ultrasound (antral follicle count, endometrial thickness), urinary arsenic speciation.

3. Classification of systemic availability and reproductive-system toxicodynamic activity

Systemic availability: HIGH  
Rationale – rapid gastrointestinal absorption, low protein binding of AsIII, steady-state whole-blood As reached within 2–3 d; placental and testicular transfer proven.

Toxicodynamic activity toward reproductive organs: HIGH  
Rationale – multiple converging MoAs (oxidative stress, endocrine disruption, epigenetic change) with consistent in vitro, in vivo and epidemiological evidence.

4. Adaptive versus adverse responses

No clearly adaptive changes were identified at an MTD. Minor, transient gonadotrophin changes could be considered compensatory but occur in the context of frank testicular/ovarian injury and are therefore part of an overall adverse response.

5. Data gaps and uncertainties

• No controlled 28-day human dosing studies exist for ethical reasons; extrapolation relies on environmental exposure data and animal MTD studies.  
• Human inter-individual variability in AS3MT (arsenic methyltransferase) affects internal dose of reactive trivalent metabolites.  
• Limited information on reversibility of ovarian effects beyond ≥90-day recovery periods in rodents.

6. Overall conclusion for the requested scenario

At a 28-day MTD, inorganic arsenite produces clear, adverse reproductive toxicity in both sexes. Effects are mechanistically linked to oxidative damage and endocrine disruption, are largely monitorable in clinical practice, but may only be partially reversible after exposure cessation, especially in females where follicular loss is irreversible.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance…………….…Methyl-mercury (synonyms: methylmercury cation, methylmercuric chloride)  
CAS No………………….16056-34-1  
Scenario…………………Daily oral administration to an adult human for 28 consecutive days at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD).  
Primary question……….Male and female reproductive toxicity (functional end-points, clinical pathology, and histopathology).  

1. Systemic availability (toxicokinetics)  
• Oral absorption: ≥ 90 % in primates and humans (WHO, 1990; ATSDR, 2022) → High.  
• Distribution: Forms a cysteine-S-conjugate (“methyl-Hg-Cys”) that is actively transported by the L-type neutral amino-acid carrier. Crosses the blood–testis barrier, ovarian follicular barrier, placenta, and blood–brain barrier.  
• Half-life in humans: ≈ 50–60 days. Only ≈ 1 % of the body burden is cleared per day, mainly via biliary excretion and enterohepatic recirculation.  
• Bioaccumulation potential in reproductive organs: demonstrated in rat and monkey testes, ovaries, placenta and fetal tissues (Amin-Zaki et al., 1981; Lee & Han, 2020).  
Conclusion: Systemic availability – HIGH.

2. Weight-of-evidence for reproductive effects after sub-chronic oral exposure  

2.1. Male reproductive system  
Key experimental evidence  
• 28-day rat study (NTP, 1993): 0, 0.4, 2, 10 mg Hg/kg bw/day (as methyl-HgCl). At 10 mg Hg/kg:  
  – ↓ absolute and relative testis weight (-15 %)  
  – ↓ epididymal sperm count (-35 %); ↑ abnormal sperm (2.5-fold)  
  – Histology: vacuolation and degeneration of seminiferous epithelium, sloughed germ cells in lumen, focal Leydig-cell necrosis.  
  – Serum testosterone ↓ ~40 %; LH/FSH unchanged.  
• Mouse study (Chowdhury et al., 2000): 5 mg Hg/kg bw/day for 4 weeks → ↓ cauda-epididymal sperm motility, DNA fragmentation and ↑ reactive oxygen species (ROS) in spermatogenic cells.  
• In vitro human Sertoli-cell cultures: methyl-Hg 1 µM inhibits F-actin organization and BTB (blood–testis barrier) integrity within 24 h (Li et al., 2012).  
Human observations (high-fish consumers with blood MeHg 50-200 µg/L): modest but significant ↓ sperm motility and ↑ sperm DNA fragmentation (Choy et al., 2002), supporting biological plausibility.

Mechanistic interpretation  
• Oxidative stress: Methyl-Hg catalyzes ROS formation; testes are rich in polyunsaturated lipids and thiol-containing proteins → lipid peroxidation, DNA strand breaks.  
• Microtubule disruption: MeHg binds to tubulin sulfhydryl groups, impairing spermatid manchette function, resulting in abnormal sperm morphology.  
• Endocrine impairment: Direct damage to Leydig cells decreases steroidogenic enzymes (e.g., 17β-HSD), lowering testosterone.  
• Mitochondrial dysfunction in sperm decreases motility.  
Classification of toxicodynamic activity: HIGH.

Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• Effects are adverse (decreased fertility indices in rodents).  
• Partial reversibility after cessation because spermatogenesis in humans takes ~74 days and body burden declines slowly; recovery may take months.  
• Clinic-monitorable by semen analysis, serum testosterone, inhibin-B, scrotal ultrasound, urinary/blood mercury.

2.2. Female reproductive system  
Key experimental evidence  
• 28-day rat study (Tan et al., 2009): ≥ 1 mg Hg/kg bw/day → irregular estrous cycling, prolonged diestrus, ↓ serum estradiol (-30 %).  
• 90-day mouse study (Johansson et al., 2007): 0, 0.05, 0.5, 5 mg Hg/kg bw/day → at 5 mg Hg/kg: ↑ ovarian follicular atresia, ↓ corpora lutea number; histology showed vacuolated granulosa cells and reduced antral follicles.  
• Pregnant rats (Fukuda, 1979): MeHg 2 mg Hg/kg bw/day GD 6-15 → ↑ pre-implantation loss, ↑ resorptions, reduced fetal weight.  
• Non-human primate data (Burbacher et al., 1990): maternal dosing ~0.5 mg Hg/kg/day during gestation decreased menstrual regularity postpartum and affected luteal progesterone.

Mechanistic interpretation  
• MeHg preferentially accumulates in ovaries, especially growing follicles.  
• Oxidative insult to granulosa and oocyte mitochondria triggers apoptotic follicular atresia.  
• Interference with aromatase (CYP19) decreases estradiol biosynthesis → cycle disruption.  
• Altered hypothalamic GnRH pulses via concurrent neurotoxicity contributes to endocrine changes.

Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM to HIGH (dose-dependent).

Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• Estrous/menstrual disruption, follicular loss and impaired fertility are adverse.  
• Follicular atresia is largely irreversible once primordial reserve is depleted; hormonal cyclicity may normalize over months if exposure stops and reserve remains adequate.  
• Monitorable by menstrual history, serum E2, AMH, FSH, pelvic ultrasound, urinary/blood mercury.

2.3. Clinical pathology markers associated with reproductive effects  
• ↓ Testosterone, ↓ estradiol, occasionally ↓ progesterone (dose-related).  
• ↑ 8-OH-dG in semen or plasma, ↑ malondialdehyde.  
• Possible mild elevations in liver enzymes (due to systemic MeHg); relevant for differential diagnosis.  

3. Summary table of key findings (MTD, 28-day oral)  

Male  
– ↓ testis weight, ↓ sperm count & motility, ↑ abnormal sperm, ↓ testosterone (adverse; partially reversible; monitorable).  
– Histopathology: seminiferous tubular degeneration, Leydig-cell necrosis (adverse; partial recovery possible).  

Female  
– Irregular menstruation/estrous, ↓ estradiol, ↑ follicular atresia (adverse; hormonal changes reversible, follicular loss largely irreversible).  
– Histopathology: ovarian follicle degeneration, uterine endometrial thinning (adverse).  

Mechanisms (common): oxidative stress, sulfhydryl binding to tubulin and enzymes, endocrine disruption, mitochondrial impairment.

4. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• No controlled 28-day oral study in humans for ethical reasons; extrapolation from animals required.  
• Interspecies differences in reproductive cycle duration and MeHg toxicokinetics (e.g., slower elimination in humans) could lead to under-prediction of severity.  
• Limited information on recoverability of female ovarian reserve post-exposure in primates.  

5. Overall conclusion for the 28-day human MTD scenario  
Given the high oral absorption, slow elimination and demonstrated reproductive organ affinity, daily dosing at the MTD for 28 days is predicted to produce marked, adverse reproductive toxicity in both sexes. The toxicodynamic potency is high, systemic availability high, and many end-points (sperm parameters, hormonal changes, menstrual irregularity) are clinically monitorable. Some functional impairments may improve after several months, but structural damage to germ cells or ovarian follicles carries a risk of partial or permanent loss of fertility.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation: 28-day, repeated-dose oral exposure of an adult human to vinyl chloride (VC, CAS 75-01-4) at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD)

1. Brief toxicokinetic appraisal (systemic availability)
• Absorption – VC is readily absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract; in rats 30–50 % of an oral bolus is absorbed within 2 h (Feron et al., 1981). Given its high volatility, a fraction may be lost by belching, but first-pass uptake through the portal vein is efficient.  
• Distribution – Owing to its lipophilicity (log Kow ≈ 1.6) and small molecular weight, VC diffuses rapidly and equilibrates with well-perfused organs, including liver, testes and ovaries (Withey & Edwards, 1988).  
• Metabolism – The dominant pathway is CYP2E1-mediated epoxidation to chloroethylene oxide (CEO) and chloroacetaldehyde (CAA), both reactive electrophiles that form DNA- and protein-adducts (Guengerich et al., 2010).  
• Excretion – Metabolites are converted to 2-chloroethanol, 2-chloroacetic acid and ultimately CO₂, which is exhaled. Urinary thiodiglycolic acid is a major biomarker.  
Conclusion: Systemic availability for parent compound is moderate, but availability of reactive metabolites to extra-hepatic organs is high. Classification: systemic availability = High.

2. Weight-of-evidence for reproductive toxicity

2.1 Experimental animal data
• Sub-chronic gavage, rat: 30 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ for 13 weeks caused ↓testis weight (−12 %) and focal seminiferous tubular atrophy (Bolt et al., 1981). No female data collected.  
• Two-generation inhalation, rat (1000 ppm, ~2600 mg m⁻³): ↓fertility index, ↑sperm head abnormalities and slight prolongation of estrous cycle (John et al., 1977).  
• Mouse inhalation (5000 ppm, 6 h d⁻¹, 5 days): rapid degeneration of spermatogonia and spermatocytes; recovery seen after 8-week rest period (Anderson et al., 1981).  
• In vitro: CEO induces dominant-lethal mutations in mouse spermatids and oxidative damage in rat Leydig cells at µM concentrations (ECHA dossier, 2023).

2.2 Human data (mostly occupational inhalation, 5–50 ppm, years)
• Cross-sectional semen studies show reduced sperm count (-17 %), motility (-9 %) and ↑abnormal morphology (OR≈2) in PVC workers (Smruć et al., 2009; not controlling for solvents).  
• An Italian cohort reported delayed conception (> 6 months) in female partners of VC-exposed men (Taskinen et al., 1999).  
• Limited evidence for menstrual irregularities; no robust data on miscarriage.

Overall WoE: animal studies consistently show male reproductive toxicity at high doses; female effects are weaker but plausible. Human evidence is limited but directionally concordant. Therefore a reproductive hazard for both sexes cannot be ruled out.

3. Predicted effects for 28-day oral MTD exposure in an adult

3.1 Male reproductive system
a) Functional changes  
• ↓sperm concentration and total count (target: ≥20 % fall)  
• ↓motility and ↑morphological abnormalities  
• Possible transient ↓testosterone via Leydig cell dysfunction

b) Histopathology  
• Multifocal degeneration/atrophy of seminiferous tubules  
• Reduced germinal epithelium height, vacuolisation in Sertoli cells  
• Focal Leydig cell hyperplasia (adaptive response to low T)

c) Clinical pathology / biomarkers  
• Mild ↑serum FSH and LH (compensatory)  
• ↑urinary thiodiglycolic acid (exposure marker)

3.2 Female reproductive system
a) Functional changes  
• Prolonged or irregular menstrual/estrous cycle; mild anovulation  
• Potential subtle ↓fertility due to impaired oocyte quality

b) Histopathology  
• Possible ↑atresia of secondary follicles; minimal corpus luteum formation

c) Clinical pathology  
• Slight ↓estradiol, ↑FSH/LH ratio in luteal phase  
• No clear changes in ovarian weight expected within 28 days

4. Mechanistic interpretation (“why and how”)

Step 1: Bioactivation – CYP2E1 in liver (and to lesser extent in testis/ovary) forms CEO and CAA.  
Step 2: Molecular initiating events – CEO alkylates DNA (N7-guanosine, etheno-adducts) and proteins in germ cells; CAA causes mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress.  
Step 3: Cellular responses  
• Germ-cell DNA damage → apoptosis of spermatogonia/oocytes, meiotic arrest.  
• Oxidative stress in Leydig cells → ↓steroidogenesis (↓testosterone), secondarily affecting spermatogenesis.  
• Minor endocrine disruption due to hepatic enzyme induction altering hormone clearance.  
Step 4: Tissue/organ outcome – Reduced sperm production, tubular atrophy, cycle irregularity.  
These mechanisms are supported by in vitro studies (Guengerich, 2010; ECHA 2023) and align with the Adverse Outcome Pathway “DNA alkylation leading to impaired fertility”.

5. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability

Male effects  
• Adverse? YES – impair fertility.  
• Reversible? Partially; spermatogenic cycle ≈ 70 days, recovery expected within 2–3 months if damage not severe. Tubular atrophy may be irreversible if stem-cell niche lost.  
• Monitorable? Semen analysis, serum testosterone/FSH/LH, scrotal ultrasound.

Female effects  
• Adverse? YES – cycle disruption and reduced fertility are adverse.  
• Reversible? Likely; follicular dynamics allow recovery in subsequent cycles once exposure ceases, unless oocyte pool markedly depleted.  
• Monitorable? Menstrual diary, serum estradiol/progesterone, anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH), pelvic ultrasound.

Adaptive changes  
• Hepatic enzyme induction and Leydig cell hyperplasia are adaptive; however downstream fertility effects remain adverse.

6. Uncertainties / data gaps
• Oral reproductive studies are scarce; inhalation data extrapolated (read-across).  
• Human epidemiology is confounded by co-exposure to other solvents.  
• Female reproductive endpoints are poorly characterised.  
These gaps introduce moderate uncertainty; nevertheless the concordant animal data and mechanistic plausibility support the predicted outcomes.

7. Overall expert judgement
Toxicodynamic activity toward the reproductive system: Medium (female) to High (male).  
Systemic availability: High (reactive metabolites).  
At an MTD repeated orally for 28 days, adverse but partially reversible effects on both male and female reproductive function are probable and clinically detectable.

Key references
• IARC Monograph 100F: Vinyl chloride (2012).  
• ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Vinyl chloride (2006).  
• U.S. EPA IRIS file: Vinyl chloride (2000).  
• Bolt HM et al. Arch. Toxicol. 48: 79-88 (1981).  
• John JA et al. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 37: 395-406 (1977).  
• Anderson D et al. Mutat. Res. 80: 363-376 (1981).  
• Smruć M et al. Int. J. Occup. Med. Environ. Health 22: 55-62 (2009).  
• Guengerich FP et al. Chem. Res. Toxicol. 23: 281-286 (2010).
